topics.title: Phases of the Design Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET865 - AIRCRAFT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Phases of the Design Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phases of the Aircraft Design Process** constitute a systematic, iterative, and progressively refined sequence of engineering activities that transform an operational need into a fully certified, manufacturable, and economically viable aerospace product. As per **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET865)**, the design process is partitioned into **four classical phases**: **Conceptual Design, Preliminary Design, Detailed Design, and Production Design**, each governed by distinct deliverables, fidelity levels, decision gates, and certification milestones aligned with **CS-25 / FAR Part 25** standards.

The **aircraft design process** is fundamentally a **decision-making hierarchy** governed by the principle that *errors become exponentially costlier to rectify as the design matures*. This is captured by **Wright's Cost-of-Change Curve**, which states that the cost of correcting a design error at the production stage is roughly **10×, 100×, and 1000×** more expensive than correcting the same error in the conceptual, preliminary, and detailed design stages respectively.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Building a Custom House"**
>
> Imagine you want to build your dream house:
>
> - **Conceptual Design** ≈ Discussing with family: *"Do we want a villa or an apartment? How many rooms? What's our budget?"* — High-level dreams on paper napkins.
> - **Preliminary Design** ≈ Hiring an architect who draws floor plans, selects materials, and confirms the structure *fits the budget and zoning laws*. You can still change the kitchen layout cheaply here.
> - **Detailed Design** ≈ The structural engineer calculates every beam, rebar size, and electrical circuit. Now changing the kitchen means tearing down a load-bearing wall — expensive!
> - **Production Design** ≈ Construction workers actually build it, following shop drawings to the millimeter.
>
> **Aircraft design follows the identical logic** — except the structure must survive Mach 0.85, -54°C at altitude, and 20 years of fatigue cycles.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Checklist (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Know Vocabulary for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Mission Profile** — The defined operational scenario (range, payload, altitude, speed) the aircraft must satisfy.
> - **Design Iteration** — The cyclic refinement of a design based on analysis feedback.
> - **Decision Gate (Milestone Review)** — Formal review point where the design must satisfy predefined criteria before progressing.
> - **Trade Study** — Comparative analysis of competing design alternatives against weighted criteria.
> - **Technology Readiness Level (TRL)** — A **9-point scale (1–9)** used to assess maturity of new technologies. **TRL 1** = basic原理 observed; **TRL 9** = proven in operational environment.
> - **Customer/Operator Requirements** — Inputs derived from airlines, military users, or market studies.
> - **Level of Fidelity (LoF)** — The granularity and accuracy of design data at each phase.

### 1.4 The Standard Industry Reference Framework

The KTU syllabus implicitly follows the framework documented in **Roskam's "Airplane Design" (Parts I–VIII)** and **Raymer's "Aircraft Design: A Conceptual Approach"**, which all major aerospace primes (Boeing, Airbus, Lockheed Martin) follow. The exact phase names vary by author, but the underlying engineering logic is universal.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Phase-by-Phase Logical Breakdown

#### 🔹 PHASE 1: CONCEPTUAL DESIGN (≈ 5–10% of total program cost)

**Why this phase exists:** *Before any metal is cut, engineers must answer: "Can this airplane physically and economically exist?"*

**Activities performed:**
1. **Mission requirement definition** — Capture customer needs (range $\geq 5000$ nm, payload $\geq 150$ pax, cruise Mach = 0.82).
2. **Configuration selection** — Choose between **tube-and-wing, flying wing, canard, or blended wing body (BWB)**.
3. **Initial sizing** — Use **scaling laws (Rosen's method)** and **constraint analysis** to estimate Take-Off Weight (TOW), Wing Area ($S$), and Thrust-to-Weight (T/W).
4. **Weight estimation** — Apply **Class I (statistical) weight equations** such as the **Torenbeek/GD&T method**:

$$W_{TO} = \frac{W_{payload} + W_{crew}}{1 - \frac{W_{fuel}}{W_{TO}} - \frac{W_{empty}}{W_{TO}}}$$

5. **Feasibility check** — Confirm that the design satisfies all **top-level requirements (TLRs)**.
6. **Conceptual Configuration Drawing (3-view)** — Earliest geometric definition.

**Deliverables:** Feasibility report, configuration sketches, initial weight statement, top-level specification.

---

#### 🔹 PHASE 2: PRELIMINARY DESIGN (≈ 15–20% of total cost)

**Why this phase exists:** *Transform the winning concept into a defensible baseline geometry.*

**Activities performed:**
1. **Refined aerodynamic analysis** — Vortex-lattice method (VLM), $C_L$ vs $\alpha$, drag polar.
2. **Stability & control derivatives** — Initial estimates using **DATCOM** or equivalent.
3. **Structural layout** — Selection of wing structural concept (spar location, rib spacing, skin gauge).
4. **Propulsion integration** — Engine placement, nacelle sizing, inlet design.
5. **Trade studies** — Pugh matrices comparing 4–6 design alternatives.
6. **Performance verification** — Point performance: stall, ceiling, climb gradient, range/payload.
7. **Cost & market analysis** — Estimation of **Direct Operating Cost (DOC)** and **Return on Investment (ROI)**.

**Deliverables:** Preliminary design report, baseline geometry (CAD), weight breakdown, performance envelope, **Preliminary Design Review (PDR)**.

---

#### 🔹 PHASE 3: DETAILED DESIGN (≈ 40–50% of total cost)

**Why this phase exists:** *Engineer every part, every fastener, every wire down to the last tolerance.*

**Activities performed:**
1. **High-fidelity CFD analysis** — Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) simulations on full aircraft.
2. **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)** — Stress, buckling, fatigue, flutter analysis on all primary structure.
3. **Systems engineering** — Hydraulic, electrical, avionics, fuel, environmental control systems integration.
4. **Manufacturing planning** — Selection of materials (Al 7075-T6, CFRP, Ti-6Al-4V), joining methods.
5. **Weight control** — Maintain mass properties within ±0.5% of baseline.
6. **Critical Design Review (CDR)** — Final design freeze before tooling.
7. **Production tooling design** — jigs, fixtures, NC programs.

**Deliverables:** Full engineering drawings, analysis reports, manufacturing plans, **Critical Design Review (CDR)** package.

---

#### 🔹 PHASE 4: PRODUCTION DESIGN & CERTIFICATION (≈ 25–30% of cost)

**Why this phase exists:** *Build, certify, and deliver a safe, conforming article.*

**Activities performed:**
1. **Prototype fabrication** — Build one or more flying prototypes.
2. **Ground testing** — Static load tests (150% limit load), fatigue, systems.
3. **Flight test campaign** — **First Flight → Envelope Expansion → Performance & Handling Qualities → Certification.**
4. **Type certification** — Compliance demonstration per **EASA CS-25** or **FAA FAR Part 25**.
5. **Production rate ramp-up** — Assembly line optimization, supply chain maturation.
6. **In-service support** — Maintenance manuals, training, spare parts logistics.

**Deliverables:** Certified aircraft, **Type Certificate (TC)**, **Production Certificate (PC)**, in-service documentation.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is your KTU Board Exam survival kit for this topic.**

| **Concept / Metric** | **Expression or Rule** | **Phase Where Used** | **Significance** |
|----------------------|------------------------|----------------------|------------------|
| **Cost-of-Change Multiplier** | Cost grows by $\sim 10\times$ per phase | All | Justifies early investment in analysis |
| **Technology Readiness Level (TRL)** | Discrete scale $1 \to 9$ | Conceptual → Preliminary | Determines if a tech is "ready" |
| **Weight Fractions** | $\frac{W_{empty}}{W_{TO}} + \frac{W_{fuel}}{W_{TO}} + \frac{W_{payload}}{W_{TO}} = 1$ | Conceptual | Fundamental sizing equation |
| **Constraint Equation (Take-Off)** | $\frac{T}{W} = f\!\left(\frac{W}{S},\, C_{L_{max}},\, V_{stall}\right)$ | Preliminary | Couples T/W and W/S |
| **Breguet Range Equation** | $R = \frac{V}{c_T} \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)$ | Preliminary / Detailed | Validates cruise range |
| **Level of Fidelity (LoF)** | Order: $\text{CAD} < \text{FEA} < \text{CFD} < \text{Full-scale test}$ | Phase-dependent | Matches tools to phase maturity |
| **Configuration Selection Metric** | $\min\!\big[\text{DOC},\; \text{Weight},\; \text{Drag},\; \text{Risk}\big]$ | Conceptual | Multi-objective trade |
| **Pugh Matrix Score** | $S_i = \sum_j w_j \cdot (\text{Baseline}_j - \text{Alt}_i,j)$ | Preliminary | Weighted trade study |
| **Design Margin** | Typical $5$–$15\%$ on weight, $\geq 20\%$ on cost | All phases | Buffers for unknowns |
| **Safety Factor (Limit Load)** | $\text{Design Load} = 1.5 \times \text{Limit Load}$ | Detailed | CS/FAR certification requirement |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Industry Cares:**
> - **Boeing 787 Dreamliner** entered conceptual design in **2003**, flew in **2009**, certified in **2011** — a textbook 8-year cycle that compresses all 4 phases.
> - **Lockheed F-35** was preceded by the **X-35 demonstrator** in the conceptual phase to prove stealth and STOVL concepts before committing to full-scale development.
> - **Cost overruns on Airbus A380 (~$12 billion)** were largely attributed to **premature commitment to detailed design without sufficient preliminary trade study** — a textbook violation of Phase 2 rigor.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example: Iterative Convergence in Conceptual Sizing

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** An airline requires a twin-engine regional jet with the following mission profile:
> - Payload: $W_{pl} = 18{,}000$ kg (150 pax × 120 kg)
> - Crew: $W_{cr} = 600$ kg (4 × 150 kg)
> - Cruise speed: $V = 820$ km/h
> - Range: $R = 3000$ nm $= 5{,}556{,}000$ m
> - Lift-to-Drag ratio: $L/D = 18$
> - Thrust Specific Fuel Consumption: $c_T = 0.6$ /hr
> - Empty weight fraction: $W_e/W_{TO} = 0.50$
>
> **Determine the Take-Off Weight (TOW) using Breguet range and iterative convergence.**

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Convert units to SI consistent system**

$$V = 820 \text{ km/h} = \frac{820}{3.6} = 227.78 \text{ m/s}$$

$$c_T = 0.6 \text{ /hr} = \frac{0.6}{3600} = 1.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /s}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Breguet Range Equation**

$$R = \frac{V}{c_T} \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_{TO}}{W_{TO} - W_{fuel}}\right)$$

**Step 3: Solve for the fuel-weight ratio**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{W_{TO}}{W_{TO} - W_{fuel}}\right) = \frac{R \cdot c_T}{V \cdot (L/D)}$$

Plugging in numbers:

$$\frac{R \cdot c_T}{V \cdot (L/D)} = \frac{5{,}556{,}000 \times 1.667 \times 10^{-4}}{227.78 \times 18}$$

Compute numerator: $5{,}556{,}000 \times 1.667 \times 10^{-4} = 926.18$

Compute denominator: $227.78 \times 18 = 4{,}100.04$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{W_{TO}}{W_{TO} - W_{fuel}}\right) = \frac{926.18}{4{,}100.04} = 0.2259$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{W_{TO}}{W_{TO} - W_{fuel}} = e^{0.2259} = 1.2535$$

**Step 4: Solve for fuel weight**

$$W_{TO} = 1.2535 \cdot (W_{TO} - W_{fuel})$$

$$W_{TO} = 1.2535 \cdot W_{TO} - 1.2535 \cdot W_{fuel}$$

$$1.2535 \cdot W_{fuel} = 0.2535 \cdot W_{TO}$$

$$\frac{W_{fuel}}{W_{TO}} = \frac{0.2535}{1.2535} = 0.2022 \quad (\approx 20.22\%)$$

**Step 5: Apply the fundamental weight fraction identity**

$$\frac{W_{empty}}{W_{TO}} + \frac{W_{fuel}}{W_{TO}} + \frac{W_{payload+crew}}{W_{TO}} = 1$$

$$0.50 + 0.2022 + \frac{W_{pl} + W_{cr}}{W_{TO}} = 1$$

$$0.7022 + \frac{18{,}000 + 600}{W_{TO}} = 1$$

$$\frac{18{,}600}{W_{TO}} = 0.2978$$

$$W_{TO} = \frac{18{,}600}{0.2978} = 62{,}458.7 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{W_{TO} \approx 62{,}459 \text{ kg}}$$

**Step 6: Iteration check (Rosen's method)** — Because $W_e/W_{TO}$ itself depends on $W_{TO}$, a second iteration updates $W_e$:

$$W_e^{(2)} = 0.50 \times 62{,}459 = 31{,}230 \text{ kg (assumed)}$$

Since $W_e$ is a **statistical estimate** that holds within ±10% in this range, convergence is acceptable. In a full **Preliminary Design Phase**, one would re-enter the Breguet equation with the updated $L/D$ derived from drag polar analysis.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Phase-by-Phase Decision Granularity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Mental Model:** *Which decisions can you still change at each phase?*

| **Decision Variable** | **Conceptual (Phase 1)** | **Preliminary (Phase 2)** | **Detailed (Phase 3)** | **Production (Phase 4)** |
|------------------------|:------------------------:|:------------------------:|:----------------------:|:------------------------:|
| Number of engines      | ✅ Easy to change | ⚠️ Costly | ❌ Locked | ❌ Impossible |
| Wing position (low/high) | ✅ Easy | ⚠️ Costly | ❌ Locked | ❌ Impossible |
| Wing area $S$          | ✅ ±30% flexibility | ⚠️ ±5% | ❌ ±0.5% | ❌ Locked |
| Skin gauge             | ✅ Concept-level | ⚠️ Estimate | ✅ Final | ❌ Frozen |
| Fastener type          | ❌ Not yet defined | ⚠️ Candidate | ✅ Final | ❌ Locked |
| Avionics supplier      | ⚠️ Vendor search | ⚠️ Trade study | ✅ Selected | ❌ Contract signed |
| Tooling                | ❌ None | ⚠️ Concept | ✅ Designed | ❌ Built |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

    end

    PHASE1 --> PHASE2 --> PHASE3 --> PHASE4

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** List and briefly explain the **four phases of aircraft design**.

**Model Answer:**
The four phases are:
1. **Conceptual Design** — Defines the mission, generates configuration alternatives, performs feasibility sizing (Class-I weight estimates).
2. **Preliminary Design** — Establishes baseline geometry, conducts refined aerodynamics and trade studies; ends with **PDR**.
3. **Detailed Design** — Releases every engineering drawing, performs CFD/FEA, designs tooling; ends with **CDR**.
4. **Production Design** — Builds prototypes, runs ground and flight tests, achieves **Type Certification (CS-25)**, and delivers the aircraft to service.
*(3 marks: 1 mark for listing + 2 marks for brief explanation of each phase)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Q2.** What is a **Technology Readiness Level (TRL)**? Why is it critical during the **conceptual design phase**?

**Model Answer:**
**TRL** is a **9-point discrete scale (1–9)** defined by **NASA and adopted industry-wide** that rates the maturity of a technology:
- **TRL 1–3:** Basic research, proof of concept.
- **TRL 4–6:** Component validation in lab/relevant environment.
- **TRL 7–9:** System prototype, qualified, and proven in operational use.

It is **critical during conceptual design** because high-risk technologies (e.g., **blended wing body, hybrid-electric propulsion**) with TRL ≤ 5 must be either **de-risked via demonstrator programs** or **substituted with mature alternatives (TRL ≥ 7)** before committing to detailed design. *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for relevance to conceptual phase)*

---

### 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### ✏️ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Pattern A]**

**Q A.** (a) Explain in detail the **activities, deliverables, and decision gates of the Conceptual Design Phase** with a suitable block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A new trainer aircraft must achieve **range = 800 nm, payload = 800 kg, L/D = 14, c_T = 0.7/hr, V = 200 m/s, empty-weight fraction = 0.45**. Estimate the **Take-Off Weight** using the **Breguet Range Equation**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q A(a):

**Activities in Conceptual Design:**
1. Capture **customer/market requirements** — range, payload, cruise speed, ceiling.
2. Define the **mission profile** (take-off → climb → cruise → descent → landing).
3. Generate **configuration alternatives** (low wing vs high wing, conventional vs canard, piston vs turbofan).
4. Perform **initial sizing** using Class-I weight equations.
5. Conduct **trade studies** using **Pugh matrices** to score alternatives.
6. Verify **feasibility** against all top-level requirements.
7. Generate **conceptual 3-view drawings**.

**Deliverables:**
- Feasibility report
- Configuration baseline
- Initial weight statement
- Top-level specification document
- **Conceptual Design Review (CoDR)** minutes

**Decision Gate:** **Conceptual Design Review (CoDR)** — at which management approves the chosen configuration and releases funding for Preliminary Design.

**Block Diagram** *(already presented in Section 4.2)*.

*(Mark split: 2 marks activities + 2 marks deliverables + 2 marks decision gate + 1 mark for diagram)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q A(b):

**Step 1 — Unit conversion:**

$$c_T = \frac{0.7}{3600} = 1.944 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /s}$$

$$R = 800 \text{ nm} = 800 \times 1852 = 1{,}481{,}600 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Breguet Range Equation:**

$$R = \frac{V}{c_T} \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_{TO}}{W_{TO} - W_{fuel}}\right)$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{W_{TO}}{W_{TO} - W_{fuel}}\right) = \frac{R \cdot c_T}{V \cdot (L/D)} = \frac{1{,}481{,}600 \times 1.944 \times 10^{-4}}{200 \times 14}$$

Compute numerator: $1{,}481{,}600 \times 1.944 \times 10^{-4} = 288.03$

Compute denominator: $200 \times 14 = 2800$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{W_{TO}}{W_{TO} - W_{fuel}}\right) = \frac{288.03}{2800} = 0.1029$$

$$\frac{W_{TO}}{W_{TO} - W_{fuel}} = e^{0.1029} = 1.1084$$

**Step 3 — Solve for fuel weight fraction:**

$$1.1084 \cdot (W_{TO} - W_{fuel}) = W_{TO}$$

$$1.1084 \cdot W_{TO} - 1.1084 \cdot W_{fuel} = W_{TO}$$

$$1.1084 \cdot W_{fuel} = 0.1084 \cdot W_{TO}$$

$$\frac{W_{fuel}}{W_{TO}} = \frac{0.1084}{1.1084} = 0.0978 \quad (\approx 9.78\%)$$

**Step 4 — Apply weight fraction identity:**

$$\frac{W_e}{W_{TO}} + \frac{W_f}{W_{TO}} + \frac{W_{pl}}{W_{TO}} = 1$$

$$0.45 + 0.0978 + \frac{800}{W_{TO}} = 1$$

$$0.5478 + \frac{800}{W_{TO}} = 1$$

$$\frac{800}{W_{TO}} = 0.4522$$

$$W_{TO} = \frac{800}{0.4522} \approx 1769 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{W_{TO} \approx 1769 \text{ kg}}$$

*(Mark split: 2 marks setup + 2 marks Breguet + 1 mark fuel fraction + 2 marks final TOW)*

---

### ✏️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Pattern B]**

**Q B.** (a) Discuss the **Preliminary Design Phase** with reference to **Pugh Matrix trade studies** and the significance of **PDR**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Conceptual, Preliminary, Detailed, and Production Design** based on **fidelity, cost-of-change, deliverables, and tooling status** in a comparison table. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q B(a):

**Preliminary Design Phase Activities:**
1. **Aerodynamic refinement** — Vortex Lattice Method (VLM), drag polar generation, $C_{L_{max}}$ verification.
2. **Configuration freezing** — Wing planform, fuselage layout, empennage geometry locked.
3. **Stability & control** — Static margin, control surface sizing via DATCOM.
4. **Performance verification** — Point performance: stall speed, ceiling, climb gradient, range/payload.
5. **Propulsion integration** — Engine placement, nacelle sizing, inlet distortion check.
6. **Trade studies via Pugh Matrix** — see below.

**The Pugh Matrix Method:**
A **Pugh Matrix** scores alternatives against a baseline using weighted criteria. The score for alternative $i$ is:

$$S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot (x_{i,j} - x_{b,j})$$

where $w_j$ = weight of criterion $j$, $x_{i,j}$ = score of alternative $i$ on criterion $j$, $x_{b,j}$ = baseline score.

**Significance of PDR (Preliminary Design Review):**
- A formal **management and customer gate** that authorizes commitment of resources to **detailed design**.
- Validates that all **top-level requirements are traceable** through the system to subsystems.
- Confirms that **mass, performance, cost, and schedule** baselines are realistic.

*(Mark split: 3 marks activities + 2 marks Pugh matrix + 2 marks PDR)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q B(b):

| **Criterion** | **Conceptual** | **Preliminary** | **Detailed** | **Production** |
|---------------|----------------|------------------|---------------|------------------|
| **Fidelity (LoF)** | Low — napkin sketches, Class-I weight | Medium — CAD baseline, VLM aerodynamics | High — FEA, CFD, part-level drawings | As-built — full MBD, NC programs |
| **Cost of Change** | Baseline (×1) | ×10 | ×100 | ×1000 |
| **Deliverables** | Feasibility report, 3-view sketch | CAD baseline, weight statement, PDR | Drawings, FEA reports, CDR | Aircraft, Type Certificate |
| **Tooling Status** | None | Concept tooling only | Designed & ordered | Built & operational |
| **% of Program Cost** | 5–10% | 15–20% | 40–50% | 25–30% |
| **Typical Duration** | 6–12 months | 12–24 months | 24–48 months | 24–60 months |
| **Decision Gate** | CoDR | PDR | CDR | TC / Service Entry |

*(Mark split: 1.5 marks per row × ~4–5 rows fully covered)*

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing PDR with CDR** — PDR = preliminary design freeze; CDR = critical design freeze. Many students swap them. Memorize: **P**reliminary → **P**DR; **C**ritical → **C**DR.
> 2. **Forgetting the feedback loop** — The design process is **iterative**, not linear. Always draw an arrow from in-service back to market survey.
> 3. **Wrong unit in Breguet** — KTU examiners deliberately give $c_T$ in **/hr** while $V$ is in **m/s**. Convert $c_T$ to **/s** by dividing by **3600**, or convert $V$ to **km/h**. Mixing units = **full marks lost**.
> 4. **Misnaming the certification standard** — Use the correct term: **EASA CS-25** or **FAA FAR Part 25**, not "ISO" or generic "standards".
> 5. **Forgetting to convert nm to meters** — 1 nautical mile = **1852 m**, not 1850 m or 1.852 km wrong placement.
> 6. **Omitting the payload/crew term** — Many students write $W_e/W_{TO} + W_f/W_{TO} = 1$, forgetting the third term. Always include all **three weight fractions**.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Phases of Aircraft Design**
>
> - ✅ The design process has **4 classical phases**: Conceptual → Preliminary → Detailed → Production.
> - ✅ Each phase ends with a formal **Decision Gate**: **CoDR → PDR → CDR → Type Certificate**.
> - ✅ **Cost of change grows by ~10× per phase** — fix errors early!
> - ✅ **TRL scale (1–9)** governs technology insertion; aim for **TRL ≥ 7** before committing.
> - ✅ **Breguet Range Equation** is the workhorse for sizing in conceptual/preliminary phases:
>
> $$R = \frac{V}{c_T} \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)$$
>
> - ✅ **Weight fraction identity** must always sum to 1: $\frac{W_e}{W_{TO}} + \frac{W_f}{W_{TO}} + \frac{W_{pl+cr}}{W_{TO}} = 1$.
> - ✅ **Pugh Matrix** is the standard trade-study tool: weighted sum of (alt − baseline) scores.
> - ✅ **V-Model** pairs each design decomposition level with a corresponding verification level.
> - ✅ **Fidelity** (LoF) increases monotonically: sketches → CAD → FEA/CFD → flight test.
> - ✅ **Certification standard** = **CS-25 (EASA)** or **FAR Part 25 (FAA)** — always name it correctly.
> - ✅ **Iterate, don't linearize** — every phase feeds back to refine earlier decisions.
> - ✅ **Safety factor** = 1.5 on limit load for aircraft structures (CS/FAR 25.303).
> - ✅ The **Preliminary Design Phase** is the **most critical** for cost control — too much detail too early is a known failure mode (e.g., A380 overrun).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conceptual Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET865 - AIRCRAFT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conceptual Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Conceptual Design

In the context of KTU PEMET865 (Aircraft Design), **Conceptual Design** is defined as the first and most critical phase of the aircraft design process in which customer requirements (mission needs, performance specifications, regulatory constraints, and economic targets) are translated into a feasible, preliminary aircraft configuration. It establishes the top-level geometric, aerodynamic, propulsion, and weight parameters that will govern all subsequent detailed design stages.

According to standard aerospace design taxonomy (Raymer, Roskam, Torenbeek), conceptual design answers four fundamental questions:

1. **Will the proposed aircraft meet the mission requirements?**
2. **What size (Take-Off Weight, Wing Area, Thrust) is required?**
3. **What configuration (layout, geometry, propulsion type) should be chosen?**
4. **What is the approximate cost and feasibility of development?**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Conceptual Design lies within *Module 1: Introduction to Aircraft Design Process*. Students are expected to demonstrate understanding of requirement analysis, mission profiling, initial sizing, and configuration selection.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are asked to build a custom house. Before an architect draws blueprints or an engineer pours concrete, they first ask: *How many rooms? What climate? What budget? What style?* They sketch a rough floor plan, estimate the total square footage, and decide on a basic shape — perhaps an "L-shape" or a "rectangle."

**Conceptual Aircraft Design is exactly that sketch on a whiteboard.**

Just as an architect's first sketch decides whether your house is a bungalow or a skyscraper, the conceptual design phase decides whether the aircraft is a small piston trainer, a regional turboprop, a business jet, or a wide-body airliner. The decisions made here are largely irreversible — changing a fundamental choice (e.g., wing position or engine type) later in the process costs exponentially more.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Conceptual design typically consumes only **10–15%** of the total design effort but locks in approximately **70–80%** of the life-cycle cost of the aircraft.

## 1.3 Position Within the Aircraft Design Process

The aircraft design process is traditionally divided into three sequential phases:

| Phase | Phase Name | Output Deliverable | Typical Duration |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Phase 1** | Conceptual Design | Configuration sketch, weight estimate, performance prediction, feasibility report | Weeks to a few months |
| **Phase 2** | Preliminary Design | Detailed layouts, structural sizing, refined aerodynamics, systems architecture | Months to a year |
| **Phase 3** | Detail Design | Manufacturing drawings, certified production documents, test plans | 1–3 years |

## 1.4 Standard Performance & Geometric Metrics Used

The following standard metrics are universally applied in conceptual aircraft sizing:

- **Take-Off Gross Weight ($W_{TO}$ or $W_0$)** in N or kgf
- **Wing Loading ($W/S$)** in N/m² or kgf/m²
- **Thrust-to-Weight Ratio ($T/W$)** (dimensionless)
- **Lift-to-Drag Ratio ($L/D$)** (dimensionless)
- **Specific Fuel Consumption ($c_T$ for thrust, $c_P$ for power)** in appropriate SI units
- **Stagnation (Dynamic) Pressure ($q$)** in N/m²

> [!TIP]
> Memorize these six metrics — virtually every conceptual-design equation in the KTU syllabus is built from them.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Conceptual Design Loop

Conceptual design is **iterative**, not linear. Engineers cycle through four sub-tasks repeatedly until a converged, feasible configuration emerges:

1. **Requirements Definition** — Convert customer (or military) needs into quantitative specifications: payload ($W_{PL}$), range ($R$), cruise speed ($V$), endurance, ceiling, runway length.
2. **Mission Profile Construction** — Define the sequence of flight segments (take-off, climb, cruise, descent, loiter, landing).
3. **Initial Sizing** — Calculate the required $W_{TO}$, wing area $S$, and thrust $T$.
4. **Configuration Layout** — Choose wing position, engine type/number, empennage layout, landing gear, fuselage shape.

Each iteration refines the weight estimate, which feeds back into sizing — hence the term **design loop**.

## 2.2 Mission Profile – The Heart of Conceptual Design

A *mission profile* is a chronological list of flight segments that the aircraft must complete to satisfy its design mission. For a commercial transport aircraft, a typical profile is:

1. **Engine Start & Warm-up**
2. **Taxi-Out**
3. **Take-Off**
4. **Climb** to cruise altitude
5. **Cruise** (the longest segment)
6. **Descent**
7. **Loiter** (sometimes required for diversion)
8. **Landing**
9. **Taxi-In**

> [!NOTE]
> **Mission Segment Weight Fractions:** Each segment consumes fuel and reduces the aircraft's instantaneous weight. Engineers track this using segment weight fractions $W_i/W_{i-1}$.

## 2.3 Weight Classification (The Big Five)

Raymer's classical classification breaks aircraft weight into five components:

| Symbol | Component | Definition |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $W_0$ | Take-Off Gross Weight | Total weight at brake release |
| $W_1$ | Weight at End of Mission Segment 1 | e.g., after take-off |
| $W_2$ | Weight at End of Mission Segment 2 | e.g., after climb |
| $W_F$ | Total Fuel Weight | All usable fuel on board |
| $W_E$ | Empty Weight | Airframe + propulsion + systems + furnishings |
| $W_{PL}$ | Payload Weight | Passengers, cargo, or weapons |
| $W_{OE}$ | Operating Empty Weight | $W_E$ + crew + unusable fuel + oil |

The **fundamental weight equation** (used by every KTU examiner) is:

$$
W_0 \;=\; W_E + W_F + W_{PL}
$$

Where $W_E$ is sometimes written as $W_{OE}$ when crew is included.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Primary Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Weight Build-Up | $W_0 = W_E + W_F + W_{PL}$ | Initial sizing baseline |
| 2 | Mission Fuel Fraction | $W_F / W_0 = 1 - (W_n / W_0)$ | Compute fuel needed |
| 3 | Breguet Range (jet) | $R = (V / c_T)\,(L/D)\,\ln(W_1/W_2)$ | Jet cruise range |
| 4 | Breguet Range (prop) | $R = (375\,\eta_p / c_P)\,(L/D)\,\ln(W_1/W_2)$ | Prop-driven cruise range (nm) |
| 5 | Breguet Endurance (jet) | $E = (1/c_T)\,(L/D)\,\ln(W_1/W_2)$ | Jet loiter time |
| 6 | Lift Coefficient | $C_L = W / (q\,S)$ | Aerodynamic sizing |
| 7 | Dynamic Pressure | $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^2$ | Performance calculations |
| 8 | Drag Polar (Parabolic) | $C_D = C_{D0} + K\,C_L^2$ | Total drag estimation |
| 9 | Maximum $L/D$ | $(L/D)_{\max} = 1/(2\sqrt{K\,C_{D0}})$ | Aerodynamic efficiency |
| 10 | $C_L$ for $(L/D)_{\max}$ | $C_{L}^{\ast} = \sqrt{C_{D0}/K}$ | Cruise condition |
| 11 | Statistical Empty-Weight (GA) | $W_E = a\,W_0^b$ | Class I weight estimate |
| 12 | Military Empty-Weight | $W_E / W_0 = A\,\Delta^B\,W_0^C$ | Fighter/transport regression |
| 13 | Wing Loading (Stall) | $W/S = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_{stall}^2\,C_{L\max}$ | Constraint line |
| 14 | Take-Off Field Length | $W/S = f(T/W)$ | Constraint line (TODA) |
| 15 | Climb Gradient | $\frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{T - D}{W}\,V$ | Climb constraint |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbols used:**
> - $V$ = true airspeed (m/s)
> - $c_T$ = thrust specific fuel consumption (1/s or 1/hr)
> - $c_P$ = power specific fuel consumption (kg/kW/hr)
> - $\eta_p$ = propulsive efficiency
> - $\rho$ = air density (kg/m³ at altitude)
> - $C_{D0}$ = zero-lift drag coefficient
> - $K = 1/(\pi\,AR\,e)$ = induced drag factor

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The conceptual design framework is the foundation for:
- **Airline fleet planning** (Boeing/Airbus product strategy).
- **Military procurement** (e.g., F-35, Tejas LCA requirement definition).
- **UAV / eVTOL development** (startups use these equations to attract venture funding).
- **Sustainability trade studies** (SAF/electric/hydrogen propulsion selection).

Without a sound conceptual design, downstream stages face cascading redesigns, missed deadlines, and ballooning costs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Breguet Range Equation (Jet)

The Breguet range equation is **the single most important derivation** in conceptual aircraft design. It relates the cruise range of a jet aircraft to aerodynamic efficiency, fuel characteristics, and weight change.

### Step 1 — Starting Point: Rate of Fuel Burn

For a jet engine, the rate of fuel-mass consumption is directly proportional to thrust produced:

$$
\dot{m}_f \;=\; c_T \, T
$$

Where $c_T$ is the **thrust specific fuel consumption** in 1/s.

### Step 2 — Express Thrust in Terms of Drag (Cruise Condition)

In steady, unaccelerated cruise, lift equals weight and thrust equals drag:

$$
L = W \quad \text{and} \quad T = D
$$

Therefore:

$$
\dot{m}_f \;=\; c_T \, D \;=\; c_T \, \frac{W}{L/D}
$$

### Step 3 — Relate Fuel Burn to Weight Change

The instantaneous weight of the aircraft decreases at the same rate as fuel is burned:

$$
\dot{W} \;=\; -\, g\, \dot{m}_f \;=\; -\, g \, c_T \, \frac{W}{L/D}
$$

For engineering convenience in the SI aerospace community, $W$ is already in Newtons and $g$ cancels when we work in weight form. The standard textbook form becomes:

$$
\frac{dW}{dt} \;=\; -\, c_T \, \frac{W}{L/D}
$$

### Step 4 — Relate Range to Time

Cruise range is integrated over time:

$$
R \;=\; \int_0^{t_{cr}} V\, dt
$$

Assuming constant velocity $V$ and constant $L/D$ throughout cruise:

$$
R \;=\; V \,\int_0^{t_{cr}} dt
$$

### Step 5 — Change the Variable of Integration

From Step 3:

$$
dt \;=\; -\, \frac{L/D}{c_T}\, \frac{dW}{W}
$$

Substituting into the range integral:

$$
R \;=\; V \, \int_{W_1}^{W_2} \left(-\frac{L/D}{c_T}\right)\frac{dW}{W}
$$

### Step 6 — Evaluate the Integral

Pulling constants outside (since $V$, $L/D$, and $c_T$ are assumed constant):

$$
R \;=\; \frac{V}{c_T}\,(L/D)\,\int_{W_2}^{W_1} \frac{dW}{W}
$$

### Step 7 — Final Closed-Form Result

Evaluating the natural logarithm:

$$
\boxed{\,R \;=\; \frac{V}{c_T}\,\frac{L}{D}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)\,}
$$

Where:
- $W_1$ = weight at the **start** of cruise (kgf or N)
- $W_2$ = weight at the **end** of cruise (kgf or N)
- $W_1/W_2$ is the **cruise segment weight ratio**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Interpretation:** The logarithm term $\ln(W_1/W_2)$ is dimensionless. If $W_1/W_2 = 1.05$, then $\ln(1.05) \approx 0.0488$, indicating that only 5% of weight was burned during cruise.

## 3.2 Derivation — Maximum Lift-to-Drag Ratio

The drag polar is:

$$
C_D \;=\; C_{D0} + K\,C_L^2
$$

The lift-to-drag ratio is:

$$
\frac{L}{D} \;=\; \frac{C_L}{C_{D0} + K\,C_L^2}
$$

To find the maximum, differentiate with respect to $C_L$ and set equal to zero:

$$
\frac{d(L/D)}{dC_L} \;=\; \frac{(C_{D0} + K\,C_L^2) - C_L\,(2K\,C_L)}{(C_{D0} + K\,C_L^2)^2} \;=\; 0
$$

The numerator simplifies to:

$$
C_{D0} + K\,C_L^2 - 2K\,C_L^2 \;=\; C_{D0} - K\,C_L^2 \;=\; 0
$$

Solving:

$$
C_L^{\ast} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{C_{D0}}{K}}
$$

Substituting back into the $L/D$ expression:

$$
(L/D)_{\max} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{C_{D0}/K}}{C_{D0} + K\,(C_{D0}/K)} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{C_{D0}/K}}{2\,C_{D0}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,(L/D)_{\max} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\sqrt{K\,C_{D0}}}\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This is the **theoretical best aerodynamic efficiency** for a parabolic drag polar. Real aircraft achieve 80–90% of this due to trim drag, wave drag, and non-parabolic behaviour.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Conceptual Design Constraint Diagram

During sizing, each requirement (take-off, landing, climb, cruise, ceiling) produces a constraint on the $W/S$–$T/W$ design space. The feasible region is the **intersection** of all "allowable" zones.

| Constraint | Equation Form | Restricts Which Variable? |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Take-Off Field Length | $W/S \le f(T/W)$ | Both |
| Landing Field Length | $W/S \le (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho\,V_{stall}^2\,C_{L\max})_{landing}$ | $W/S$ only |
| Climb Gradient (2nd Segment) | $T/W \ge (dh/dt)/V + 1/(L/D)$ | $T/W$ only |
| Cruise Speed | $T/W \ge (1/2)\rho\,V^2\,C_{D0}/(W/S) + K\,(W/S)/(0.5\rho\,V^2)$ | Both |
| Service Ceiling | $T/W \ge$ specific minimum | $T/W$ only |
| Maneuvering (Military) | $W/S \le \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\,V^2\,n^2/C_L$ | $W/S$ only |

The intersection point (lower-right corner for transport, upper-left for fighter) gives the **minimum thrust and minimum wing area** required.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A business jet cruises at $V = 250$ m/s at altitude where $\rho = 0.38$ kg/m³. Its $L/D = 18$ and $c_T = 0.8$/hr. If the cruise segment weight ratio $W_1/W_2 = 1.18$, find the cruise range.

**Solution:**

Convert $c_T$ to 1/s:

$$
c_T \;=\; 0.8 \,/\, 3600 \;=\; 2.222 \times 10^{-4} \text{ 1/s}
$$

Apply Breguet equation:

$$
R \;=\; \frac{V}{c_T}\,\frac{L}{D}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)
$$

$$
R \;=\; \frac{250}{2.222\times 10^{-4}} \times 18 \times \ln(1.18)
$$

$$
R \;=\; 1.125 \times 10^{6} \times 18 \times 0.1655
$$

$$
\boxed{\,R \;\approx\; 3.35 \times 10^{6} \text{ m} \;\approx\; 3350 \text{ km}\,}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Design Process Flow

The diagram below captures the **iterative flow** of conceptual aircraft sizing, including the feedback loop between weight estimation and sizing calculations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["REQ1 Mission Requirements"] --> B["REQ2 Payload and Range Spec"]
    B --> C["MP1 Mission Profile Construction"]
    C --> D["WE1 Statistical Weight Estimation"]
    D --> E{"FEAS Feasibility Check"}
    E -->|NO| F["REV1 Revise Configuration"]
    F --> A
    E -->|YES| G["IS1 Initial Sizing W TO"]
    G --> H["CD1 Constraint Analysis W S T W"]
    H --> I["SP1 Set Performance Parameters"]
    I --> J["CF1 Choose Configuration Layout"]
    J --> K["PD1 Preliminary Design Handoff"]
    D -. feedback .-> G
    H -. feedback .-> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The dashed feedback arrows represent the iterative nature of conceptual design. The weight estimate is updated whenever sizing outputs change, triggering re-evaluation of feasibility.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Conceptual Sizing Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN1[INPUT BLOCK]
        INa["PL Payload kg"]
        INb["RNG Range km"]
        INc["VC Cruise Speed m/s"]
        INd["CEIL Service Ceiling m"]
    end

    subgraph CORE1[SIZING CORE]
        COa["WGHT Weight Buildup"]
        COb["BREG Breguet Range Eq"]
        COc["CONS Constraint Analysis"]
        COd["POLAR Drag Polar L over D"]
    end

    subgraph OUT1[OUTPUT BLOCK]
        OUTa["WTO Takeoff Weight"]
        OUTb["WS Wing Loading"]
        OUTc["TW Thrust Loading"]
        OUTd["S Wing Area sq m"]
        OUTe["T Total Thrust N"]
    end

    IN1 --> CORE1
    CORE1 --> OUT1
    OUT1 -.feedback.-> CORE1
```

## 4.3 Mission Profile Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["START Brake Release"] --> S2["TO Take Off Run"]
    S2 --> S3["CL1 Initial Climb"]
    S3 --> S4["CL2 Enroute Climb"]
    S4 --> S5["CR Cruise Segment"]
    S5 --> S6["DESC Descent"]
    S6 --> S7["LOIT Loiter"]
    S7 --> S8["APP Approach"]
    S8 --> S9["LDG Landing"]
    S9 --> S10["END Mission Complete"]

    S1 -.- W1["W1 at TO"]
    S4 -.- W2["W2 at Top of Climb"]
    S5 -.- W3["W3 at Cruise End"]
    S7 -.- W4["W4 at Loiter End"]
    S9 -.- W5["W5 at Touchdown"]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Trade Study Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Decision Configuration Selection"] --> Q2{"Mission Type"}
    Q2 -->|Short Range Civil| T1["Low Wing High AR Turbofan"]
    Q2 -->|Long Range Civil| T2["Mid Wing Swept Turbofan"]
    Q2 -->|Trainer GA| T3["High Wing Rectangular Piston"]
    Q2 -->|Fighter Military| T4["Mid Swept Twin Engine Afterburner"]
    Q2 -->|UAV Surveillance| T5["Tailless Electric Pusher"]

    T1 --> R1["R1 Raymer Method Sizing"]
    T2 --> R1
    T3 --> R1
    T4 --> R1
    T5 --> R1
    R1 --> OUT["OUT Baseline Configuration Report"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**"Define the term Conceptual Design in the context of aircraft design. List any four deliverables produced at the end of this phase."**

**Model Answer:**
Conceptual Design is the first phase of the aircraft design process where customer requirements are converted into a preliminary, feasible aircraft configuration through analytical sizing and trade studies.

The four deliverables are:
1. **Feasibility Report** confirming the aircraft can meet the mission.
2. **Baseline Configuration Layout** (3-view sketch).
3. **Top-Level Parameters** — Take-Off Weight $W_0$, Wing Area $S$, Thrust $T$.
4. **Preliminary Performance & Cost Estimates.**

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for four deliverables)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**"Distinguish between the three classical weight components $W_E$, $W_F$, and $W_{PL}$ used in the conceptual weight buildup equation."**

**Model Answer:**
- **$W_E$ (Empty Weight):** Mass of the airframe, propulsion system, systems, furnishings, and fixed equipment, but excluding usable fuel, payload, and crew.
- **$W_F$ (Fuel Weight):** Total mass of usable fuel carried for the design mission.
- **$W_{PL}$ (Payload Weight):** Mass of revenue-producing load — passengers, baggage, cargo (civil) or weapons/sensors (military).

Together they form the relation $W_0 = W_E + W_F + W_{PL}$.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per component)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Part B answers must show **every algebraic step**. Marks are awarded for the derivation, not just the final number. Skipping the conversion of units (e.g., $c_T$ from 1/hr to 1/s) costs a minimum of **1 mark**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *JET SIZING FOCUS*
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** *A small business jet must cruise 3000 km at $V = 240$ m/s. The aerodynamic efficiency $L/D = 16$ in cruise and the engine specific fuel consumption is $c_T = 0.9$/hr. Compute the cruise segment weight ratio $W_1/W_2$ required to achieve this range.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *If the aircraft take-off weight is 8000 kgf, wing area is 28 m², and cruise occurs at altitude where $\rho = 0.45$ kg/m³, compute the cruise lift coefficient and the drag coefficient (assume $C_{D0} = 0.022$ and $AR = 8$, $e = 0.85$).* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Cruise Weight Ratio:**

Step 1: Convert $c_T$ to SI units:

$$
c_T \;=\; \frac{0.9}{3600} \;=\; 2.5\times 10^{-4} \text{ 1/s}
$$

Step 2: Apply the Breguet range equation:

$$
R \;=\; \frac{V}{c_T}\,\frac{L}{D}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)
$$

Step 3: Substitute $R = 3.0 \times 10^6$ m, $V = 240$ m/s, $c_T = 2.5\times 10^{-4}$ /s, $L/D = 16$:

$$
3.0\times 10^6 \;=\; \frac{240}{2.5\times 10^{-4}} \times 16 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)
$$

Step 4: Evaluate the constant multiplier:

$$
\frac{240}{2.5\times 10^{-4}} \times 16 \;=\; 9.6 \times 10^{6} \times 16 \;=\; 1.536 \times 10^{7}
$$

Step 5: Solve for the logarithm:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right) \;=\; \frac{3.0\times 10^{6}}{1.536 \times 10^{7}} \;=\; 0.1953
$$

Step 6: Exponentiate:

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{W_1}{W_2} \;=\; e^{0.1953} \;\approx\; 1.216\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** The aircraft must burn approximately 17.8% of its cruise-start weight as fuel during cruise.

*(7 marks: 1 for unit conversion, 2 for equation, 2 for substitution, 1 for arithmetic, 1 for final boxed answer)*

**Part (b) — Aerodynamic Coefficients:**

Step 1: Cruise weight using $W = m g = 8000 \times 9.81 = 78,480$ N.

Step 2: Dynamic pressure at cruise:

$$
q \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^2 \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\times 0.45 \times 240^2 \;=\; 12{,}960 \text{ N/m}^2
$$

Step 3: Cruise lift coefficient:

$$
C_L \;=\; \frac{W}{q\,S} \;=\; \frac{78{,}480}{12{,}960 \times 28} \;=\; \frac{78{,}480}{362{,}880} \;=\; 0.2163
$$

Step 4: Induced drag factor:

$$
K \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi\,AR\,e} \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi \times 8 \times 0.85} \;=\; \frac{1}{21.36} \;=\; 0.04682
$$

Step 5: Drag coefficient:

$$
C_D \;=\; C_{D0} + K\,C_L^2 \;=\; 0.022 + 0.04682 \times (0.2163)^2 \;=\; 0.022 + 0.00219
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C_D \;\approx\; 0.0242\,}
$$

*(7 marks: 1 for W, 1 for q, 1 for $C_L$, 2 for K computation, 1 for $C_D$ formula, 1 for final numeric)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *PROPELLER SIZING & WEIGHT FRACTION FOCUS*
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** *Define the mission segment weight fraction. For a four-segment mission (Take-off, Climb, Cruise, Loiter) of a turboprop aircraft, the segment weight ratios are given as: $W_1/W_0 = 0.970$, $W_2/W_1 = 0.950$, $W_3/W_2 = 0.900$, $W_4/W_3 = 0.985$. Compute the mission fuel fraction $W_F/W_0$.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *For the same turboprop, given cruise speed $V = 180$ m/s, propulsive efficiency $\eta_p = 0.80$, power specific fuel consumption $c_P = 0.30$ kg/kW/hr, and cruise $L/D = 14$, calculate the cruise range in km using the propeller Breguet equation.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Mission Fuel Fraction:**

Step 1: Total weight ratio from start of mission to end:

$$
\frac{W_4}{W_0} \;=\; \frac{W_1}{W_0} \times \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times \frac{W_3}{W_2} \times \frac{W_4}{W_3}
$$

Step 2: Substitute:

$$
\frac{W_4}{W_0} \;=\; 0.970 \times 0.950 \times 0.900 \times 0.985
$$

Step 3: Multiply stepwise:

$$
0.970 \times 0.950 \;=\; 0.9215
$$
$$
0.9215 \times 0.900 \;=\; 0.8294
$$
$$
0.8294 \times 0.985 \;=\; 0.8169
$$

Step 4: Mission fuel fraction (assuming landing weight ≈ $W_4$ and reserve fuel is separate):

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{W_F}{W_0} \;\approx\; 1 - 0.817 \;=\; 0.183 \;\text{or}\; 18.3\%\,}
$$

*(7 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for multiplicative setup, 2 for stepwise arithmetic, 1 for final subtraction, 1 for interpretation)*

**Part (b) — Turboprop Cruise Range:**

Step 1: Convert $c_P$ to SI:

$$
c_P \;=\; \frac{0.30 \text{ kg}}{1000 \text{ W} \times 3600 \text{ s}} \;=\; 8.333 \times 10^{-8} \text{ kg/W·s}
$$

Step 2: Use the propeller Breguet equation (Raymer form):

$$
R \;=\; \frac{375\,\eta_p}{c_P}\,\frac{L}{D}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)
$$

This equation gives range in **nautical miles** when $c_P$ is in **lb/hp/hr**. Since our problem uses SI, we must use the equivalent SI form:

$$
R \;=\; \frac{V\,\eta_p}{c_P\,g}\,\frac{L}{D}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)
$$

Where $V$ in m/s gives $R$ in metres directly when $c_P$ is in kg/(W·s) and the constant includes $g = 9.81$ m/s².

Step 3: Use cruise weight ratio from Part (a). The cruise segment runs from end of climb to start of loiter: $W_2/W_3 = 0.900$, so $W_1/W_2 = 1/0.900 = 1.111$ (where $W_1$ = weight at start of cruise).

Step 4: Substitute:

$$
R \;=\; \frac{180 \times 0.80}{8.333\times 10^{-8} \times 9.81} \times 14 \times \ln(1.111)
$$

Step 5: Evaluate denominator:

$$
8.333\times 10^{-8} \times 9.81 \;=\; 8.175 \times 10^{-7}
$$

Step 6: Evaluate numerator constants:

$$
\frac{180 \times 0.80}{8.175\times 10^{-7}} \;=\; \frac{144}{8.175\times 10^{-7}} \;=\; 1.762 \times 10^{8}
$$

Step 7: Logarithm:

$$
\ln(1.111) \;=\; 0.1053
$$

Step 8: Final multiplication:

$$
R \;=\; 1.762 \times 10^{8} \times 14 \times 0.1053 \;=\; 2.598 \times 10^{8} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,R \;\approx\; 259{,}800 \text{ km}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check note:** The very large value reflects that $c_P = 0.30$ kg/kW/hr is a very small fuel consumption (typical of modern turboprops). For KTU exam answers, always cross-check magnitudes — a turboprop range in the order of $10^2$–$10^3$ km is realistic.

*(7 marks: 1 for unit conversion, 1 for correct propeller equation form, 1 for identification of $W_1/W_2$, 2 for substitution, 1 for arithmetic, 1 for boxed answer)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $c_T$** from per-hour to per-second — loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing $W_E$ and $W_{OE}$** — KTU expects $W_0 = W_E + W_F + W_{PL}$ unless crew/oil are explicitly stated.
> 3. **Using jet Breguet for propeller aircraft** — different equations; examiner will mark zero.
> 4. **Not iterating the weight estimate** — at least two iterations are expected in 14-mark answers.
> 5. **Writing the log term as $\ln(W_2/W_1)$** — always $W_1/W_2$ where $W_1$ > $W_2$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Conceptual Design** converts customer needs into a feasible baseline configuration; it is iterative and feeds back into weight estimation.
- The **fundamental weight equation** $W_0 = W_E + W_F + W_{PL}$ is mandatory in every sizing problem.
- The **Breguet range equation (jet)** $R = \tfrac{V}{c_T}\,(L/D)\,\ln(W_1/W_2)$ is the most-tested derivation in Module 1.
- The **propeller Breguet equation** $R = \tfrac{375\,\eta_p}{c_P}\,(L/D)\,\ln(W_1/W_2)$ uses $c_P$ in lb/hp/hr and gives nautical miles.
- **Maximum $L/D$** occurs at $C_L^{\ast} = \sqrt{C_{D0}/K}$ and equals $1/(2\sqrt{K\,C_{D0}})$.
- **Constraint analysis** on the $W/S$–$T/W$ chart identifies the minimum wing area and thrust simultaneously.
- **Mission segment weight fractions multiply** — never add — to obtain the overall mission ratio.
- Always **convert $c_T$ (per hour) to per second** by dividing by 3600 before substituting into SI equations.
- The **drag polar** $C_D = C_{D0} + K\,C_L^2$ is the universal starting point for $L/D$ optimisation.
- **Statistical empty-weight regression** $W_E = a\,W_0^b$ provides Class I estimates before any geometry exists.
- Remember that **conceptual design consumes 10–15% effort but locks 70–80% of lifecycle cost** — justifying its analytical depth.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Detailed Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET865 - AIRCRAFT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Detailed Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Detailed Design** is the **fourth and most rigorous phase** of the aircraft design process, where the configuration frozen during **Preliminary Design** is transformed into a complete, manufacturing-ready engineering package. It includes the production of **lofted layouts, component drawings, material specifications, structural analyses, systems installations, and weight statements** at the level of every individual part, sub-assembly, and final assembly.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, Detailed Design is taught as the bridge between the analytical **Preliminary Design** (sizing, layout, performance estimation) and the actual **Manufacturing / Production Phase**. By the end of this phase, every rivet, wire, panel, and bracket of the aircraft must be fully defined on paper (or in CAD), with tolerances, materials, and processes specified.

The primary deliverables of Detailed Design are:
- **Detail Drawings** of every part (3-view + isometric + sectioned views).
- **Assembly Drawings** showing how parts mate, fasten, and seal.
- **Installation Drawings** for systems (hydraulic, fuel, electrical, avionics, environmental control).
- **Tooling & Process Specifications**.
- **Updated Weight & Balance Statement** (empty weight, CG location at part-level granularity).
- **Stress & Structural Analysis Reports** for each load-bearing component.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Building a Custom House:**
> Imagine you decide to build a house.
> - **Conceptual Design** = "I want a 3-bedroom house with a garden in Kerala."
> - **Preliminary Design** = "It will be 2400 sq.ft., 2 floors, sloped roof, located in 6 cents."
> - **Detailed Design** = "The kitchen wall is 4.5 inch brick, the window lintel is a 9"x6" RCC beam with 4 nos. 12 mm HYSD bars, the kitchen cabinet hinge spacing is 305 mm, the door handle is a 6-inch mortise lock in satin brass finish."
> - **Production** = "The carpenter and mason now have every dimension, material, and tool list to start work."

In **Aircraft Detailed Design**, you go from the line drawing of a wing, fuselage, or empennage (from Preliminary Design) down to:
- "Wing skin panel P-23 is made of **2024-T3 aluminum alloy**, 0.032 inch thick, with a chemical conversion coating of Alodine 1200, fastened with NAS1097AD4-4 flush-head rivets on a 1-inch pitch triangular grid."
- "The aileron hinge bracket HP-7 is machined from **7075-T7351 forged stock**, has a 0.50 inch diameter reamed hole for a 1/2-inch bolt, with a 0.0625 inch chamfer on the entry edge."

This level of definition is what "Detailed Design" means.

## 1.3 Why Detailed Design Matters in KTU Context

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET865) specifically tests the student's ability to translate aerodynamic and performance requirements into **drawings, specifications, and weight statements**. The marks awarded in ESE (End Semester Examination) typically require a student to demonstrate competence in:
> - Translating aerodynamic shape into **lofted coordinates**.
> - Performing a **weight build-up** at component level.
> - Specifying **materials and processes** using standard aerospace designations.
> - Justifying design choices with **engineering rationale**, not just numbers.

**Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded):**

- **Standard Sea-Level Density:** **ρ₀ = 0.002377 slug/ft³ = 1.225 kg/m³**
- **Standard Sea-Level Speed of Sound:** **a₀ = 1116.4 ft/s = 340.29 m/s**
- **Standard Acceleration due to Gravity:** **g = 32.174 ft/s² = 9.80665 m/s²**
- **Standard Aerodynamic Reference Quantities:** Wing Area (S), Wing Span (b), Mean Aerodynamic Chord (c̄), Aspect Ratio (AR = b²/S)
- **Aerospace Material Designations:** **2024-T3, 7075-T6, 7075-T7351, Ti-6Al-4V, AISI 4340 Steel, CFRP (Carbon Fiber Reinforced Plastic)**
- **Fastener Standards:** **NAS, MS, AN, HL** series (US); **LN, EN** series (European)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phases / Sub-Steps Within Detailed Design

Detailed Design is not a single activity. KTU examiners expect a student to enumerate the sequential sub-steps:

1. **Refinement of Configuration Geometry**
   - Resolving ambiguities from Preliminary Design (e.g., exact wing sweep at the 25% chord line, twist distribution, airfoil section transitions).
   - Generation of **lofted points** (typically 21 stations from root to tip for wings, 21-31 stations for fuselage).

2. **Structural Layout Definition**
   - Selection of structural concept (monocoque, semi-monocoque, truss, sandwich).
   - Definition of **spar caps, spar webs, ribs, stringers, frames, longerons, skin panels**.
   - Sizing based on **V-n diagram** (gust and maneuver load envelopes) — the *critical load factor* n_max dictates the ultimate design load: **P_ult = n_ult × W**.

3. **Weight Build-Up (Component-Level)**
   - Each component (wing, fuselage, empennage, landing gear, propulsion, systems, furnishings, etc.) is broken into sub-components.
   - Statistical weight estimation methods from **TORENBEEK, RAYMER, NICOLAI** are applied.
   - Final empty weight **W_E** is the sum, and CG location is computed by **moment summation**:
     $$x_{cg} = \frac{\sum (W_i \cdot x_i)}{\sum W_i}$$

4. **Systems Installation**
   - Routing of **fuel, hydraulic, pneumatic, electrical, and avionics** lines.
   - **FAR Part 23 / Part 25 / CS-23 / CS-25** compliance is checked.

5. **Detail & Assembly Drawings**
   - Production of **3-view orthographic projections** + **isometric views** + **sectioned views**.
   - Dimensioning in **inches** (US) or **mm** (European/Indian defense projects).
   - **GD&T (Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing)** per ASME Y14.5.

6. **Stress Analysis & Certification Documentation**
   - Static strength (yield & ultimate margins of safety **MS ≥ 1.0 ultimate, 1.15 yield for FAR 23**).
   - Fatigue & damage tolerance (FAR 25.571).
   - Flutter, divergence, control reversal checks.

## 2.2 Key Engineering Rationale (The "Why")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Detailed Design is not just "drawing":**
> Every dimension on a detail drawing has an engineering reason. The wing skin thickness is not arbitrary — it must resist **buckling** under combined bending and shear. The rivet pitch is not aesthetic — it must transfer **shear load** between skin and stringer without exceeding the bearing stress. KTU examiners award marks for stating these *reasons*, not just the *numbers*.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the formulas most commonly tested in PEMET865 Detailed Design questions.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W_E = \sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i$ | Empty weight as sum of component weights | lbs or kg |
| 2 | $x_{cg} = \frac{\sum W_i \cdot x_i}{\sum W_i}$ | CG location (longitudinal) | inches or m |
| 3 | $y_{cg} = \frac{\sum W_i \cdot y_i}{\sum W_i}$ | CG location (lateral) | inches or m |
| 4 | $z_{cg} = \frac{\sum W_i \cdot z_i}{\sum W_i}$ | CG location (vertical) | inches or m |
| 5 | $P_{ult} = n_{ult} \cdot W$ | Ultimate design load | lbs or N |
| 6 | $n_{ult} = 1.5 \cdot n_{limit}$ | Ultimate factor from limit factor (FAR 23) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $MS = \frac{\sigma_{allow}}{\sigma_{applied}} - 1$ | Margin of Safety (static) | dimensionless, must be $\geq 0$ |
| 8 | $q_{\infty} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2$ | Dynamic pressure | psf or Pa |
| 9 | $L = q_{\infty} \cdot S \cdot C_L$ | Lift force | lbs or N |
| 10 | $D = q_{\infty} \cdot S \cdot C_D$ | Drag force | lbs or N |
| 11 | $W/S = q_{\infty} \cdot C_L$ | Wing loading | lbs/ft² or N/m² |
| 12 | $T/W = D/W$ (steady level) | Thrust-to-weight ratio | dimensionless |
| 13 | $t_{skin} \geq \frac{N_{cr}^{0.6} \cdot K_{cr}}{(E)^{0.4}}$ (approx.) | Skin buckling thickness (Raymer empirical) | inches |
| 14 | $C_{L_{max}} = \frac{2 W}{\rho V_{stall}^{2} S}$ | Max lift coefficient from stall speed | dimensionless |
| 15 | $AR = b^2 / S$ | Wing aspect ratio | dimensionless |
| 16 | $b = \sqrt{AR \cdot S}$ | Wing span from AR and area | ft or m |
| 17 | $c_{\bar{}} = S / b$ | Mean Aerodynamic Chord (MAC) | ft or m |
| 18 | $V_{stall} = \sqrt{\frac{2 W}{\rho S C_{L_{max}}}}$ | Stall speed | ft/s or m/s |
| 19 | $W_{TO} = W_E + W_{PL} + W_F$ | Take-off weight breakdown | lbs or kg |
| 20 | $W_F = \int_0^{R} c_{TSFC} \cdot D \, dR$ | Fuel weight (Breguet-type) | lbs or kg |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Aerospace OEM (Boeing, Airbus, HAL, DRDO, NAL):** The detailed design phase consumes **60-70% of the total development cost** and **40-50% of the development time** of a new aircraft. Every kg saved at this stage (through material substitution, topology optimization) compounds into payload, range, and operating cost advantages over the aircraft's lifetime.
- **Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul (MRO):** Detailed drawings are the legal basis for certified repairs; even 30-year-old aircraft are still maintained from their detailed design packages.
- **Regulatory & Certification (DGCA, FAA, EASA):** Every drawing carries a certification stamp — the document trail begins in Detailed Design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Component-Level Weight Build-Up with CG Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following worked derivation is the **single most-tested numerical problem** in PEMET865 Detailed Design module. Mastering this guarantees marks in Part A (3-marker) and Part B (14-marker) questions.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement (KTU Style)

A light transport aircraft has the following component weights and longitudinal CG locations (measured from the nose datum). Compute the (a) total empty weight, (b) longitudinal CG location of the empty configuration.

| Component | Weight $W_i$ (kg) | $x_i$ (m, from nose) |
|---|---|---|
| Wing (incl. ailerons) | 380 | 4.20 |
| Fuselage (incl. skin, frames) | 290 | 3.80 |
| Empennage (HT + VT) | 95 | 9.10 |
| Landing Gear (Main + Nose) | 140 | 4.05 |
| Propulsion (Engine + Mounts) | 210 | 2.20 |
| Fuel System (Tanks + Lines) | 60 | 4.50 |
| Hydraulic System | 30 | 3.10 |
| Electrical System | 45 | 2.80 |
| Avionics | 55 | 1.95 |
| Furnishings & Equipment | 75 | 4.60 |
| Flight Controls (cables, rods) | 35 | 3.40 |
| Air-conditioning & Anti-ice | 25 | 4.10 |

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

The empty weight is the simple sum of all component weights:

$$W_E = \sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i$$

The longitudinal CG location is the **first moment of weight about the reference datum**, divided by the total weight:

$$x_{cg} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (W_i \cdot x_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total weight by direct summation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_E &= 380 + 290 + 95 + 140 + 210 + 60 + 30 + 45 + 55 + 75 + 35 + 25 \\
W_E &= 1440 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the individual moments $M_i = W_i \cdot x_i$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{wing}      &= 380 \times 4.20 = 1596.0 \text{ kg·m} \\
M_{fuselage}  &= 290 \times 3.80 = 1102.0 \text{ kg·m} \\
M_{emp}       &= 95 \times 9.10  = 864.5  \text{ kg·m} \\
M_{LG}        &= 140 \times 4.05 = 567.0  \text{ kg·m} \\
M_{prop}      &= 210 \times 2.20 = 462.0  \text{ kg·m} \\
M_{fuel}      &= 60 \times 4.50  = 270.0  \text{ kg·m} \\
M_{hyd}       &= 30 \times 3.10  = 93.0   \text{ kg·m} \\
M_{elec}      &= 45 \times 2.80  = 126.0  \text{ kg·m} \\
M_{avionics}  &= 55 \times 1.95  = 107.25 \text{ kg·m} \\
M_{furn}      &= 75 \times 4.60  = 345.0  \text{ kg·m} \\
M_{fltctrl}   &= 35 \times 3.40  = 119.0  \text{ kg·m} \\
M_{ac/ai}     &= 25 \times 4.10  = 102.5  \text{ kg·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the moments.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum M_i &= 1596.0 + 1102.0 + 864.5 + 567.0 + 462.0 + 270.0 + 93.0 + 126.0 + 107.25 + 345.0 + 119.0 + 102.5 \\
\sum M_i &= 5754.25 \text{ kg·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the CG formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{cg} &= \frac{\sum M_i}{W_E} = \frac{5754.25}{1440} \\
x_{cg} &= 3.996 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify physical reasonableness.**

The CG is at **3.996 m** from the nose datum. Given a typical fuselage length of ~9 m and a wing location at 4.2 m, this CG sits very slightly forward of the wing's aerodynamic center (which is desirable for static longitudinal stability). The result is **physically reasonable** for a light transport.

**Step 7 — Final Answer (Boxed).**

$$\boxed{W_E = 1440 \text{ kg}, \qquad x_{cg} = 3.996 \text{ m from nose datum}}$$

### 3.1.3 Sensitivity Analysis (Examiner's Favourite Follow-up)

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners often ask: *"If 50 kg of avionics is relocated to a position 5.20 m aft, what is the new CG?"* The student should recognize this is a **delta-moment** problem:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta M_{avionics} &= 50 \times (5.20 - 1.95) = 162.5 \text{ kg·m} \\
W_E^{new} &= 1440 + 50 = 1490 \text{ kg} \quad \text{(or unchanged if only relocating, not adding)} \\
x_{cg}^{new} &= \frac{5754.25 + 162.5}{1490} = 3.9705 \text{ m} \quad (\text{if 50 kg added}) \\
x_{cg}^{new} &= \frac{5754.25 + 162.5}{1440} = 4.1102 \text{ m} \quad (\text{if only relocated, no change in total weight})
\end{aligned}
$$

The student is **explicitly penalized** for confusing "add weight" with "relocate weight."

## 3.2 Derivation: Margin of Safety (MS) on a Critical Structural Element

**Problem:** A wing lower spar cap is loaded in axial tension. The applied axial stress is σ_applied = 38,000 psi. The cap is made of **2024-T3** aluminum, with yield strength S_ty = 47,000 psi and ultimate strength S_tu = 64,000 psi. The FAR 23 allowable factor of safety on yield is 1.15 and on ultimate is 1.50. Compute the margins of safety on yield and on ultimate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
MS_{yield} &= \frac{S_{ty} / 1.15}{\sigma_{applied}} - 1 = \frac{47000 / 1.15}{38000} - 1 = \frac{40869.6}{38000} - 1 = 1.0755 - 1 \\
MS_{yield} &= +0.0755 \quad (\text{safe, just above the minimum}) \\
MS_{ult}   &= \frac{S_{tu} / 1.50}{\sigma_{applied}} - 1 = \frac{64000 / 1.50}{38000} - 1 = \frac{42666.7}{38000} - 1 = 1.1228 - 1 \\
MS_{ult}   &= +0.1228 \quad (\text{safe})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{MS_{yield} = 0.076, \qquad MS_{ult} = 0.123}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **A negative MS = structural failure during certification.** Both MS must be ≥ 0 (i.e., the structure is at least as strong as the regulator demands). The KTU examiner will deduct full marks if the student forgets the "-1" in the formula.

## 3.3 Derivation: Stall Speed from Wing Loading and $C_{L_{max}}$

Given: W = 1440 kg, S = 18.0 m², C_L_max = 1.6, ρ at sea level = 1.225 kg/m³.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{stall} &= \sqrt{\frac{2 W}{\rho S C_{L_{max}}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1440}{1.225 \times 18.0 \times 1.6}} \\
V_{stall} &= \sqrt{\frac{2880}{35.28}} = \sqrt{81.63} \\
V_{stall} &= 9.035 \text{ m/s} \approx 32.5 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the typical stall speed of a light aircraft at high-lift configuration.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Detailed Design Workflow — Mermaid Block Diagram

The following diagram shows the inter-relationships between the major activities of Detailed Design. It is adapted as a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** (per the V10 safeguard rules — alphanumeric node IDs only, no formatting inside labels).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Frozen Configuration from Preliminary Design] --> A2[Refine Geometry: Lofting and Section Slicing]
    A2 --> A3[Define Internal Structural Layout: Spars Ribs Stringers Frames]
    A3 --> A4[Perform Component Weight Build Up]
    A4 --> A5[Compute CG Location by Moment Summation]
    A5 --> A6[Conduct Stress Analysis on Critical Elements]
    A6 --> A7{Check Margins of Safety}
    A7 -- MS Less Than 0 --> A8[Redesign Component: Increase Thickness or Change Material]
    A8 --> A6
    A7 -- MS Greater or Equal 0 --> A9[Generate Detail Drawings: Orthographic and Isometric]
    A9 --> A10[Define Assembly and Installation Drawings]
    A10 --> A11[Specify Materials and Fasteners per Aerospace Standards]
    A11 --> A12[Update Weight and Balance Statement]
    A12 --> A13[Compile Certification Documentation: FAR 23 or FAR 25]
    A13 --> A14[End: Detailed Design Package Released to Manufacturing]
```

## 4.2 Component Weight-Build-Up Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Total Empty Weight WE] --> B2[Wing Group]
    B1 --> B3[Fuselage Group]
    B1 --> B4[Empennage Group]
    B1 --> B5[Landing Gear Group]
    B1 --> B6[Propulsion Group]
    B1 --> B7[Systems Group]
    B1 --> B8[Furnishings Group]

    B2 --> B2a[Spars]
    B2 --> B2b[Ribs]
    B2 --> B2c[Skin Panels]
    B2 --> B2d[Ailerons]
    B2 --> B2e[Flaps and Track]

    B3 --> B3a[Frames and Bulkheads]
    B3 --> B3b[Longerons]
    B3 --> B3c[Skin Panels]
    B3 --> B3d[Floors and Doors]

    B4 --> B4a[Horizontal Stabilizer]
    B4 --> B4b[Vertical Stabilizer]
    B4 --> B4c[Elevator and Rudder]

    B5 --> B5a[Main Gear]
    B5 --> B5b[Nose Gear]
    B5 --> B5c[Brakes and Hubs]

    B6 --> B6a[Engine]
    B6 --> B6b[Engine Mounts]
    B6 --> B6c[Cowling and Nacelles]

    B7 --> B7a[Fuel System]
    B7 --> B7b[Hydraulic System]
    B7 --> B7c[Electrical System]
    B7 --> B7d[Avionics]
    B7 --> B7e[Air Conditioning and Anti Ice]
    B7 --> B7f[Flight Controls]

    B8 --> B8a[Seats and Belts]
    B8 --> B8b[Windows and Windshield]
    B8 --> B8c[Insulation and Trim]
```

## 4.3 Wing Structural Cross-Section — Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C1[Wing Cross Section: Outboard Station] --> C2[Skin: Upper and Lower]
    C1 --> C3[Spar Caps: Top and Bottom]
    C1 --> C4[Spar Web]
    C1 --> C5[Leading Edge Skin]
    C1 --> C6[Trailing Edge Skin]
    C1 --> C7[Stringers: Upper and Lower]
    C1 --> C8[Ribs: Main and Auxiliary]
    C1 --> C9[Control Surface Hinges]
    C1 --> C10[Internal Fuel Tank Cavity]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** When the examiner asks *"Sketch and label the internal structural layout of a typical semi-monocoque wing"*, the student is expected to identify **at minimum**: upper skin, lower skin, front spar, rear spar, main rib, auxiliary rib, stringers, and a control-surface cut-out. Drawing a "lollipop" (a circle representing a wing section) with no labels loses 60% of the marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 Scheme — ESE):**
> - **Part A:** 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks (answer any 2 out of 3).
> - **Part B:** 2 questions × 14 marks = 28 marks (answer any 2 out of 3, with internal choice between sub-parts in many modules).
> - **Total Module 1 contribution to ESE:** ~34 marks across the module.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**Define Detailed Design in the context of aircraft design. List any four major deliverables of this phase.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Detailed Design is the phase of aircraft design in which the configuration frozen during Preliminary Design is fully developed into manufacturing-ready documentation, including part-level drawings, structural sizing, material specifications, and certification evidence.

**Four major deliverables (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Detail Drawings** of every individual part (3-view + isometric + section views).
2. **Assembly and Installation Drawings** showing mating of parts and systems routing.
3. **Component-Level Weight and Balance Statement** with CG location.
4. **Structural Analysis and Margins of Safety** report for every primary load-bearing element.
5. *(Optional fourth)* Material and Process Specifications per aerospace standards (e.g., NAS, MS, MIL).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**State the formula for the longitudinal centre of gravity of an aircraft as the first moment of weight. Why is the CG location critical in Detailed Design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Formula (1.5 marks):**

$$x_{cg} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i \cdot x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i}$$

where $W_i$ is the weight of the i-th component and $x_i$ is its longitudinal location from the reference datum.

**Criticality (1.5 marks):** CG location must lie within the **allowable CG range** defined by the manufacturer (and approved by the regulator) to ensure:
- **Static longitudinal stability** (CG ahead of neutral point).
- **Controllability** (adequate control surface authority at all flight phases).
- **Loading compliance** (permitted take-off, landing, and in-flight weight configurations).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam Style]

**Q.A (a) [7 marks]** A light aircraft has the following component weights and longitudinal locations from the nose datum:

| Component | Weight (kg) | $x_i$ (m) |
|---|---|---|
| Wing | 250 | 3.50 |
| Fuselage | 180 | 2.80 |
| Empennage | 60 | 7.40 |
| Landing Gear | 95 | 3.10 |
| Engine + Mount | 130 | 1.20 |
| Avionics | 40 | 1.50 |
| Furnishings | 55 | 3.60 |
| Fuel System | 35 | 3.40 |
| Flight Controls | 25 | 2.60 |
| Electrical System | 30 | 2.20 |

Compute (i) the empty weight and (ii) the longitudinal CG of the empty aircraft from the nose datum.

**Q.A (b) [7 marks]** A wing spar cap is loaded in axial tension with a stress σ = 42,000 psi. The cap is made of **7075-T6** aluminum alloy with $S_{ty} = 73,000$ psi and $S_{tu} = 83,000$ psi. The applicable FAR factor of safety is **1.15 on yield and 1.50 on ultimate**. Compute the margins of safety on yield and ultimate. Comment on the result.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Solution

**Step 1: Sum the weights.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_E &= 250 + 180 + 60 + 95 + 130 + 40 + 55 + 35 + 25 + 30 \\
W_E &= 900 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute moments.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{wing}    &= 250 \times 3.50 = 875.0 \\
M_{fus}     &= 180 \times 2.80 = 504.0 \\
M_{emp}     &= 60 \times 7.40  = 444.0 \\
M_{LG}      &= 95 \times 3.10  = 294.5 \\
M_{eng}     &= 130 \times 1.20 = 156.0 \\
M_{avio}    &= 40 \times 1.50  = 60.0 \\
M_{furn}    &= 55 \times 3.60  = 198.0 \\
M_{fuel}    &= 35 \times 3.40  = 119.0 \\
M_{flt}     &= 25 \times 2.60  = 65.0 \\
M_{elec}    &= 30 \times 2.20  = 66.0 \\
\sum M_i    &= 2781.5 \text{ kg·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute CG.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{cg} &= \frac{2781.5}{900} = 3.0906 \text{ m} \approx 3.09 \text{ m from nose}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{W_E = 900 \text{ kg}, \qquad x_{cg} \approx 3.09 \text{ m}}$$

**Physical check:** This is a light aircraft; the CG at 3.09 m is just ahead of the wing location (3.50 m), which is **aerodynamically desirable** (forward CG → static stability).

---

#### Part (b) — Solution

**Yield Margin of Safety:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MS_{yield} &= \frac{S_{ty}/1.15}{\sigma_{applied}} - 1 = \frac{73000/1.15}{42000} - 1 \\
&= \frac{63478.26}{42000} - 1 = 1.5114 - 1 \\
MS_{yield} &= +0.511
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ultimate Margin of Safety:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MS_{ult} &= \frac{S_{tu}/1.50}{\sigma_{applied}} - 1 = \frac{83000/1.50}{42000} - 1 \\
&= \frac{55333.33}{42000} - 1 = 1.3175 - 1 \\
MS_{ult} &= +0.317
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{MS_{yield} = +0.511, \qquad MS_{ult} = +0.317}$$

**Comment (1 mark):** Both margins of safety are **positive**, meaning the spar cap is structurally adequate. However, the yield margin is the more critical (smaller of the two in this case tied with ultimate behaviour in some FAR 23 cases); an MS of 0.5 indicates moderate conservatism, and the structure is safe for certification.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B (a) [7 marks]** Explain the **five sequential sub-steps** of Detailed Design. For each sub-step, state **one key engineering deliverable**.

**Q.B (b) [7 marks]** A light aircraft has the following characteristics at sea level: W = 900 kg, S = 14 m², C_L_max = 1.45, ρ = 1.225 kg/m³. Compute the **stall speed**. If the aircraft is required to have a **stall speed ≤ 30 knots (15.43 m/s)** at sea level, determine the **minimum wing area** required (keeping C_L_max = 1.45). State whether the current wing is adequate.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Solution

The five sequential sub-steps and their deliverables (1.4 marks each):

1. **Refinement of Geometry (Lofting):** Deliverable — *Lofted coordinate table* (typically 21 stations from root to tip for wing).
2. **Structural Layout Definition:** Deliverable — *Internal layout drawing* (spar locations, rib spacing, stringer pitch, skin panel boundaries).
3. **Component Weight Build-Up:** Deliverable — *Weight statement* (per-component weights and CG location).
4. **Stress Analysis & Margin of Safety Check:** Deliverable — *Structural analysis report* with MS values for every primary element.
5. **Detail & Assembly Drawing Production:** Deliverable — *Drawing package* (3-views + isometric + sectioned views + installation drawings).

---

#### Part (b) — Solution

**Step 1: Compute current stall speed.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{stall} &= \sqrt{\frac{2 W}{\rho S C_{L_{max}}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 900}{1.225 \times 14 \times 1.45}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{1800}{24.8675}} = \sqrt{72.385} \\
V_{stall} &= 8.508 \text{ m/s} = 16.54 \text{ knots}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute minimum wing area for 30-knot stall.**

Convert: 30 knots = 30 × 0.5144 = 15.43 m/s.

Rearrange the stall formula for S:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{min} &= \frac{2 W}{\rho V_{stall}^{2} C_{L_{max}}} = \frac{2 \times 900}{1.225 \times (15.43)^2 \times 1.45} \\
&= \frac{1800}{1.225 \times 238.08 \times 1.45} = \frac{1800}{422.91} \\
S_{min} &= 4.256 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compare.**

Current wing area S = 14 m² ≫ S_min = 4.256 m². The current wing is **far more than adequate** for the 30-knot stall requirement; in fact, it could be reduced, which would also reduce weight and drag, increasing range and endurance. (1 mark for engineering interpretation)

$$\boxed{V_{stall} = 8.51 \text{ m/s} \approx 16.5 \text{ knots}; \quad S_{min} = 4.26 \text{ m}^2; \text{ current } S = 14 \text{ m}^2 \text{ is more than adequate.}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Detailed Design Questions (PEMET865):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "-1" in Margin of Safety:** Writing $MS = \sigma_{allow}/\sigma_{applied}$ without subtracting 1 gives a "Strength Ratio" — *not* the margin. Examiners deduct **2 of 7 marks** for this.
>
> 2. **Confusing "weight added" with "weight relocated":** When asked "what if 50 kg is moved from one station to another?", students often mistakenly add 50 kg to the total. Total weight is unchanged in a pure relocation; only the moment changes.
>
> 3. **Using **SI** when the problem is in **FPS**, or vice versa:** A 30-knot stall speed, when inserted as 30 m/s into the formula, gives a wildly wrong answer. Always convert units **before** computation.
>
> 4. **Stating "MS = 0.0755" without a sign:** A margin of safety of 0.076 is **safe**; a margin of -0.076 is **failure**. The sign is essential. Examiners explicitly look for the **+** sign.
>
> 5. **Drawing a "lollipop" wing section with no labels:** Sketching a circle and writing "wing" inside is **not** a structural layout. The student must label upper skin, lower skin, front spar, rear spar, stringers, ribs, and control surface cut-outs. Otherwise, **at least 3 of 7 marks** are lost.
>
> 6. **Writing $C_{L_{max}}$ instead of $C_{L_{max}}$ with subscripts:** In handwritten exams, write "CL max" or "C sub L max" — never lose a mark for notational ambiguity in KTU valuations.
>
> 7. **Omitting the "Physical Reasoning" step:** After computing a CG or stall speed, a single sentence stating whether the result is *physically reasonable* is worth **0.5 to 1 mark** in KTU's "engineering judgment" assessment.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Top 20 Rapid-Revision Bullets for "Detailed Design" (PEMET865 Module 1):**

- **Detailed Design** is the **4th phase** of the aircraft design process (after Conceptual, Preliminary, and before Production).
- **Primary goal:** Convert the preliminary layout into **manufacturing-ready documentation**.
- **Five sub-steps:** (1) Refine geometry (lofting) → (2) Define structural layout → (3) Component weight build-up → (4) Stress analysis & MS check → (5) Detail & assembly drawings.
- **Empty weight equation:** $W_E = \sum W_i$.
