It unifies climb, cruise, and acceleration into a single scalar metric. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are riding a bicycle up a hill, but instead of just climbing, you are also speeding up. Your legs (thrust) are doing **two jobs simultaneously**: lifting you higher (potential energy gain) AND making you go faster (kinetic energy gain). The bicycle's brakes, friction, and wind resistance (drag) are constantly eating away at your effort. - **Specific Excess Power ($P_s$)** is like the "**net wattage**" your legs have left over after paying the friction bill. - A bicycle in **steep climb + slow speed** uses most of the energy for potential energy (height). - A bicycle on **flat ground + fast acceleration** uses most of the energy for kinetic energy (speed). - An aircraft in **accelerated climb** is doing BOTH at once — and $P_s$ tells you exactly how fast the total energy reservoir is filling up. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in Aircraft Design:** Combat aircraft (fighter jets) are almost never in "steady" flight. They are constantly converting excess thrust into combinations of altitude and speed. The Energy Method allows a designer to plot the entire **energy-maneuverability envelope** on a single chart and determine which aircraft "wins" a dogfight — the one with higher $P_s$ at a given altitude and speed. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Definitions | Term | Definition | Typical Value (Civilian) | Typical Value (Military) | |------|------------|--------------------------|---------------------------| | **Specific Excess Power ($P_s$)** | Energy gain rate per unit weight | 100–500 m/min | Up to **1500 m/min** (fighters) | | **Service Ceiling** | Altitude where max RC = **100 ft/min (0.508 m/s)** | ~12,500 m (B777) | ~15,000 m (F-15) | | **Absolute Ceiling** | Altitude where max RC = 0 | — | ~20,000 m (SR-71) | | **Combat Ceiling** | Altitude where $P_s$ = 0 in a **30° bank turn** | — | ~18,000 m (typical fighter) | | **Energy Height ($h_e$)** | Altitude equivalent of total specific energy | Variable | Variable | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Physical Constant:** Standard sea-level gravity **g = 9.80665 m/s²**, Standard sea-level density **$\rho_0$ = 1.225 kg/m³**, Speed of sound at sea level **a = 340.29 m/s**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Energy Method — Step-by-Step Logic The energy method is built upon Newton's Second Law applied along the flight path. Consider an aircraft climbing and accelerating simultaneously. Resolving forces along the flight path: **Step 1 — Force balance along flight path:** The component of thrust along the flight path exceeds drag, with the excess accelerating the aircraft and/or raising it against gravity. $$T - D - W \sin\gamma = \frac{W}{g} \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}$$ where $\gamma$ is the flight path angle. **Step 2 — Geometric relation for small $\gamma$:** For small climb angles, $\sin\gamma \approx \tan\gamma \approx \gamma \approx \dfrac{dh/dt}{V}$. **Step 3 — Substituting and rearranging:** $$T - D = W \left(\frac{1}{V} \cdot \frac{dh}{dt}\right) + \frac{W}{g} \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}$$ **Step 4 — Multiply by V/W (per unit weight per unit speed):** $$\frac{(T-D) V}{W} = \frac{dh}{dt} + \frac{V}{g} \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}$$ **Step 5 — Recognizing Specific Excess Power:** $$P_s = R/C + \frac{V}{g} \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}$$ **Step 6 — Defining Energy Height $h_e$:** $$h_e = h + \frac{V^2}{2g} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dh_e}{dt} = P_s$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this is beautiful:** $P_s$ (a single number) tells you how fast the aircraft's "energy height" is growing. If you spend all $P_s$ on altitude ($dV/dt = 0$), $P_s$ = climb rate. If you spend all $P_s$ on speed ($dh/dt = 0$), $P_s$ = $(V/g)(dV/dt)$. Real flight does a mix — exactly how the pilot or autopilot chooses. ## 2.2 Climbing Flight (Steady Climb, $dV/dt = 0$) $$P_s = R/C = \frac{(T-D) V}{W} = \frac{\text{Excess Power}}{W}$$ - **Service Ceiling:** $R/C_{max} = 100 \text{ ft/min} = 0.508 \text{ m/s}$ - **Absolute Ceiling:** $R/C_{max} = 0 \Rightarrow T = D$ - **Time to climb** from $h_1$ to $h_2$: $t_{climb} = \displaystyle\int_{h_1}^{h_2} \dfrac{dh}{R/C(h)}$ ## 2.3 Accelerated Level Flight ($dh/dt = 0$) $$P_s = \frac{V}{g} \cdot \frac{dV}{dt} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{g \cdot P_s}{V} = \frac{g(T-D)}{W}$$ Acceleration at constant altitude occurs when **Thrust > Drag** (clean configuration, afterburner ON, etc.). ## 2.4 Turning Flight — Coordinated Level Turn In a steady, coordinated level turn at bank angle $\phi$: - **Load factor:** $n = \dfrac{1}{\cos\phi}$ - **Turn rate:** $\dot{\psi} = \dfrac{g \tan\phi}{V} = \dfrac{g \sqrt{n^2 - 1}}{V}$ (rad/s) - **Turn radius:** $R = \dfrac{V^2}{g \tan\phi} = \dfrac{V^2}{g\sqrt{n^2 - 1}}$ (m) - **Drag in turn:** $D_{turn} = n \cdot D_{level}$ (lift equals $nW$) - **Specific Excess Power in turn:** $$P_{s,turn} = \frac{(T - nD_{level}) V}{W} = P_s - \frac{(n-1) D \cdot V}{W}$$ ## 2.5 Accelerated Climbing Turn (Most General Case) Combining altitude, speed, and turn effects: $$P_s = \frac{dh}{dt} + \frac{V}{g}\frac{dV}{dt} + \frac{n-1}{V}\int_0^V \text{(additional terms)}$$ A practical engineering approximation: $$T = D \cdot n + \frac{W}{g}\frac{dV}{dt} + W \sin\gamma$$ ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $e = gh + V^2/2$ | Specific total energy | m²/s² | | 2 | $h_e = h + V^2/(2g)$ | Energy height | m | | 3 | $P_s = (T-D)V/W$ | Specific excess power | m/s or W/N | | 4 | $P_s = R/C + (V/g)(dV/dt)$ | Energy rate decomposition | m/s | | 5 | $R/C_{max} = (T_{max}-D)V/W$ | Maximum rate of climb | m/s | | 6 | $n = 1/\cos\phi$ | Load factor in level turn | dimensionless | | 7 | $\dot{\psi} = g\tan\phi / V$ | Turn rate | rad/s | | 8 | $R_{turn} = V^2/(g\tan\phi)$ | Turn radius | m | | 9 | $D_{turn} = n \cdot D_{level}$ | Drag in level turn | N | | 10 | $P_{s,turn} = (T-nD)V/W$ | $P_s$ in turn | m/s | | 11 | $P_{s,climb\ turn} = (T-D_n)V/W - \dot{\psi} \cdot R$ | $P_s$ in turning climb | m/s | | 12 | $V_{stall,turn} = V_{stall,level}\sqrt{n}$ | Stall speed in turn | m/s | | 13 | $T/W = D/W + (V/g)(dV/dt) + (R/C)$ | Thrust-to-weight decomposition | dimensionless | | 14 | $\eta_c = R/C / [(T-D)V/W]$ | Climb efficiency | dimensionless | | 15 | $h_{abs} = h_{serv} + \text{(correction)}$ | Absolute ceiling estimate | m | ## 2.7 Real-World Utility - **Civil Aviation:** $P_s$ curves are used to design **climb gradients** required by FAR Part 25 (one-engine-inoperative climb, balked landing climb, etc.). - **Military Aviation:** **Energy-Maneuverability (E-M) Theory** plots $P_s$ vs. Mach number for various altitudes; the curve's area gives instantaneous and sustained **turn performance**. - **UAV/Drone Design:** $P_s$ defines the **service ceiling** and **maximum speed** envelope. - **Spacecraft / Re-entry:** Analogous **specific energy** concept (vis-viva equation) governs orbital mechanics. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** The combination $T/W$ (thrust loading) and $W/S$ (wing loading) is THE design driver's pair. Higher $T/W$ → better climb + acceleration; higher $W/S$ → higher stall speed but better cruise. Designers always work in the $T/W$ vs. $W/S$ plane. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Specific Excess Power from Energy Conservation **Starting Premise:** An aircraft's kinetic + potential energy changes at a rate equal to net propulsive power minus drag power. **Step 1 — Total mechanical energy:** $$E = mgh + \frac{1}{2}mV^2$$ **Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. time:** $$\frac{dE}{dt} = mg\frac{dh}{dt} + mV\frac{dV}{dt}$$ **Step 3 — Power balance (First Law of Thermodynamics, mechanical):** The propulsive power $(T \cdot V)$ supplies both the energy storage rate and the drag dissipation: $$T \cdot V = D \cdot V + mg\frac{dh}{dt} + mV\frac{dV}{dt}$$ **Step 4 — Divide by weight $W = mg$:** $$\frac{(T-D)V}{W} = \frac{dh}{dt} + \frac{V}{g}\frac{dV}{dt}$$ **Step 5 — Identify the LHS as Specific Excess Power:** $$P_s \equiv \frac{(T-D)V}{W}$$ **Step 6 — Therefore:** $$\boxed{P_s = \underbrace{\frac{dh}{dt}}_{R/C} + \underbrace{\frac{V}{g}\frac{dV}{dt}}_{\text{acceleration contribution}}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** $P_s$ (m/s) has units of *velocity*, but represents *rate of energy-height change*. It is the universal currency of aircraft performance. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Height and Time-to-Climb **Step 1 — Integrate $P_s$ over altitude to obtain energy height:** $$h_e = h + \frac{V^2}{2g}$$ If we define $h_e$ as the altitude at which the aircraft would be if it converted all its kinetic energy to potential energy (V → 0), then: $$h_e = h + \frac{V^2}{2g} \Rightarrow \frac{dh_e}{dt} = \frac{dh}{dt} + \frac{V}{g}\frac{dV}{dt} = P_s$$ **Step 2 — Time to climb from $h_1$ to $h_2$ at constant velocity $V$:** For a steady climb at the **best climb speed $V_{R/C,max}$**: $$t_{climb} = \int_{h_1}^{h_2} \frac{dh}{R/C(h)}$$ **Step 3 — Analytic approximation using **Césare Law** (1910):** Assume $R/C$ decreases linearly with altitude: $R/C(h) = R/C_0 \cdot (1 - h/h_{abs})$ $$t_{climb}(h_1 \to h_2) = \frac{h_{abs}}{R/C_0} \ln\left(\frac{1 - h_1/h_{abs}}{1 - h_2/h_{abs}}\right)$$ This is the **closed-form time-to-climb** used in preliminary design. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Specific Excess Power in a Climbing Turn (Energy Maneuverability) **Step 1 — Force balance in a climbing, turning flight path:** In body-axis frame, the thrust minus drag must overcome gravity component, centripetal force, and tangential acceleration: $$T - D = W\sin\gamma + \frac{W}{g}\frac{dV}{dt} + \frac{W}{g}\frac{V^2}{R_{turn}}$$ **Step 2 — Express turn radius via bank angle and load factor:** $$R_{turn} = \frac{V^2}{g\sqrt{n^2-1}}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$T - D = W\sin\gamma + \frac{W}{g}\frac{dV}{dt} + W\sqrt{n^2-1}$$ **Step 4 — Multiply by $V/W$:** $$P_s = V\sin\gamma + \frac{V^2}{g}\frac{1}{V}\frac{dV}{dt} + V\sqrt{n^2-1}$$ Simplifying for level accelerated turn ($\gamma = 0$, $dV/dt \neq 0$): $$\boxed{P_{s,turn} = \frac{V}{g}\frac{dV}{dt} + V\sqrt{n^2-1}}$$ **Step 5 — Sustained turn rate (no acceleration, $dV/dt = 0$):** $$\dot{\psi} = \frac{g\sqrt{n^2-1}}{V}, \quad P_s = V\sqrt{n^2-1} = V \cdot \frac{\dot{\psi}V}{g} = \frac{V^2 \dot{\psi}}{g}$$ **Step 6 — Energy height rate in sustained turn:** $$\boxed{\frac{dh_e}{dt}\bigg|_{turn} = \frac{(T - nD)V}{W} = P_s - (n-1)\frac{DV}{W}}$$ This is the famous **Specific Excess Power in a turn** equation used in all E-M charts. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Maximum Sustained Turn Rate **Step 1 — For sustained turn, $P_{s,turn} = 0$ at the absolute maneuver ceiling:** Setting $P_{s,turn} = 0$ for maximum load factor $n_{max,sustained}$: $$(T - n_{max,sustained} \cdot D) V = 0 \Rightarrow n_{max,sustained} = \frac{T}{D}$$ **Step 2 — Maximum sustained turn rate:** $$\dot{\psi}_{max,sustained} = \frac{g\sqrt{n_{max}^2 - 1}}{V} = \frac{g}{V}\sqrt{\left(\frac{T}{D}\right)^2 - 1}$$ **Step 3 — Practical instantaneous turn rate (using $P_s > 0$):** $$\dot{\psi}_{inst} = \frac{P_s}{V} \text{ (rad/s, when sacrificing energy)}$$ --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5: Relationship Between Service and Absolute Ceiling **Step 1 — Assume linear thrust degradation with altitude:** For a turbojet/turbofan, $T(h) \approx T_0(1 - h/h_T)$ where $h_T$ is the thrust ceiling. **Step 2 — Drag at sea level at $V_{R/C,max}$:** From $L=W$ and $D = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_{D_0} + \frac{2kW^2}{\rho V^2 S}$: At minimum $D$ (which approximately coincides with $V_{R/C,max}$): $V = V_{md} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S}\sqrt{\frac{k}{C_{D_0}}}}$ **Step 3 — Service ceiling condition** $R/C = 0.508$ m/s = 100 ft/min: $$\frac{(T_{serv} - D_{serv}) V}{W} = 0.508$$ **Step 4 — Empirical rule:** $$h_{abs} \approx 1.2 \cdot h_{serv} \text{ (for jet aircraft)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering shortcut:** For propeller aircraft, $h_{abs} \approx 1.4 \cdot h_{serv}$ because prop efficiency drops faster than jet thrust with altitude. --- ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard) **Problem:** A jet aircraft weighs $W = 80,000$ N, has wing area $S = 30$ m², at sea level where $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $T = 40,000$ N, drag polar $C_D = 0.02 + 0.05 C_L^2$. **Find:** (i) Velocity for max $R/C$, (ii) Max $R/C$, (iii) $P_s$ at this velocity. **Solution:** **Step (i) — Velocity for max R/C:** For jet at low altitude, $\max(R/C)$ occurs near $V_{md}$: $$V_{md} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S}\sqrt{\frac{k}{C_{D_0}}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 80000}{1.225 \times 30}\sqrt{\frac{0.05}{0.02}}}$$ $$V_{md} = \sqrt{4353.4 \times 1.5811} = \sqrt{6884.4} \approx 82.97 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step (ii) — Compute Drag at $V_{md}$:** $C_L = \dfrac{W}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S} = \dfrac{80000}{0.5 \times 1.225 \times 6884.4 \times 30} = \dfrac{80000}{126,517} = 0.6323$ $C_D = 0.02 + 0.05 \times (0.6323)^2 = 0.02 + 0.05 \times 0.3998 = 0.02 + 0.01999 = 0.03999$ Drag $D = C_D \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S = 0.04 \times 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 6884.4 \times 30 = 2529.5$ N **Step (iii) — Max R/C and $P_s$:** $$R/C_{max} = \frac{(T-D)V}{W} = \frac{(40000 - 2529.5) \times 82.97}{80000} = \frac{37470.5 \times 82.97}{80000}$$ $$R/C_{max} = \frac{3,108,977}{80000} \approx 38.86 \text{ m/s} = 2331 \text{ m/min}$$ $$\boxed{P_s = 38.86 \text{ m/s} \approx 2331 \text{ m/min} \approx 7647 \text{ ft/min}}$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Energy-Maneuverability (E-M) Chart — Functional Block Architecture The E-M chart is the **central performance diagram** for accelerated flight. It plots Specific Excess Power ($P_s$) as a function of Mach number (or velocity) for various altitudes. The diagram below maps the **data flow** and **computational dependencies** used to generate this chart in aircraft design software. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Module:
Aircraft Geometry and Atmosphere] --> B[Compute Weight and Wing Loading
W over S at current fuel state] A --> C[Compute Thrust Available T
from Engine Deck at h and M] A --> D[Compute Drag Polar
CD equals CD0 plus K times CL squared] B --> E[Compute Lift Coefficient
CL equals 2W over rho V squared S] E --> D C --> F[Compute Excess Thrust
T minus D] D --> F F --> G[Compute Specific Excess Power
Ps equals T minus D times V over W] G --> H[E-M Chart Layer 1:
Ps versus M at fixed altitude] G --> I[E-M Chart Layer 2:
Ps versus h at fixed Mach] H --> J[Overlay Sustained Turn Rate
psi dot equals g times Ps over V squared] I --> J J --> K[Energy Maneuverability Envelope
Operational Boundary for Pilot] K --> L[Output:
Combat Ceiling, Sustained Turn Number,
Instantaneous Turn Rate] ``` ## 4.2 Specific Excess Power Decomposition — Functional Flow The decomposition of $P_s$ into its climb and acceleration components is the heart of accelerated flight analysis. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input[Thrust and Drag Module] T[Thrust T of V, h] DR[Drag D of V, h] end subgraph Compute[Energy Equation Solver] EX[T minus D equals Excess Thrust] SP[Excess Thrust times V over W equals Ps] end subgraph Output[Output Modes] MODE1[Mode 1: Steady Climb
Ps equals Rate of Climb] MODE2[Mode 2: Level Acceleration
Ps equals V over g times dV by dt] MODE3[Mode 3: Climbing Turn
Ps equals Climb plus Turn plus Accel] MODE4[Mode 4: Energy Storage
Ps equals dhe by dt] end T --> EX DR --> EX EX --> SP SP --> MODE1 SP --> MODE2 SP --> MODE3 SP --> MODE4 ``` ## 4.3 Flight Regime Map — Acceleration, Climb, Turn Regions A pilot (or autopilot) selects flight regimes by trading off altitude gain, speed gain, and turn rate. The diagram below shows the **decision flow** during accelerated flight. ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Pilot Command Given:
Target Altitude, Speed, Heading] --> Q1{Energy Available?
Check Ps at current state} Q1 -- Ps less than 0 --> DECEL[Decelerate or Descend:
Aircraft Cannot Maintain State] Q1 -- Ps equals 0 --> STEADY[Steady Cruise or Co-ordinated Turn
at Constant Energy] Q1 -- Ps greater than 0 --> Q2{Pilot Intent?} Q2 -- Convert to Altitude --> CLIMB[Climb Mode:
dh by dt equals Ps] Q2 -- Convert to Speed --> ACCEL[Acceleration Mode:
dV by dt equals g Ps over V] Q2 -- Convert to Turn --> TURN[Turn Mode:
psi dot equals g Ps over V squared] Q2 -- Mixed Optimization --> OPTIM[Optimal Energy Distribution
via Optimal Control Theory
Hamiltonian Maximization] CLIMB --> ENDO[End of Maneuver] ACCEL --> ENDO TURN --> ENDO OPTIM --> ENDO STEADY --> ENDO DECEL --> ENDO ``` ## 4.4 Climb and Ceiling Relationships — Architecture Block This block diagram shows the **relationship between climb performance, ceiling definitions, and thrust/weight loading** in preliminary design. ```mermaid flowchart TD INPUTS[Aircraft Parameters:
W, S, T, CD0, K, Altitude h] --> TW[Compute T over W
Thrust Loading] INPUTS --> WS[Compute W over S
Wing Loading] TW --> RC[Max Rate of Climb at h:
RC equals T minus D times V over W] WS --> RC RC --> SERV{Select Ceiling Definition} SERV -- RC equals 100 ft per min --> H_SERV[Service Ceiling h_serv] SERV -- RC equals 0 --> H_ABS[Absolute Ceiling h_abs] SERV -- RC equals 0 in 30 degree bank turn --> H_COMB[Combat Ceiling h_comb] H_SERV --> VERIFY[Validate against FAR 25 or MIL Spec] H_ABS --> VERIFY H_COMB --> VERIFY VERIFY --> OUTPUT[Performance Report:
h_serv, h_abs, h_comb, Time to Climb] ``` ## 4.5 Turn Performance Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD V_IN[True Airspeed V] --> N[Load Factor n from Bank phi:
n equals 1 over cos phi] N --> R_T[Turn Radius:
R equals V squared over g tan phi] N --> PSI[Turn Rate:
psi dot equals g tan phi over V] R_T --> OUT1[Output: R_T in meters] PSI --> OUT2[Output: psi dot in degrees per second] N --> D_T[Drag in Turn:
D_turn equals n times D_level] D_T --> PS_T[Specific Excess Power in Turn:
Ps_turn equals T minus nD times V over W] PS_T --> OUT3[Output: Sustained Turn Capability] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading these diagrams:** In KTU theory exams, examiners expect students to be able to draw these block diagrams showing how $P_s$ decomposes and how ceilings relate to thrust loading. Practice redrawing them from memory. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** **Define Specific Excess Power. Explain its physical significance in accelerated flight analysis.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Specific Excess Power ($P_s$) is defined as the rate of change of specific energy of an aircraft with respect to time. Mathematically: $$P_s = \frac{(T - D) V}{W}$$ where $T$ = thrust, $D$ = drag, $V$ = true airspeed, $W$ = weight. Units: m/s (also written as W/N or ft/min per unit weight). **Physical Significance (1.5 marks):** $P_s$ represents the **rate at which the aircraft's total mechanical energy (kinetic + potential) is increasing** per unit weight. In accelerated flight, where both altitude and speed change simultaneously: $$P_s = \frac{dh}{dt} + \frac{V}{g}\frac{dV}{dt}$$ A positive $P_s$ means the aircraft is gaining energy (climbing, accelerating, or both); $P_s = 0$ defines the **ceiling**; negative $P_s$ means loss of energy. It is the **single scalar metric** that unifies climb, acceleration, and maneuvering performance — making it the fundamental currency of accelerated flight analysis. --- ### Question A2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **What is the difference between Service Ceiling and Absolute Ceiling? How are they related empirically?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Service Ceiling (1.5 marks):** The altitude at which the maximum rate of climb (R/C) reduces to **100 ft/min (0.508 m/s)** for a jet aircraft. At this altitude, the aircraft can still climb but very slowly — typically considered the operational limit for sustained flight. **Absolute Ceiling (1.5 marks):** The altitude at which the maximum rate of climb reduces to **zero**. At this altitude, the aircraft can no longer climb in steady flight — $T = D$. It is a theoretical limit; actual operations stay well below it. **Empirical Relationship:** For jet aircraft, **$h_{abs} \approx 1.2 \times h_{serv}$**; for propeller aircraft, **$h_{abs} \approx 1.4 \times h_{serv}$** due to faster prop-efficiency degradation with altitude. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question B-A (Choice A): **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **A jet transport aircraft has the following specifications:** - Weight: $W = 80,000$ N - Wing area: $S = 30$ m² - Thrust at sea level: $T_0 = 36,000$ N - Drag polar: $C_D = 0.018 + 0.05 C_L^2$ - Sea-level density: $\rho_0 = 1.225$ kg/m³ **(a)** Derive the equation for Specific Excess Power and compute the **maximum rate of climb at sea level**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** If the aircraft executes a coordinated level turn at bank angle $\phi = 30°$, compute the **load factor**, **turn radius**, **turn rate**, and the **specific excess power in the turn** at the velocity of maximum climb rate. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Derivation of $P_s$ and Max R/C:** **Step 1 — Derive $P_s$:** From energy conservation, the rate of change of specific energy equals specific excess power: $$P_s = \frac{(T-D)V}{W} = \frac{dh}{dt} + \frac{V}{g}\frac{dV}{dt}$$ For steady climb ($dV/dt = 0$): $P_s = R/C = \dfrac{(T-D)V}{W}$ **Step 2 — Velocity for max R/C:** Occurs approximately at velocity for minimum drag (jet, low altitude): $$V_{md} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S}\sqrt{\frac{k}{C_{D_0}}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 80000}{1.225 \times 30}\sqrt{\frac{0.05}{0.018}}}$$ $$V_{md} = \sqrt{4353.4 \times 1.667} = \sqrt{7257.2} \approx 85.19 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $C_L$ at $V_{md}$:** $$C_L = \frac{2W}{\rho V^2 S} = \frac{2 \times 80000}{1.225 \times 7257.2 \times 30} = \frac{160000}{266,677} = 0.6000$$ **Step 4 — Compute $C_D$ and Drag:** $$C_D = 0.018 + 0.05 \times (0.6)^2 = 0.018 + 0.05 \times 0.36 = 0.018 + 0.018 = 0.036$$ $$D = C_D \cdot q \cdot S = 0.036 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 7257.2 \times 30 = 0.036 \times 133,338 = 4800.2 \text{ N}$$ **Step 5 — Max R/C and $P_s$:** $$R/C_{max} = \frac{(36000 - 4800.2) \times 85.19}{80000} = \frac{31,199.8 \times 85.19}{80,000}$$ $$\boxed{R/C_{max} = \frac{2,657,663}{80,000} \approx 33.22 \text{ m/s} = 1993 \text{ m/min} \approx 6540 \text{ ft/min}}$$ $$P_s = 33.22 \text{ m/s}$$ --- **Part (b) — Turning Flight at $\phi = 30°$:** **Step 1 — Load factor:** $$n = \frac{1}{\cos 30°} = \frac{1}{0.866} = 1.1547$$ **Step 2 — Turn radius at $V = 85.19$ m/s:** $$R = \frac{V^2}{g \tan\phi} = \frac{(85.19)^2}{9.81 \times \tan 30°} = \frac{7257.2}{9.81 \times 0.5774} = \frac{7257.2}{5.664}$$ $$R = 1281.2 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Turn rate:** $$\dot{\psi} = \frac{g \tan\phi}{V} = \frac{5.664}{85.19} = 0.0665 \text{ rad/s} = 3.81°/\text{s}$$ **Step 4 — Drag in turn:** $D_{turn} = n \cdot D_{level} = 1.1547 \times 4800.2 = 5542.4$ N **Step 5 — Specific Excess Power in turn:** $$P_{s,turn} = \frac{(T - D_{turn}) V}{W} = \frac{(36000 - 5542.4) \times 85.19}{80000}$$ $$P_{s,turn} = \frac{30,457.6 \times 85.19}{80,000} = \frac{2,594,690}{80,000}$$ $$\boxed{P_{s,turn} \approx 32.43 \text{ m/s} = 1946 \text{ m/min}}$$ **Comparison:** $P_s$ decreases from 33.22 m/s to 32.43 m/s in the turn — a **2.4% loss** due to increased lift-dependent drag. This is why combat pilots bank the aircraft *only as much as needed*. --- ### Question B-B (Choice B): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **An aircraft has the following performance data at sea level:** - Weight: $W = 60,000$ N - Wing area: $S = 25$ m² - Max thrust: $T = 30,000$ N - Drag polar: $C_D = 0.022 + 0.045 C_L^2$ - Sea-level density: $\rho_0 = 1.225$ kg/m³ **(a)** Derive the equation for Energy Height. Show that in **accelerated climbing flight**, the rate of change of energy height equals Specific Excess Power. **(7 marks)** **(b)** If the pilot performs an **accelerated climb at constant climb gradient $\gamma = 5°$** such that $R/C = 15$ m/s, determine the **acceleration** and **time to climb from 1000 m to 5000 m**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Derivation of Energy Height:** **Step 1 — Define specific energy:** $$e = gh + \frac{V^2}{2}$$ **Step 2 — Energy height (divide by g):** $$h_e = \frac{e}{g} = h + \frac{V^2}{2g}$$ **Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. time:** $$\frac{dh_e}{dt} = \frac{dh}{dt} + \frac{V}{g}\frac{dV}{dt}$$ **Step 4 — From power balance (already derived in Section 3.1):** $$\frac{(T-D)V}{W} = \frac{dh}{dt} + \frac{V}{g}\frac{dV}{dt}$$ **Step 5 — Therefore:** $$\boxed{\frac{dh_e}{dt} = \frac{(T-D)V}{W} = P_s}$$ This shows that the rate of change of energy height equals the specific excess power — proving the **equivalence between energy-height growth and $P_s$**. --- **Part (b) — Accelerated Climb Calculations:** **Step 1 — Compute $P_s$ for the climb:** For a climb angle of $\gamma = 5°$: $$R/C = V \sin\gamma \Rightarrow V = \frac{R/C}{\sin\gamma} = \frac{15}{\sin 5°} = \frac{15}{0.08716} = 172.1 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $C_L$ at this $V$:** $$C_L = \frac{2W}{\rho V^2 S} = \frac{2 \times 60000}{1.225 \times (172.1)^2 \times 25} = \frac{120000}{906,946} = 0.1323$$ **Step 3 — Compute $C_D$ and Drag:** $$C_D = 0.022 + 0.045 \times (0.1323)^2 = 0.022 + 0.045 \times 0.0175 = 0.022 + 0.000788 = 0.02279$$ $$D = C_D \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S = 0.02279 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 29,619 \times 25$$ Wait — recalculate: $V^2 = (172.1)^2 = 29,618.4$; $\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 29,618.4 \times 25 = 453,473$ $$D = 0.02279 \times 453,473 = 10,335 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 — Specific Excess Power:** $$P_s = \frac{(T-D)V}{W} = \frac{(30000 - 10335) \times 172.1}{60000} = \frac{19,665 \times 172.1}{60,000} = \frac{3,384,346}{60,000} \approx 56.41 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 5 — Acceleration from $P_s = R/C + (V/g)(dV/dt)$:** $$56.41 = 15 + \frac{172.1}{9.81}\frac{dV}{dt}$$ $$\frac{172.1}{9.81}\frac{dV}{dt} = 41.41 \Rightarrow \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{41.41 \times 9.81}{172.1} = \frac{406.2}{172.1}$$ $$\boxed{\frac{dV}{dt} \approx 2.36 \text{ m/s}^2}$$ **Step 6 — Time to climb from 1000 m to 5000 m:** $$\Delta h = 4000 \text{ m}; \quad t_{climb} = \frac{\Delta h}{R/C} = \frac{4000}{15} \approx 266.7 \text{ s} = 4.44 \text{ min}$$ (In this simplified analysis assuming constant $R/C$; in reality, $R/C$ varies with altitude.) --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Valuation Key Patterns):** > > 1. **Forgetting the $V/g$ factor in $P_s$ decomposition** — The equation is $P_s = R/C + (V/g)(dV/dt)$, NOT $P_s = R/C + dV/dt$. Many students drop the $V/g$ factor. **(Lose 1-2 marks per occurrence)** > > 2. **Using $V_{stall}$ instead of $V_{md}$ for max R/C** — For jet aircraft at low altitude, max $R/C$ occurs at $V_{md}$ (velocity for minimum drag), NOT at $V_{stall}$. **(Lose 2 marks)** > > 3. **Forgetting to convert units** — $P_s$ in m/s vs ft/min vs W/N. Standard KTU expects **SI units (m/s)** unless specified otherwise. > > 4. **Incorrect drag polar expansion** — $C_D = C_{D_0} + K C_L^2$ — students often forget the factor of 2 in $K = 1/(\pi \cdot AR \cdot e)$. **(Lose 1 mark)** > > 5. **Turn radius vs turn rate confusion** — $R$ and $\dot{\psi}$ are reciprocally related through $V$. Always show the unit conversions. > > 6. **Load factor error in turns** — $n = 1/\cos\phi$ ONLY for **level** turn. For climbing turn, $n$ must be resolved properly. **(Lose 3 marks in Part B)** > > 7. **Confusion between absolute and service ceiling values** — In numerical problems, always state which ceiling you are computing and verify $R/C = 0.508$ m/s for service ceiling. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Accelerated Flight (Module 3)** ### **Core Concepts (Must Memorize):** - [x] **Specific Excess Power** $P_s = (T-D)V/W$ — universal performance metric - [x] **Energy Height** $h_e = h + V^2/(2g)$ — unified altitude + speed state - [x] **$P_s$ decomposition**: $P_s = R/C + (V/g)(dV/dt) + V\sqrt{n^2-1}$ (full form) - [x] **Load factor in level turn**: $n = 1/\cos\phi$ - [x] **Turn rate**: $\dot{\psi} = g\tan\phi/V$ (rad/s) - [x] **Turn radius**: $R = V^2/(g\tan\phi)$ - [x] **Stall speed in turn**: $V_{stall,turn} = V_{stall,level}\sqrt{n}$ - [x] **Service ceiling**: $R/C = 100$ ft/min = **0.508 m/s** - [x] **Absolute ceiling**: $R/C = 0 \Rightarrow T = D$ - [x] **Combat ceiling**: $P_s = 0$ in a **30° bank turn** - [x] **$h_{abs} \approx 1.2 \cdot h_{serv}$** for jets; $\approx 1.4 \cdot h_{serv}$ for props - [x] **Maximum $R/C$ velocity** for jets: $V_{md} = \sqrt{(2W/\rho S)\sqrt{k/C_{D_0}}}$ - [x] **Drag in turn**: $D_{turn} = n \cdot D_{level}$ - [x] **$P_s$ in turn**: $P_{s,turn} = (T - nD)V/W$ - [x] **Energy method unifies** climb, acceleration, and turn in ONE scalar ### **Physical Constants:** - [x] $g = 9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2$ - [x] $\rho_0 = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at sea level, ISA - [x] $a_0 = 340.29 \text{ m/s}$ at sea level, ISA ### **Design Drivers (T/W and W/S):** - [x] **High T/W** → Better climb, acceleration, ceiling - [x] **High W/S** → Higher stall speed, better cruise efficiency - [x] **E-M chart** plots $P_s$ vs. Mach, parameterized by altitude ### **Exam Strategy Tips:** - [x] Always derive $P_s$ from **first principles** (energy conservation) before using - [x] In numerical problems, compute $C_L$ first, then $C_D$, then $D$, then $P_s$ - [x] State the **flight regime** explicitly (steady climb, accelerated climb, level turn, etc.) - [x] Convert all units to **SI** unless stated otherwise - [x] In ESE answers, **draw the energy diagram** to earn full method marks --- > **End of Notes — PEMET865 Module 3: Special Performance Requirements — Accelerated Flight** > *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme regulations and NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework.*

topics.title: Propulsion System Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET865 - AIRCRAFT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Propulsion System Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Propulsion System Selection** is the iterative decision-making process in preliminary aircraft design wherein the design engineer systematically evaluates, compares, and selects the most suitable powerplant (reciprocating, turboprop, turbojet, turbofan, or turboshaft) that optimally satisfies the **mission requirements**, **performance constraints**, **economic factors**, and **regulatory airworthiness standards** of the proposed aircraft.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this selection is governed by a multi-objective trade study between **specific fuel consumption (SFC)**, **thrust-to-weight ratio (T/W)**, **altitude capability**, **range/endurance**, **acquisition cost**, **maintenance cost**, **noise signature**, and **emissions profile**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hauling Truck" Intuition

Imagine you need to transport a heavy load of bricks over a long distance:
- If the road is **flat and short**, a small motorcycle with high fuel efficiency (turbojet/turbofan) makes sense.
- If the road is **mountainous and long**, you need a heavy truck with massive torque but lower fuel efficiency per mile (turboprop).
- If you must remain **stationary mid-air for hours** (like a helicopter observing traffic), you need a hovering engine (turboshaft).
- For **slow but powerful lifting** (like cargo gliders), you might even use a piston engine with a large propeller.

The aircraft propulsion selection is identical: the **mission profile** dictates which "engine personality" best fits the job. A supersonic fighter needs a turbojet/ramjet (high speed, poor SFC), while a long-range airliner needs a high-bypass turbofan (excellent SFC at cruise).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Selection (Highlighted)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Propulsion Selection Metrics (must memorize for KTU ESE):**
> - **TSFC** — Thrust Specific Fuel Consumption $\left[ \frac{\text{lb}_f}{\text{lb}_f \cdot \text{hr}} \right]$ or $\left[ \frac{\text{N}}{\text{kN} \cdot \text{s}} \right]$
> - **PSFC** — Power Specific Fuel Consumption $\left[ \frac{\text{lb}_f}{\text{hp} \cdot \text{hr}} \right]$
> - **BPR** — Bypass Ratio (dimensionless)
> - **OPR** — Overall Pressure Ratio (dimensionless)
> - **T/W** — Static Thrust-to-Weight ratio
> - **W/W** — Engine Weight-to-Thrust ratio $\left[ \frac{\text{lb}_f}{\text{lb}_f} \right]$
> - **Flight Mach Number $M$** and **Altitude $h$**

## 1.4 Categories of Aircraft Propulsion Systems

| Class | Typical Application | Speed Regime |
|---|---|---|
| **Piston + Propeller** | General Aviation (Cessna 172) | $M \le 0.4$ |
| **Turboprop** | Regional transports (ATR 72) | $M \approx 0.4 - 0.7$ |
| **Turbojet** | Early jets, some military | $M \approx 0.8 - 1.6$ |
| **Low-Bypass Turbofan** | Fighters, business jets | $M \approx 0.8 - 1.0$ |
| **High-Bypass Turbofan** | Commercial airliners | $M \approx 0.78 - 0.85$ |
| **Turboshaft** | Helicopters | $M = 0$ (hover) |
| **Ramjet / Scramjet** | Supersonic / Hypersonic | $M > 2.5$ |
| **Rocket** | Space launch, missile | All regimes |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Propulsion Selection

### Pillar 1 — **Mission Performance Requirements**
- **Maximum Mach number $M_{max}$**
- **Service ceiling $h_{ceiling}$**
- **Range $R$** and **Endurance $E$**
- **Take-off distance $S_{TO}$** and **Landing distance $S_L$**
- **Rate of climb $RC$**

### Pillar 2 — **Propulsive Efficiency $\eta_p$**
The fraction of propulsive power that becomes useful thrust work. For any engine:

$$\eta_p = \frac{T \cdot V_0}{\text{Energy added per second to flow}}$$

For a **propeller** (acting like a disc actuator):

$$\eta_p = \frac{2}{1 + \frac{V_\infty}{V_j}}$$

For a **jet** (momentum theory):

$$\eta_p = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{V_\infty}{V_j}}{1 + \left(\frac{V_\infty}{V_j}\right)^2}$$

where $V_j$ is the jet exhaust velocity and $V_\infty$ is the freestream velocity.

### Pillar 3 — **Thermal / Propulsive Efficiency Combined**
Overall efficiency of the engine:

$$\eta_0 = \eta_t \cdot \eta_p$$

where $\eta_t$ is the **thermal efficiency** of the thermodynamic cycle (Brayton cycle for jets).

### Pillar 4 — **Installation & Cost Considerations**
- Engine **dry weight $W_e$**
- **Acquisition cost** (USD per unit)
- **Direct Operating Cost (DOC)** including maintenance hours per flight hour
- **Maintenance cost per hour (CPH)**
- **Noise certification** (ICAO Stage 4/5)
- **Emission compliance** (CAEP/6)

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU board-exam essentials. Memorize the units and sign conventions.**

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_{jet} = \dfrac{V_\infty}{c_T} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W_1}{W_2}\right)$ | Breguet Range for jet | nmi, ft/s |
| 2 | $R_{prop} = \dfrac{\eta_p}{c_P} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W_1}{W_2}\right)$ | Breguet Range for propeller | nmi |
| 3 | $E_{jet} = \dfrac{1}{c_T} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W_1}{W_2}\right)$ | Endurance for jet | s |
| 4 | $E_{prop} = \dfrac{\eta_p}{c_P} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W_1}{W_2}\right)$ | Endurance for propeller | s |
| 5 | $T = \dot{m} (V_j - V_\infty)$ | Thrust equation (Newton's 2nd) | N |
| 6 | $F_{net} = \dot{m}(V_j - V_\infty) + (p_e - p_\infty)A_e$ | Generalized thrust | N |
| 7 | $c_T = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{T}$ | Thrust SFC | $1/\text{s}$ or $1/\text{hr}$ |
| 8 | $c_P = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_{shaft}}$ | Power SFC | $\text{lb}_f/\text{hp}\cdot\text{hr}$ |
| 9 | $\eta_p^{jet} = \dfrac{2 V_\infty / V_j}{1 + (V_\infty / V_j)^2}$ | Jet propulsive efficiency | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\eta_p^{prop} = \dfrac{2}{1 + V_\infty / V_j}$ | Prop propulsive efficiency | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\eta_t = 1 - \dfrac{T_0}{T_4} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | Brayton thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 12 | $T_{sl} = W_e \cdot (T/W)_{design}$ | Engine static thrust | N or $\text{lb}_f$ |
| 13 | $C_{D0} + \dfrac{C_L^2}{\pi A R e}$ | Drag polar (parabolic) | dimensionless |
| 14 | $W_{fuel} = W_1 (1 - e^{-Rc_T/(V L/D)})$ | Fuel weight for jet range | $\text{lb}_f$ |

> **Where:**
> - $V_\infty$ = freestream velocity
> - $c_T$ = thrust specific fuel consumption
> - $c_P$ = power specific fuel consumption
> - $L/D$ = lift-to-drag ratio
> - $W_1, W_2$ = initial and final weights
> - $\dot{m}_f$ = fuel mass flow rate
> - $\dot{m}$ = air mass flow rate
> - $T_{sl}$ = sea-level static thrust
> - $W_e$ = engine weight
> - $A$ = disc area
> - $R$ = range
> - $C_{D0}$ = zero-lift drag coefficient
> - $AR$ = aspect ratio
> - $e$ = Oswald efficiency factor

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In airline industry practice (Boeing/Airbus design philosophy), the **propulsion system selection** drives **30-40% of the Direct Operating Cost (DOC)** of a commercial aircraft. The transition from turbofan BPR = 4 (JT9D era) to BPR = 10-12 (GEnx, GTF) was purely a **propulsion selection optimization** for fuel burn reduction — the airframes changed only modestly between Boeing 747 and 777 generations, but the engine choice revolutionized economics.

In military aviation, propulsion selection is dominated by **thrust-to-weight ratio** because of supersonic dash requirements and combat maneuverability. The F-22 Raptor uses **F119 turbofans with thrust vectoring** because no other engine class could deliver the required $T/W > 1.1$ with afterburner.

## 2.4 Selection Decision Logic (KTU Board Style)

The standard KTU approach is to **plot contours of constant range and endurance** in the **TSFC vs. Mach number space** and identify the engine class whose operating envelope contains the design point. This is the **Raymer/Anderson methodology**.

$$c_T = f(M, h) \quad \text{and} \quad T_{available} = f(M, h)$$

Both must satisfy:

$$T_{required} \le T_{available} \quad \text{at all mission segments}$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Breguet Range Equation for a Turbojet (Most Important for KTU)

The Breguet Range equation is the cornerstone of propulsion selection. We derive it here in exhaustive detail.

**Step 1 — Force balance on aircraft in steady cruise**

In unaccelerated level cruise, lift equals weight and thrust equals drag:

$$L = W \quad \text{and} \quad T = D$$

Therefore, the lift-to-drag ratio is:

$$\frac{L}{D} = \frac{W}{T}$$

**Step 2 — Express fuel flow in terms of thrust**

By definition of thrust specific fuel consumption:

$$c_T = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{m}_f = c_T \cdot T$$

**Step 3 — Relate fuel flow to weight change**

As the aircraft burns fuel, its weight decreases. The rate of weight change equals the negative of fuel mass flow:

$$\dot{m}_f = -\frac{dW}{dt} \quad \Rightarrow \quad -\frac{dW}{dt} = c_T \cdot T$$

Substituting $T = W \cdot \dfrac{D}{L} = W \cdot \dfrac{1}{L/D}$:

$$-\frac{dW}{dt} = c_T \cdot W \cdot \frac{1}{L/D}$$

Rearranging:

$$-\frac{dW}{W} = \frac{c_T}{(L/D)} \cdot dt$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over the cruise segment**

Integrate from initial weight $W_1$ at $t = 0$ to final weight $W_2$ at $t = t_{cruise}$:

$$\int_{W_1}^{W_2} \left(-\frac{dW}{W}\right) = \int_{0}^{t_{cruise}} \frac{c_T}{(L/D)} \, dt$$

Assuming $c_T$, $L/D$, and $V_\infty$ are approximately constant during cruise:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right) = \frac{c_T}{(L/D)} \cdot t_{cruise}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for cruise time and then range**

$$t_{cruise} = \frac{(L/D)}{c_T} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)$$

Range is the integral of velocity over time. For constant $V_\infty$:

$$R = V_\infty \cdot t_{cruise} = \frac{V_\infty \cdot (L/D)}{c_T} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$\boxed{\,R = \frac{V_\infty}{c_T} \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)\,}$$

This is the **Breguet Range Equation for a turbojet/turbofan**, the most critical formula in the entire propulsion selection module.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Breguet Range Equation for a Turboprop

For propeller-driven aircraft, the relevant fuel consumption parameter is **power specific fuel consumption $c_P$** measured in $\dfrac{\text{lb}_f}{\text{hp}\cdot\text{hr}}$.

**Step 1 — Thrust power available**

For a propeller, the useful power delivered to the air is:

$$P_{shaft} = T \cdot V_\infty / \eta_p$$

This is because propulsive efficiency $\eta_p$ accounts for the slipstream kinetic energy losses.

**Step 2 — Express fuel flow in terms of shaft power**

$$c_P = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{P_{shaft}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{m}_f = c_P \cdot P_{shaft}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $P_{shaft} = T \cdot V_\infty / \eta_p$**

$$\dot{m}_f = c_P \cdot \frac{T \cdot V_\infty}{\eta_p}$$

**Step 4 — Apply cruise force balance $T = D$**

$$\dot{m}_f = c_P \cdot \frac{D \cdot V_\infty}{\eta_p} = c_P \cdot \frac{W \cdot V_\infty}{\eta_p \cdot (L/D)}$$

**Step 5 — Equate to weight change**

$$-\frac{dW}{dt} = c_P \cdot \frac{V_\infty}{\eta_p \cdot (L/D)} \cdot W$$

Rearranging:

$$-\frac{dW}{W} = \frac{c_P \cdot V_\infty}{\eta_p \cdot (L/D)} \cdot dt$$

**Step 6 — Integrate**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right) = \frac{c_P \cdot V_\infty}{\eta_p \cdot (L/D)} \cdot t_{cruise}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for range**

$$\boxed{\,R_{prop} = \frac{\eta_p \cdot (L/D)}{c_P} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)\,}$$

Note the absence of $V_\infty$ in the final form — the propulsive efficiency already absorbs the velocity dependence.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thrust Equation from Momentum Theory

**Step 1 — Control volume around engine**

Apply conservation of mass and momentum to a control volume enclosing the engine.

**Step 2 — Mass conservation**

For steady flow, the mass entering equals the mass leaving. The fuel mass flow is much smaller than the air mass flow, so:

$$\dot{m}_{air} \approx \dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out}$$

**Step 3 — Momentum flux**

The momentum leaving the control volume minus momentum entering equals the thrust:

$$T = \dot{m} \cdot (V_j - V_\infty) + (p_e - p_\infty) \cdot A_e$$

**Step 4 — For fully expanded jet** ($p_e = p_\infty$)

The pressure term vanishes:

$$\boxed{\,T = \dot{m} \cdot (V_j - V_\infty)\,}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Propulsive Efficiency $\eta_p$ for a Jet

**Step 1 — Define propulsive efficiency**

Propulsive efficiency is the ratio of useful propulsive power output to the rate of kinetic energy addition to the flow:

$$\eta_p = \frac{T \cdot V_\infty}{\frac{1}{2} \dot{m} (V_j^2 - V_\infty^2)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $T = \dot{m}(V_j - V_\infty)$**

$$\eta_p = \frac{\dot{m}(V_j - V_\infty) \cdot V_\infty}{\frac{1}{2} \dot{m} (V_j^2 - V_\infty^2)} = \frac{2 V_\infty (V_j - V_\infty)}{V_j^2 - V_\infty^2}$$

**Step 3 — Factor the denominator**

$$V_j^2 - V_\infty^2 = (V_j - V_\infty)(V_j + V_\infty)$$

So:

$$\eta_p = \frac{2 V_\infty}{V_j + V_\infty}$$

**Step 4 — Final form**

$$\boxed{\,\eta_p^{jet} = \frac{2}{1 + V_j / V_\infty}\,}$$

Maximum propulsive efficiency is reached when $V_j = V_\infty$ (perfect momentum matching), giving $\eta_p = 1$, but this corresponds to zero thrust. The practical optimum is at $V_j / V_\infty \approx 1.1 - 1.3$.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Select Which Engine

| Engine Class | Cruise $M$ | $c_T$ at cruise | $\eta_p$ at cruise | Range Penalty vs. Ideal | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Turbojet | 0.85 | 0.7 - 0.9 | 0.40 | 50% lost to jet KE | Supersonic fighters |
| Low-Bypass Turbofan (BPR 0.5-1.5) | 0.80 | 0.6 - 0.8 | 0.55 | 35% lost | Fighters, bizjets |
| High-Bypass Turbofan (BPR 5-12) | 0.78 - 0.85 | 0.4 - 0.55 | 0.78 | 15% lost | Airliners |
| Turboprop | 0.4 - 0.65 | (as $c_P \approx 0.5$) | 0.85 | 10% lost | Regional/commuter |
| Piston Prop | 0.2 - 0.4 | (as $c_P \approx 0.4$) | 0.88 | 8% lost | GA aircraft |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the matrix:** For long-range missions, always select the engine with the **lowest $c_T$** (or $c_P$) at the design cruise point. The high-bypass turbofan dominates subsonic airliners because its $\eta_p$ is highest in the $M = 0.78$ range.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

> **Problem:** A turbofan-powered transport aircraft cruises at $V_\infty = 460$ ft/s at $h = 35000$ ft. The engine has $c_T = 0.65$ /hr. The $L/D = 18$. The initial cruise weight is $W_1 = 150000$ lb and final weight is $W_2 = 130000$ lb. Compute the cruise range.

**Solution using Breguet equation:**

$$R = \frac{V_\infty}{c_T} \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)$$

Step 1: Compute velocity in consistent units. $c_T$ is in /hr, so $V_\infty$ must be in ft/hr:

$$V_\infty = 460 \, \text{ft/s} \times 3600 = 1{,}656{,}000 \, \text{ft/hr}$$

Step 2: Compute the weight ratio logarithm:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{150000}{130000}\right) = \ln(1.1538) = 0.1431$$

Step 3: Substitute:

$$R = \frac{1{,}656{,}000}{0.65} \times 18 \times 0.1431$$

Step 4: Evaluate numerator step-by-step:

$$1{,}656{,}000 \times 18 = 29{,}808{,}000$$

Step 5: Divide by 0.65:

$$\frac{29{,}808{,}000}{0.65} = 45{,}858{,}462$$

Step 6: Multiply by 0.1431:

$$R = 45{,}858{,}462 \times 0.1431 = 6{,}562{,}358 \, \text{ft}$$

Step 7: Convert to nautical miles (1 nmi = 6076.12 ft):

$$R = \frac{6{,}562{,}358}{6076.12} = 1080 \, \text{nmi}$$

**Result:** $R \approx 1080$ nmi — a reasonable transcontinental range for a mid-size jet transport.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Propulsion System Selection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Mission Requirements] --> B{Mission Profile Type}
    B -->|Subsonic, Long Range| C[High-Bypass Turbofan]
    B -->|Supersonic Dash| D[Low-Bypass Turbofan or Turbojet]
    B -->|Short Takeoff and Landing| E[Turboprop]
    B -->|Hover and Vertical Lift| F[Turboshaft]
    B -->|Hypersonic M greater than 2.5| G[Ramjet or Scramjet]
    B -->|General Aviation| H[Piston Engine]
    C --> I[Compute Breguet Range]
    D --> I
    E --> J[Compute Breguet Range Prop]
    F --> K[Compute Hover Endurance]
    G --> L[Compute Specific Thrust]
    H --> M[Compute PSFC Range]
    I --> N{Does Range Meet Spec?}
    J --> N
    M --> N
    N -->|Yes| O[Verify T over W Ratio]
    N -->|No| P[Re-evaluate Aerodynamics]
    O --> Q{Does T over W meet Spec?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Check Cost and DOC]
    Q -->|No| S[Select Higher Thrust Engine]
    R --> T{Within Budget?}
    T -->|Yes| U[Final Engine Selection]
    T -->|No| V[Negotiate or Redesign]
    P --> B
    S --> B
    V --> B
    U --> W[End: Selected Engine]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Decision Layer Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Layer 1: Mission Analysis] --> L2[Layer 2: Aerodynamic Sizing]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Engine Cycle Selection]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: TSFC and SFC Evaluation]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Weight and Cost Iteration]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6: Final Locked Propulsion]
    L6 -.Feedback Loop.-> L2
    L6 -.Feedback Loop.-> L3
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Engine Performance Envelope Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ENVELOPE[Propulsion Selection Envelope]
        E1[Design Point: Altitude h and Mach M]
        E2[Required Thrust Curve Tr Required]
        E3[Available Thrust Curve Ta Available]
        E4[Specific Fuel Consumption Contour cT iso]
        E5[Propulsive Efficiency Contour eta p iso]
    end
    E1 --> E2
    E1 --> E3
    E2 --> CHECK{Is Tr less than or equal to Ta at all segments}
    E3 --> CHECK
    CHECK -->|Yes| OK[Engine Selection Valid]
    CHECK -->|No| FAIL[Re-select Engine Class]
    E4 --> OPTIMIZE{Find Minimum cT Operating Point}
    E5 --> OPTIMIZE
    OPTIMIZE --> OK
```

## 4.4 Functional Comparison Block — Engine Classes

| Engine Class | Breguet Range Equation | Speed Sweet Spot | Selection Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|
| Piston Propeller | $R = \dfrac{\eta_p}{c_P} \dfrac{L}{D} \ln(W_1/W_2)$ | $M \le 0.4$ | Cost-sensitive GA |
| Turboprop | $R = \dfrac{\eta_p}{c_P} \dfrac{L}{D} \ln(W_1/W_2)$ | $0.4 \le M \le 0.7$ | Short-haul commuter |
| Turbojet | $R = \dfrac{V_\infty}{c_T} \dfrac{L}{D} \ln(W_1/W_2)$ | $M > 0.9$ | Supersonic |
| Turbofan | $R = \dfrac{V_\infty}{c_T} \dfrac{L}{D} \ln(W_1/W_2)$ | $0.75 \le M \le 0.9$ | Long-range transport |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guidance:** All jet-class engines (turbojet and turbofan) use the same Breguet equation form with $c_T$. The difference is in the **value of $c_T$**, which is dramatically lower for high-bypass turbofans.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Breguet Range Equation for a jet-propelled aircraft. What does each term represent?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Breguet Range Equation for a jet-propelled aircraft is:

$$R = \frac{V_\infty}{c_T} \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)$$

**Each term represents:**
- $R$ = cruise range (nmi or km)
- $V_\infty$ = true airspeed of the aircraft during cruise (ft/s or m/s)
- $c_T$ = thrust-specific fuel consumption (1/hr or 1/s)
- $L/D$ = lift-to-drag ratio during cruise (dimensionless)
- $W_1$ = gross weight of aircraft at start of cruise (lb or kg)
- $W_2$ = weight of aircraft at end of cruise (lb or kg)

This equation is fundamental in propulsion selection because it quantifies how the choice of $c_T$ (an engine property) directly determines achievable range.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any four factors that govern the selection of a propulsion system during preliminary aircraft design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, 0.75 per point):**

1. **Mission Mach number and altitude envelope** — the engine must produce required thrust across the flight envelope.
2. **Specific fuel consumption (SFC)** — determines range and endurance capability of the aircraft.
3. **Thrust-to-weight ratio (T/W)** — must meet take-off, climb, and acceleration requirements.
4. **Acquisition and lifecycle cost** — capital cost, fuel cost, and maintenance cost per flight hour.
5. **Engine weight and dimensions** — affects center-of-gravity, wing mount loads, and installation drag.
6. **Noise and emissions compliance** — ICAO Stage 4/5 and CAEP/6 standards.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module question carries 14 marks, with internal choice (a) or (b). Sub-parts typically split as 7 + 7 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the Breguet Range Equation for a turbojet engine, clearly stating all assumptions used. (7 marks)

**(b)** A business jet cruises at $M = 0.78$ at $h = 41000$ ft. The aircraft has $L/D = 16$ at cruise. The chosen engine is a low-bypass turbofan with TSFC $c_T = 0.78$ /hr. Initial cruise weight is $W_1 = 32000$ lb, final cruise weight is $W_2 = 28000$ lb. Calculate the cruise range in nautical miles. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions:**
- Steady, level, unaccelerated flight
- Constant $V_\infty$, $c_T$, and $L/D$ during cruise
- Fully expanded exhaust ($p_e = p_\infty$)
- Negligible fuel addition effect on mass flow

**Derivation steps:**

Force equilibrium gives $L = W$ and $T = D$. Therefore $L/D = W/T$.

By definition $c_T = \dot{m}_f/T \Rightarrow T = \dot{m}_f / c_T$.

By mass conservation during fuel burn: $\dot{m}_f = -dW/dt$.

Therefore:

$$-\frac{dW}{dt} = c_T \cdot T = c_T \cdot W \cdot \frac{1}{L/D}$$

Rearranging:

$$-\frac{dW}{W} = \frac{c_T}{L/D} \cdot dt$$

Integrating from $W_1$ to $W_2$ over cruise time $t$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right) = \frac{c_T}{L/D} \cdot t$$

$$t = \frac{L/D}{c_T} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)$$

Range $R = V_\infty \cdot t$, so:

$$\boxed{\,R = \frac{V_\infty}{c_T} \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)\,}$$

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

Step 1: Compute true airspeed at $M = 0.78$, $h = 41000$ ft.

Speed of sound at 41000 ft: $a \approx 968$ ft/s (ISA value).
So $V_\infty = 0.78 \times 968 = 755$ ft/s.

Step 2: Convert to ft/hr: $V_\infty = 755 \times 3600 = 2{,}718{,}000$ ft/hr.

Step 3: Compute weight ratio: $\ln(32000/28000) = \ln(1.1429) = 0.1335$.

Step 4: Substitute:

$$R = \frac{2{,}718{,}000}{0.78} \times 16 \times 0.1335$$

Step 5: $2{,}718{,}000 / 0.78 = 3{,}484{,}615$ ft/hr.

Step 6: $3{,}484{,}615 \times 16 = 55{,}753{,}846$.

Step 7: $55{,}753{,}846 \times 0.1335 = 7{,}443{,}138$ ft.

Step 8: Convert to nautical miles: $7{,}443{,}138 / 6076.12 = 1225$ nmi.

**Answer: $R \approx 1225$ nautical miles.**

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 4]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast turboprop and turbofan propulsion systems in terms of specific fuel consumption, propulsive efficiency, and typical mission applications. (7 marks)

**(b)** A regional turboprop aircraft has the following cruise data: $L/D = 17$, $c_P = 0.45$ lb/(hp·hr), $\eta_p = 0.82$, $V_\infty = 280$ ft/s. Initial and final cruise weights are $W_1 = 36000$ lb and $W_2 = 33000$ lb respectively. Compute the range using the propeller Breguet equation. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Turboprop | Turbofan |
|---|---|---|
| **Specific fuel consumption metric** | $c_P$ in lb/(hp·hr) — typical 0.4 - 0.6 | $c_T$ in /hr — typical 0.4 - 0.9 |
| **Propulsive efficiency** | High (0.80 - 0.88) because propeller accelerates large mass slightly | Moderate (0.55 - 0.80) depending on BPR |
| **Cruise Mach range** | $M = 0.4 - 0.7$ | $M = 0.75 - 0.9$ |
| **Fuel efficiency at design point** | Excellent at low altitude and low speed | Excellent at high altitude and high subsonic |
| **Typical applications** | Regional commuters, military transports, bush planes | Commercial airliners, business jets, fighters |
| **Power output** | Drives a propeller via reduction gearbox | Direct thrust from jet exhaust |
| **Engine weight-to-power** | Lower weight for given power | Higher thrust-to-weight |
| **Noise signature** | Lower external, higher cabin | Higher external, lower cabin |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Turboprops are selected for short-haul, low-altitude, fuel-sensitive missions; turbofans are selected for high-subsonic, long-range missions.

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

For a propeller-driven aircraft, Breguet range equation is:

$$R = \frac{\eta_p}{c_P} \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)$$

**Note on units:** This equation gives range in miles (statute) when $c_P$ is in lb/(hp·hr) and $\eta_p$, $L/D$ are dimensionless. We use $V_\infty$ only to convert unit consistency — alternative form gives $R$ in nmi.

Let us use the form that yields range in statute miles directly:

$$R = \frac{325.7 \cdot \eta_p \cdot (L/D)}{c_P} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right) \quad \text{[nmi]}$$

(where 325.7 is a unit conversion constant for the $c_P$ version, derived from $550$ ft·lb/s per hp, $3600$ s/hr, $6076$ ft/nmi constants).

Step 1: Compute $\ln(W_1/W_2) = \ln(36000/33000) = \ln(1.0909) = 0.0870$.

Step 2: Substitute constants:

$$R = \frac{325.7 \times 0.82 \times 17}{0.45} \times 0.0870$$

Step 3: Numerator: $325.7 \times 0.82 = 267.07$; $267.07 \times 17 = 4539.2$.

Step 4: Divide by 0.45: $4539.2 / 0.45 = 10087.1$.

Step 5: Multiply by 0.0870: $10087.1 \times 0.0870 = 877.6$ nmi.

**Answer: $R \approx 877.6$ nautical miles.**

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Propulsion Selection Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch (most common — 3-4 marks lost):** Students forget to convert $V_\infty$ from ft/s to ft/hr when $c_T$ is in /hr. Always check unit consistency FIRST.
>
> 2. **Wrong Breguet form:** Using the **jet** equation for a **turboprop** (or vice versa) loses 7 marks outright. Identify engine class FIRST.
>
> 3. **Forgetting $V_\infty$ in jet equation but including it in propeller equation:** The two Breguet equations have different structures. Jet has $V_\infty / c_T$; propeller has $\eta_p / c_P$.
>
> 4. **Not showing assumptions in derivations:** Examiners allocate 2 marks for listing assumptions (steady flight, constant $L/D$, etc.). Skipping them loses easy marks.
>
> 5. **Range unit confusion:** Final answer must be in nmi or km. Don't leave it in ft.
>
> 6. **Confusing TSFC and PSFC:** $c_T$ has units of $1/\text{time}$; $c_P$ has units of $\text{mass}/(\text{power} \cdot \text{time})$. They are NOT interchangeable.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Propulsion System Selection (Module 4)**
>
> - [x] **Propulsion selection** is driven by mission profile, performance, cost, and certification requirements.
> - [x] Engine classes: Piston-Prop, Turboprop, Turbojet, Turbofan, Turboshaft, Ramjet, Scramjet, Rocket.
> - [x] **TSFC $c_T$** is used for jet-class engines; **PSFC $c_P$** is used for propeller-driven engines.
> - [x] **Breguet Range (jet):** $R = \dfrac{V_\infty}{c_T} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln(W_1/W_2)$
> - [x] **Breguet Range (propeller):** $R = \dfrac{\eta_p}{c_P} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln(W_1/W_2)$
> - [x] **Breguet Endurance (jet):** $E = \dfrac{1}{c_T} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln(W_1/W_2)$
> - [x] **Breguet Endurance (propeller):** $E = \dfrac{\eta_p}{c_P} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln(W_1/W_2)$
> - [x] **Thrust equation:** $T = \dot{m}(V_j - V_\infty)$
> - [x] **Jet propulsive efficiency:** $\eta_p = \dfrac{2 V_\infty}{V_j + V_\infty}$
> - [x] **Propeller propulsive efficiency:** $\eta_p = \dfrac{2}{1 + V_j / V_\infty}$
> - [x] **Thermal efficiency (Brayton):** $\eta_t = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$
> - [x] **Overall efficiency:** $\eta_0 = \eta_t \cdot \eta_p$
> - [x] **Bypass ratio BPR** is the dividing criterion between low-bypass (military) and high-bypass (civilian) turbofans.
> - [x] **High-bypass turbofans** dominate airliner propulsion because of high $\eta_p$ at $M \approx 0.78$.
> - [x] Always check **unit consistency** before plugging numbers into Breguet equation.
> - [x] Always state **assumptions** before any derivation question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flight Regimes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET865 - AIRCRAFT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flight Regimes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FLIGHT REGIMES (DEFINITION):**
> *Flight regimes* refer to the systematic classification of aircraft flight conditions based on the **freestream Mach number (M∞)**, the dimensionless ratio of the aircraft's true airspeed (V) to the local speed of sound (a). Each regime exhibits fundamentally distinct compressibility behaviour, shock wave phenomena, thermodynamic heating, and aerodynamic design constraints. The KTU 2024 syllabus categorises these into **Subsonic, Transonic, Supersonic, Hypersonic, and (briefly) Free Molecular Flow** regimes.

### 1.2 The Governing Dimensionless Parameter: Mach Number

The **Mach Number (M)** is the cardinal parameter governing compressible flow physics. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$
M = \frac{V_{\infty}}{a_{\infty}}
$$

where $V_{\infty}$ is the **freestream velocity** (m/s) and $a_{\infty}$ is the **local speed of sound** (m/s), given by:

$$
a_{\infty} = \sqrt{\gamma \, R \, T_{\infty}}
$$

Here, $\gamma$ is the **ratio of specific heats** ($\gamma = 1.4$ for air), $R$ is the **specific gas constant** ($R = 287$ J/kg·K for air), and $T_{\infty}$ is the **freestream static temperature** (K).

### 1.3 The Five Canonical Flight Regimes

| Mach Range | Regime Name | Dominant Physical Phenomenon |
|---|---|---|
| M < 0.3 | **Incompressible / Low Subsonic** | Negligible density variation; Bernoulli valid |
| 0.3 ≤ M < 0.8 | **Subsonic (Compressible)** | Density variation matters, no shocks |
| 0.8 ≤ M < 1.2 | **Transonic** | Mixed subsonic/supersonic flow, weak shocks |
| 1.2 ≤ M < 5.0 | **Supersonic** | Attached oblique shocks, Mach wave drag |
| 5.0 ≤ M < 10–20 | **Hypersonic** | Real gas effects, strong shock–boundary layer interaction |
| M > 10–20 | **Re-Entry / Free Molecular** | Discrete molecular collisions, slip flow |

> [!NOTE]
> **SYLLABUS HIGHLIGHT:** The KTU board consistently tests the *boundaries* of these regimes. Memorise that **0.8 and 1.2** are the canonical transonic boundaries, and **M = 5.0** is the conventional hypersonic threshold (after which real gas effects dominate).

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Speed of Sound as a Universal Speed Limit"

Imagine a crowd of people (air molecules) standing shoulder-to-shoulder in a corridor (an air parcel). Information travels through the crowd as a chain of "elbow nudges." The speed at which these nudges propagate is the **speed of sound**.

- **If you walk slowly (M < 0.3):** You never outpace the information ripples. Air ahead of you smoothly "gets out of the way" — this is **incompressible flow**.
- **If you jog (0.3 < M < 0.8):** Density starts varying; you feel a slight pressure bulge, but no "wall" forms.
- **If you sprint at the exact nudge-propagation speed (M ≈ 1.0):** Information can't outrun you. Pressure, density, and temperature spike discontinuously — a **shock wave** forms, like a sonic boom.
- **If you sprint faster than the crowd's nudge-speed (M > 1.0):** You leave a **conical pressure wake** behind you (Mach cone), heard as a sharp *boom*.
- **If you leap through the crowd at extreme speed (M > 5):** The corridor walls (air) ionise and glow; the air molecules dissociate — **hypersonic regime**.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Atmosphere Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Sea-level speed of sound:** $a_0 = 340.29$ m/s $\approx$ **1115 ft/s**
> - **Sea-level pressure:** $p_0 = 101325$ Pa
> - **Sea-level temperature:** $T_0 = 288.15$ K (15 °C)
> - **Sea-level density:** $\rho_0 = 1.225$ kg/m³
> - **Specific heat ratio (air):** $\gamma = 1.4$
> - **Universal gas constant:** $R^* = 8.314$ J/mol·K
> - **Air-specific gas constant:** $R = 287.05$ J/kg·K

These values are **assumed at standard sea-level (SSL) conditions** in all KTU numerical problems unless otherwise specified.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Subsonic Regime (M < 0.8)

- **Compressibility** is mild; density variations are < 5%.
- The **Prandtl–Glauert transformation** is valid for the *linearised* subsonic regime:

$$
C_p = \frac{C_{p,\, \text{inc}}}{\sqrt{1 - M_{\infty}^2}}
$$

where $C_p$ is the **pressure coefficient** corrected for compressibility, and $C_{p,\,\text{inc}}$ is the **incompressible** value.

- **Critical Mach Number (M_cr):** The freestream Mach number at which the *local* Mach number first reaches unity somewhere on the aircraft surface. This is the **trigger point for transonic shock formation**.

$$
M_{cr} = \frac{M_{\infty}}{\sqrt{1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M_{\infty}^2 \cdot f(\text{geometry})}}
$$

- The **Karmán–Tsien correction** gives a better subsonic compressibility correction than Prandtl–Glauert:

$$
C_p = \frac{C_{p,\,\text{inc}}}{\sqrt{1 - M_{\infty}^2} + \frac{M_{\infty}^2(1 + \sqrt{1 - M_{\infty}^2})}{2\sqrt{1 - M_{\infty}^2}} \cdot C_{p,\,\text{inc}}}
$$

### 2.2 Transonic Regime (0.8 ≤ M < 1.2)

- **Mixed flow field**: subsonic and supersonic pockets coexist on the same airfoil.
- **Critical Mach Number (M_cr)** is *exceeded* — local sonic condition (M_local = 1.0) is reached.
- **Drag Divergence Mach Number (M_DD):** The freestream M at which the drag coefficient exhibits a *sharp* non-linear rise due to wave drag. For a typical airfoil: **M_DD ≈ M_cr + 0.05 to 0.1**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two Defining Mach Numbers in Transonic Design:**
> 1. **M_cr** — onset of *local sonic flow* (where shocks *can* form).
> 2. **M_DD** — onset of *unacceptable drag rise* (where shocks *do* form with strength sufficient to cause wave drag spike).

- **Whitcomb Area Rule (1952):** The transonic drag rise can be *dramatically* reduced by shaping the fuselage cross-sectional area distribution to be smooth and near-elliptical. The derivative:

$$
\frac{d(\text{Wave Drag})}{dx} \propto \left( \frac{dA}{dx} \right)^2
$$

must be minimised — a *coke-bottle* or *wasp-waist* fuselage cross-section is the design manifestation.

### 2.3 Supersonic Regime (1.2 ≤ M < 5.0)

- **Oblique shocks** form at leading edges, terminating in **Mach waves** in the freestream.
- **Mach Angle (μ):** The half-angle of the Mach cone:

$$
\sin \mu = \frac{1}{M_{\infty}}
$$

- **Supersonic wave drag** scales with the *square* of the thickness ratio and with the lift coefficient:

$$
C_{D,w} \propto \frac{(t/c)^2}{\sqrt{M_{\infty}^2 - 1}} \quad \text{(linearised theory)}
$$

- **Sweepback Effect (Leading-Edge Sweep, Λ):** The *effective* Mach number normal to the leading edge is:

$$
M_n = M_{\infty} \cos \Lambda
$$

- A 45° swept wing sees its effective Mach number drop by a factor of $\cos 45° = 0.707$, pushing M_DD to much higher freestream M.

### 2.4 Hypersonic Regime (M ≥ 5.0)

- **Real gas effects** become non-negligible: vibrational modes of N₂ and O₂ are excited; O₂ dissociates above ~2000 K; N₂ dissociates above ~4000 K.
- **Aerodynamic heating** scales as:

$$
q_{\text{stag}} \approx \rho_{\infty} V_{\infty}^3 \propto M_{\infty}^3
$$

- **Newtonian sine-squared law** (hypersonic blunt-body approximation):

$$
C_p = 2 \sin^2 \theta
$$

where $\theta$ is the local body angle relative to the freestream.

- **Hypersonic similarity parameters:**
  - **Mach number independence:** $C_p$ becomes a function of body shape but *not* of M (for slender bodies at high M).
  - **Hypersonic equivalence principle:** $K = M_{\infty} \cdot \tau$ (where $\tau$ = thickness ratio) is the key combined parameter.

### 2.5 Free Molecular Flow (M > 10–20, very high altitude)

- The Knudsen number $Kn = \lambda / L$ (mean free path $\lambda$ vs. body length $L$) exceeds 10.
- Air behaves as discrete particles rather than a continuum; conventional Navier–Stokes equations **fail**.
- Relevant for **re-entry vehicles** at altitudes > 100 km.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | Typical KTU Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = V_{\infty} / a_{\infty}$ | Mach number definition | 1–2 |
| 2 | $a_{\infty} = \sqrt{\gamma R T_{\infty}}$ | Speed of sound | 1–2 |
| 3 | $T_0 / T_{\infty} = 1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M_{\infty}^2$ | Stagnation temperature ratio | 2–3 |
| 4 | $p_0 / p_{\infty} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M_{\infty}^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ | Stagnation pressure ratio | 2–3 |
| 5 | $\rho_0 / \rho_{\infty} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M_{\infty}^2\right)^{1/(\gamma-1)}$ | Stagnation density ratio | 2 |
| 6 | $\sin \mu = 1 / M_{\infty}$ | Mach angle | 1–2 |
| 7 | $M_n = M_{\infty} \cos \Lambda$ | Normal Mach (sweep effect) | 2 |
| 8 | $M_{cr}$ (from local M = 1 condition) | Critical Mach number | 3–5 |
| 9 | $C_p = C_{p,\text{inc}} / \sqrt{1 - M_{\infty}^2}$ | Prandtl–Glauert correction | 2–3 |
| 10 | $q_{\text{stag}} \propto M_{\infty}^3$ | Hypersonic heating | 2 |
| 11 | $\text{Wave Drag} \propto (dA/dx)^2$ | Whitcomb area rule | 2–3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU EXAM TIP:** Always write the **standard atmospheric values** in a "Given Data" block at the start of any numerical problem. Examiners award partial credit generously for correctly setting up the problem.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Commercial aviation (Boeing 787, Airbus A350):** Cruise at M ≈ 0.85 — deliberately *below* M_DD for fuel efficiency.
- **Fighter jets (F-22 Raptor):** Supercruise at M = 1.5+ — uses **highly swept wings** (42°), **supercritical airfoils**, and **area-ruled fuselage**.
- **Concorde:** Cruised at M = 2.02 (supersonic) with **droop nose** for low-speed visibility and **ogival delta wing** for supersonic efficiency.
- **Space Shuttle / Re-entry vehicles:** Hypersonic regime M = 15–25; uses **blunt nose** (Newtonian stagnation) for *deliberate* shock standoff to reduce heating flux.
- **X-15:** M = 6.7, the iconic hypersonic research platform.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Speed of Sound from Thermodynamics

We begin from the isentropic relation between pressure and density for a perfect gas:

$$
a^2 = \left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial \rho}\right)_s
$$

For an isentropic process in a calorically perfect gas:

$$
p = C \rho^{\gamma} \quad \text{(Poisson's relation)}
$$

Differentiating with respect to $\rho$:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial \rho}\right)_s = C \gamma \rho^{\gamma - 1} = \gamma \frac{p}{\rho}
$$

Using the ideal gas law $p = \rho R T$:

$$
a^2 = \gamma R T
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}}
$$

**Physical insight:** Sound speed scales with $\sqrt{T}$, which is why **higher-altitude (cooler) flight gives a lower speed of sound** — true airspeed for a given Mach *decreases* with altitude.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Stagnation Temperature Ratio

Apply the steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) between freestream and a stagnation point:

$$
h_0 = h_{\infty} + \frac{V_{\infty}^2}{2}
$$

For a calorically perfect gas, $h = c_p T$, so:

$$
c_p T_0 = c_p T_{\infty} + \frac{V_{\infty}^2}{2}
$$

Substituting $V_{\infty} = M_{\infty} a_{\infty}$ and $a_{\infty}^2 = \gamma R T_{\infty}$:

$$
c_p T_0 = c_p T_{\infty} + \frac{M_{\infty}^2 \gamma R T_{\infty}}{2}
$$

Dividing by $c_p T_{\infty}$ and using $c_p = \gamma R / (\gamma - 1)$:

$$
\frac{T_0}{T_{\infty}} = 1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M_{\infty}^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most important equation in compressible flow.** It governs the *thermal limit* of all high-speed aircraft. Note that $T_0$ depends **only on Mach and freestream temperature**, not on the body geometry.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Mach Angle from Geometry

Consider a point source of disturbance moving at velocity $V_{\infty}$ through a stationary medium. At time $t = 0$, the source is at point O. At time $t = t_0$, it is at point P. Pressure disturbances propagate spherically at speed $a_{\infty}$.

After time $t_0$, the disturbance emitted at $t = 0$ lies on a sphere of radius $a_{\infty} t_0$ centred at O. The source has moved a distance $V_{\infty} t_0$ from O to P.

The **envelope** of all such spheres is a cone (the Mach cone). The half-angle $\mu$ satisfies:

$$
\sin \mu = \frac{a_{\infty} t_0}{V_{\infty} t_0} = \frac{a_{\infty}}{V_{\infty}} = \frac{1}{M_{\infty}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\mu = \arcsin\left(\frac{1}{M_{\infty}}\right)}
$$

**Numerical check:** For $M = 2.0$, $\mu = 30°$. For $M = \sqrt{2}$, $\mu = 45°$.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Critical Mach Number (M_cr) for a Symmetric Airfoil

For a symmetric airfoil at small angle of attack, the maximum local Mach number occurs at the **crest** of the upper surface. By compressible potential flow theory, the local Mach number is related to the local $C_p$:

$$
\frac{M_{\text{local}}^2 - 1}{M_{\text{local}}^2} = \left( \frac{M_{\infty}^2 - 1}{M_{\infty}^2} \right) \cdot \frac{C_p}{C_{p,\,\text{crit}}}
$$

The critical condition is $M_{\text{local}} = 1$ at the airfoil crest, where $C_p = C_{p,\text{max}}$. In incompressible flow, using thin-airfoil theory:

$$
C_{p,\text{max, inc}} = 2 \sqrt{\frac{t/c}{1 - \sqrt{1 - (2t/c)^2}} \cdot \text{(shape factor)}}
$$

For a flat plate, a simpler engineering approximation (Kuchemann/Korving) is:

$$
M_{cr} \approx 1 - \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} \cdot C_{p,\text{max, inc}} \quad \text{(for } M_{cr} \approx 1 \text{)}
$$

Or equivalently, the **exact** expression for a flat plate is:

$$
M_{cr} = \left[ \frac{1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} \cdot C_{p,\text{max,inc}}}{1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} \cdot M_{\infty}^2} \right]^{1/2} \cdot \frac{1}{M_{\infty}}
$$

which must be solved **iteratively** since $M_{cr}$ appears on both sides.

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Sweepback Effect on M_DD

For a wing with leading-edge sweep angle $\Lambda_{LE}$, the component of freestream velocity *normal* to the leading edge is:

$$
V_n = V_{\infty} \cos \Lambda_{LE}
$$

The corresponding normal Mach number is:

$$
M_n = \frac{V_n}{a_{\infty}} = M_{\infty} \cos \Lambda_{LE}
$$

Subsonic compressibility effects (and shock formation) are governed by $M_n$, not $M_{\infty}$. So the **effective critical Mach number** of the swept wing is:

$$
M_{cr, \text{swept}} = \frac{M_{cr, \text{unswept}}}{\cos \Lambda_{LE}}
$$

This is why **swept wings** push the drag-divergence boundary to higher freestream Mach numbers — the cornerstone of transonic and supersonic transport design.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Regime-by-Regime Comparison

| Property | Subsonic | Transonic | Supersonic | Hypersonic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **M∞ range** | < 0.8 | 0.8–1.2 | 1.2–5.0 | > 5.0 |
| **Density variation** | < 5% | 5–20% | > 20% | Extreme |
| **Mach cone** | Doesn't exist | Just forming | Well-defined | Very narrow |
| **Shock waves** | None | Weak normal | Oblique/attached | Strong, near-body |
| **Wave drag** | None | Sudden rise | ~ 50% of total | Dominant |
| **Heating** | Negligible | Mild | Significant | Critical — material limit |
| **Real gas effects** | None | None | Mild onset | Dominant |
| **Governing theory** | Bernoulli / Prandtl-Glauert | Mixed; Euler with shocks | Linearised supersonic / shock-expansion | Newtonian / tangent-wedge / blunt-body |
| **Design priority** | Lift/drag polars | Area rule, sweep | Volume, slenderness | Heating, bluntness |
| **Example aircraft** | Cessna 172, GA aircraft | Boeing 737, F-16 | Concorde, F-15 | X-15, Space Shuttle |

### 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** An aircraft cruises at altitude 11 km, where the standard atmosphere gives $T_{\infty} = 216.65$ K and $a_{\infty} = 295.07$ m/s. The aircraft's true airspeed is 240 m/s. Determine the Mach number, stagnation temperature, and stagnation pressure.

**Solution:**

$$
M_{\infty} = \frac{V_{\infty}}{a_{\infty}} = \frac{240}{295.07} = 0.8133
$$

**Step 1 — Identify regime:** $M = 0.8133$ lies in the **transonic** range (0.8–1.2). The aircraft is in the *lower transonic* band.

**Step 2 — Stagnation temperature ratio:**

$$
\frac{T_0}{T_{\infty}} = 1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M_{\infty}^2 = 1 + \frac{0.4}{2} (0.8133)^2 = 1 + 0.1323 = 1.1323
$$

$$
T_0 = 216.65 \times 1.1323 = 245.30 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 3 — Stagnation pressure ratio:**

$$
\frac{p_0}{p_{\infty}} = (1.1323)^{3.5} = 1.5581
$$

If $p_{\infty} = 22632$ Pa at 11 km, then $p_0 = 35260$ Pa.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Strategy:** In transonic problems, the moment you compute $M > 0.8$, immediately state *"the aircraft is operating in the transonic regime; shock-wave formation and wave drag are likely."* This contextual line is awarded **viva-style marks** by KTU examiners.

    end

    subgraph TRANS[TRANSONIC 0.8 to 1.2]

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
Define Mach number and list the five flight regimes with their Mach number boundaries.

**Model Answer:**

The Mach number is the ratio of the true airspeed of an aircraft to the local speed of sound:

$$
M = \frac{V_{\infty}}{a_{\infty}}, \quad a_{\infty} = \sqrt{\gamma R T_{\infty}}
$$

The five flight regimes are:

1. **Subsonic:** M < 0.8
2. **Transonic:** 0.8 ≤ M < 1.2
3. **Supersonic:** 1.2 ≤ M < 5.0
4. **Hypersonic:** 5.0 ≤ M < 10–20
5. **Free Molecular / Re-entry:** M > 10–20

Each regime is distinguished by the dominant physical phenomenon: density variation (subsonic), local shock formation (transonic), oblique shocks and Mach cones (supersonic), real gas effects and heating (hypersonic), and continuum breakdown (free molecular).

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the regime list with boundaries]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
Explain the significance of the **critical Mach number** and the **drag divergence Mach number** in transonic aircraft design.

**Model Answer:**

The **critical Mach number (M_cr)** is the freestream Mach number at which the *local* Mach number first reaches unity at some point on the aircraft surface (typically the upper-surface crest of a wing). It is the *onset* point for compressibility effects and is a *necessary* condition for shock formation.

The **drag divergence Mach number (M_DD)** is the freestream Mach number at which the drag coefficient exhibits a sharp, non-linear rise due to wave drag from shock-induced boundary-layer separation. It is the *practical* limit for cruise design, since the drag rise causes a steep thrust-required increase.

**Key relationship:** $M_{DD} \approx M_{cr} + (0.05 \text{ to } 0.1)$.

**Designers** push M_DD to higher values by using:
- **Sweepback** to reduce normal Mach: $M_n = M_{\infty} \cos \Lambda$.
- **Supercritical airfoils** to flatten the upper-surface velocity distribution.
- **Whitcomb area rule** to smooth the fuselage cross-section.

**[3 Marks: 1 each for M_cr, M_DD, and design application]**

---

### **PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

(a) Derive the relation between the stagnation temperature ratio and the freestream Mach number for a calorically perfect gas. State clearly all assumptions used. **(7 Marks)**

(b) An aircraft cruises at 12,000 m altitude where $T_{\infty} = 216.65$ K, $a_{\infty} = 295.07$ m/s, and $p_{\infty} = 19,400$ Pa. The aircraft's true airspeed is 260 m/s. Determine: (i) the Mach number, (ii) the stagnation temperature, (iii) the stagnation pressure, (iv) the stagnation density, and (v) the Mach angle. Identify the flight regime. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, one-dimensional flow.
2. Calorically perfect gas ($\gamma$, $c_p$ constant).
3. Isentropic (adiabatic + reversible) deceleration to stagnation.
4. No shaft work or heat addition.

**Starting from the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE):**

$$
h_0 = h_{\infty} + \frac{V_{\infty}^2}{2}
$$

For a calorically perfect gas: $h = c_p T$. Therefore:

$$
c_p T_0 = c_p T_{\infty} + \frac{V_{\infty}^2}{2}
$$

Substitute $V_{\infty} = M_{\infty} a_{\infty}$ and $a_{\infty}^2 = \gamma R T_{\infty}$:

$$
c_p T_0 = c_p T_{\infty} + \frac{M_{\infty}^2 \cdot \gamma R T_{\infty}}{2}
$$

Divide throughout by $c_p T_{\infty}$. Using the relation $c_p = \frac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$:

$$
\frac{T_0}{T_{\infty}} = 1 + \frac{M_{\infty}^2 \gamma R T_{\infty}}{2 c_p T_{\infty}} = 1 + \frac{M_{\infty}^2 \gamma R}{2 c_p}
$$

$$
\frac{T_0}{T_{\infty}} = 1 + \frac{M_{\infty}^2 (\gamma - 1)}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{T_0}{T_{\infty}} = 1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M_{\infty}^2}
$$

This is the **Rayleigh pitot-tube formula** for stagnation temperature.

**[Marks distribution: 1 for assumptions, 2 for SFEE setup, 2 for substitution, 1 for gas-law relation, 1 for final expression]**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Mach number:**

$$
M_{\infty} = \frac{V_{\infty}}{a_{\infty}} = \frac{260}{295.07} = 0.8811
$$

**(ii) Stagnation temperature:**

$$
\frac{T_0}{T_{\infty}} = 1 + \frac{0.4}{2}(0.8811)^2 = 1 + 0.1553 = 1.1553
$$

$$
T_0 = 216.65 \times 1.1553 = 250.30 \text{ K}
$$

**(iii) Stagnation pressure:**

$$
