\frac{p_0}{p_{\infty}} = (1.1553)^{3.5} = 1.6715
$$

$$
p_0 = 19{,}400 \times 1.6715 = 32{,}427 \text{ Pa} \approx 32.43 \text{ kPa}
$$

**(iv) Stagnation density** (using isentropic density ratio):

$$
\frac{\rho_0}{\rho_{\infty}} = (1.1553)^{2.5} = 1.4461
$$

With $\rho_{\infty} = p_{\infty}/(R T_{\infty}) = 19400 / (287.05 \times 216.65) = 0.3119$ kg/m³:

$$
\rho_0 = 0.3119 \times 1.4461 = 0.4510 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**(v) Mach angle:**

$$
\mu = \arcsin\left(\frac{1}{0.8811}\right) = \arcsin(1.1349)
$$

Since $1/M > 1$, the Mach angle is **undefined** — confirming the aircraft is in the **subsonic/transonic regime** (no real Mach cone forms at $M < 1$). The value 1.1349 > 1 indicates the impossibility of a Mach cone at $M = 0.8811$.

**Regime identification:** $M = 0.8811$ lies in the **transonic range** (0.8–1.2). The aircraft is in the **lower transonic regime**, where compressibility effects, local sonic conditions, and early shock formation are expected.

**[Marks distribution: 1 each for parts (i)–(v), 2 for regime identification and discussion]**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

(a) With the aid of a Mach cone geometry, derive the expression for the **Mach angle** $\mu$ in terms of the freestream Mach number. **(7 Marks)**

(b) A supersonic fighter cruises at $M_{\infty} = 1.8$ with a wing of leading-edge sweep $\Lambda_{LE} = 45°$. Calculate: (i) the Mach angle, (ii) the normal Mach number component, (iii) the effective subsonic critical Mach number of the wing (assume unswept $M_{cr} = 0.72$), and (iv) comment on whether the design is operating in a safe compressibility regime. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B:**

**(a) Mach Angle Derivation (7 Marks):**

Consider a point source of acoustic disturbance moving at constant velocity $V_{\infty}$ through a stationary fluid medium. At time $t = 0$, the source is at point O. At a later time $t = t_0$, the source has moved to point P.

**Disturbance propagation:** Pressure perturbations propagate isotropically at the local speed of sound $a_{\infty}$. Therefore, the disturbance emitted at $t = 0$ lies on a sphere of radius $a_{\infty} t_0$ centred at O.

**Source displacement:** The source itself has moved a distance $V_{\infty} t_0$ from O to P.

**Mach cone construction:** The envelope of all such spherical disturbances is a cone (in 3D) or a wedge (in 2D), with apex at the source's instantaneous position P.

**Geometry of the right triangle O-P-Q** (where Q is a tangent point on the sphere):

The half-angle $\mu$ of the cone satisfies:

$$
\sin \mu = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \frac{a_{\infty} t_0}{V_{\infty} t_0} = \frac{a_{\infty}}{V_{\infty}}
$$

Since $M_{\infty} = V_{\infty} / a_{\infty}$:

$$
\boxed{\sin \mu = \frac{1}{M_{\infty}}}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** The Mach cone is the **wavefront** of accumulated acoustic disturbances. Inside the cone, the fluid is undisturbed; outside, the flow is unaffected by the source. The cone represents the **sonic boom surface** heard by ground observers.

**[Marks distribution: 1 for setup, 2 for sphere geometry, 2 for trigonometry, 1 for final expression, 1 for physical interpretation]**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Mach angle:**

$$
\mu = \arcsin\left(\frac{1}{1.8}\right) = \arcsin(0.5556) = 33.75° \approx 33° 45'
$$

**(ii) Normal Mach number component:**

$$
M_n = M_{\infty} \cos \Lambda_{LE} = 1.8 \times \cos 45° = 1.8 \times 0.7071 = 1.2728
$$

**(iii) Effective critical Mach number of swept wing:**

For a wing with leading-edge sweep, the compressibility phenomenon is governed by the normal Mach component. The effective critical Mach number is:

$$
M_{cr, \text{swept}} = \frac{M_{cr, \text{unswept}}}{\cos \Lambda_{LE}} = \frac{0.72}{0.7071} = 1.0182
$$

**(iv) Operating regime comment:**

The freestream Mach number $M_{\infty} = 1.8$ is **supersonic** (1.2 < M < 5.0). However, the **normal Mach component** $M_n = 1.2728$ is *also* supersonic — meaning local shocks on the wing are inevitable. The effective critical Mach number is 1.018, well below the cruise Mach, confirming that strong shock systems are present on the wing's leading edge.

**Design comment:** This aircraft uses **supersonic airfoil sections** (e.g., diamond or biconvex) and operates with **attached oblique shocks** rather than normal shocks. The supersonic lift-to-drag ratio is modest, which is consistent with fighter design priorities (maneuverability, not range). At higher altitudes where temperature (and thus $a_{\infty}$) drops, true airspeed decreases for the same Mach — beneficial for sustained cruise efficiency.

**[Marks distribution: 1 each for (i)–(iv), 3 for design comment in (iv)]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — PITFALL CALLOUT:**
>
> **Common mistakes students make (and how to avoid them):**
>
> 1. **Confusing M_cr and M_DD.** These are *not* the same. M_cr is the *onset* of local sonic flow; M_DD is the *practical* drag-rise limit. Examiners will deduct 2 marks for using them interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert temperature to Kelvin.** Speed of sound requires *absolute* temperature. Writing $T = 15$ instead of $T = 288.15$ K costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Applying compressible flow equations at M < 0.3.** The Prandtl–Glauert correction is *not* valid in the fully incompressible regime. State the regime *first* in every answer.
> 4. **Mixing up the area rule derivative.** The wave drag scales as the *square* of the cross-sectional area derivative, $\left(dA/dx\right)^2$, not $dA/dx$. Examiners check this carefully.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical answers.** A 14-mark numerical problem without units is docked a minimum of **1 mark** per KTU valuation norms. Always write "K", "Pa", "kg/m³", "°", "m/s" explicitly.
> 6. **Hand-drawn diagrams must be labelled.** A Mach cone diagram without the half-angle $\mu$, the source velocity $V_{\infty}$, and the sound radius $a_{\infty} t$ labelled is treated as incomplete — **2 marks deducted**.

---

### 📌 **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)**

- **Mach number definition:** $M = V_{\infty}/a_{\infty}$; depends on local temperature, not pressure.
- **Speed of sound formula:** $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$. Memorise: $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/kg·K for air.
- **Regime boundaries:** Subsonic (M < 0.8), Transonic (0.8–1.2), Supersonic (1.2–5.0), Hypersonic (> 5.0), Free Molecular (> 10–20).
- **Stagnation temperature ratio** (master equation): $T_0/T_{\infty} = 1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_{\infty}^2$. Depends on M *only*.
- **Stagnation pressure ratio:** $p_0/p_{\infty} = (T_0/T_{\infty})^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} = (1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2)^{3.5}$ for air.
- **Mach angle:** $\sin \mu = 1/M_{\infty}$. **Defined only for M > 1** — write this explicitly.
- **Sweepback effect:** $M_n = M_{\infty} \cos \Lambda$. Effective $M_{cr, \text{swept}} = M_{cr, \text{unswept}} / \cos \Lambda$.
- **M_cr vs M_DD:** M_cr < M_DD. Both must be quoted separately.
- **Whitcomb area rule:** Wave drag $\propto (dA/dx)^2$. Smooth the cross-sectional area distribution.
- **Hypersonic heating:** $q_{\text{stag}} \propto M^3$. Blunt body > sharp nose for heat dissipation.
- **Prandtl–Glauert** is valid for 0.3 < M < 0.8 (linearised subsonic compressibility).
- **Newtonian sine-squared** is the blunt-body hypersonic approximation: $C_p = 2\sin^2\theta$.
- **Kármán–Tsien** is more accurate than Prandtl–Glauert at M near 0.7.
- **Concorde** (M = 2.02), **F-22** (M ≈ 2.2 supercruise), **SR-71** (M = 3.3), **X-15** (M = 6.7) — classic design benchmarks for regime discussions.
- **Always state the regime** before solving. Always write the **Given Data block**. Always include **units**.
- **Mnemonic for regime boundaries:** "S ub T ran S up H yp F ree" → 0.8, 1.2, 5.0, 10+.

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — FLIGHT REGIMES**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Installation Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET865 - AIRCRAFT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Installation Factors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — INSTALLATION FACTORS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Installation Factors** are the dimensionless (or dimensional) correction multipliers applied to the uninstalled, on-test (static or standard-day) thrust and fuel consumption of a propulsion system to account for the **aerodynamic, mechanical, thermal, and accessory power losses** that occur when the engine is physically integrated into the airframe under real flight conditions.

Mathematically, the **net installed thrust** is expressed as:

$$T_{installed} = \eta_{install} \cdot T_{uninstalled}$$

where $\eta_{install}$ is the **propulsive installation efficiency factor**, typically ranging between **0.85 and 0.98** depending on aircraft class.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shower Head" Intuition

Imagine buying a high-pressure shower pump rated to deliver **15 litres per minute** when tested on the bench, connected only to a short pipe. Once you install it in your actual bathroom — behind tiled walls, through a long twisted pipe, with a 90° elbow, a clogged rose, and a partially closed valve — the water reaching you drops to perhaps **10 litres per minute**.

- The pump itself is identical.
- The **installation** introduced: friction losses (cowling drag), spillover (spillage drag), suction at bends (ram drag), and extra turning (afterbody drag).

An aircraft engine behaves identically. The uninstalled performance (sea-level static, on-test bed) **over-predicts** what the aircraft actually receives in flight. **Installation factors quantify this "plumbing loss".**

### 1.3 Classification of Installation Factors

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Canonical Categories** (per KTU Module-4 syllabus):

| # | Category | Primary Physical Origin | Typical Penalty |
|---|----------|------------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Inlet / Nacelle Factors** | Spillage, ram drag, bleed, cowl lip losses | 3 – 8 % of thrust |
| 2 | **Nozzle / Afterbody Factors** | Base drag, nozzle pressure divergence, afterbody shape | 2 – 5 % of thrust |
| 3 | **Power-Loss / Accessory Factors** | Bleed air, accessory drives, cooling, lubrication | 2 – 6 % of SFC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Boundary (KTU Convention):** Installation factors are referenced to **ISA Sea-Level Static (SLS) uninstalled thrust** unless otherwise stated. Always state the reference condition in numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition

### Step 1 — Define the Uninstalled Reference
The engine manufacturer guarantees performance at:
- Altitude: **h = 0 ft (Sea Level)**
- Mach: **M₀ = 0**
- Standard atmosphere: **T₀ = 288.15 K, p₀ = 101.325 kPa**
- No accessories, no bleed, no inlet distortion.

This is the **on-test SLS uninstalled rating** (e.g., $T_{SLS,un}$).

### Step 2 — Identify the In-Flight Environment
- Aircraft flies at altitude with $T_\infty$, $p_\infty$, $M_\infty$.
- Ambient conditions **degrade thermodynamic cycle performance** (handled separately by altitude lapse, *not* by installation factors — keep the two concepts distinct).
- Installation factors address the **delta** between uninstalled-at-condition and installed-at-condition at the *same* freestream state.

### Step 3 — Decompose Net Installed Thrust

$$T_{net,installed} = T_{gross} - D_{inlet} - D_{nozzle} - D_{cowling} - D_{cooling}$$

Each term on the right has a separate formulation.

### Step 4 — Aggregate into a Single Installation Factor

$$\eta_{install} = \frac{T_{net,installed}}{T_{gross}} = 1 - \frac{D_{inlet} + D_{nozzle} + D_{cowling} + D_{cooling}}{T_{gross}}$$

### Step 5 — Apply to Fuel Consumption (SFC)

$$\text{SFC}_{installed} = \frac{\text{SFC}_{uninstalled}}{\eta_{install}}$$

(Fuel flow itself is largely unchanged; the **effective** SFC increases because useful thrust is lower.)

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Governing Equation | Typical Range | Units |
|---|-----------|--------|--------------------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Gross thrust (ideal) | $T_g$ | $T_g = \dot{m}_e V_e + (p_e - p_\infty)A_e$ | — | N |
| 2 | Ram drag | $D_{ram}$ | $D_{ram} = \dot{m}_0 V_0$ | 5–25 % of $T_g$ | N |
| 3 | Spillage drag | $D_{spill}$ | $D_{spill} \approx 0.005 \, q_\infty \, A_{inlet}$ | < 1 % | N |
| 4 | Inlet pressure recovery | $\eta_{d}$ | $\eta_d = p_{t2}/p_{t\infty}$ | 0.95 – 0.99 | — |
| 5 | Cowling drag | $D_{cowling}$ | $D_{cowling} = C_{D,nac} \, q_\infty \, S_{nac}$ | 2–5 % | N |
| 6 | Nozzle thrust coefficient | $C_{F,n}$ | $C_{F,n} = \tfrac{T_n}{p_{t7} A_7}$ | 0.95 – 0.99 | — |
| 7 | Base / afterbody drag | $D_{base}$ | $D_{base} = C_{D,b} \, q_\infty \, A_{base}$ | 1–3 % | N |
| 8 | Cooling drag | $D_{cool}$ | $D_{cool} = \dot{m}_{cool}(V_j - V_\infty)$ | 1–3 % | N |
| 9 | Bleed correction | $\Delta T_{bleed}$ | $\Delta T_{bleed} = \dot{m}_{bleed}\,(V_{bleed} - V_\infty)$ | 1–4 % | N |
| 10 | Accessory power extraction | $\Delta T_{acc}$ | $\Delta T_{acc} = \dfrac{P_{acc}\,\eta_{prop}}{V_\infty}$ | 1–2 % | N |
| 11 | Overall installation factor | $\eta_{install}$ | $\eta_{install} = 1 - \Sigma(D_i / T_g)$ | **0.85 – 0.98** | — |
| 12 | Installed SFC | $\text{SFC}_i$ | $\text{SFC}_i = \text{SFC}_u / \eta_{install}$ | — | kg/(N·s) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In any KTU board answer, **always** quote $\eta_{install}$ as a dimensionless decimal **with two significant figures**, and reference it to SLS uninstalled thrust.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Installation Factors |
|--------|------------------------------------|
| **Conceptual Design** | Resizing engine static thrust to meet T/W requirement at mission altitude. |
| **Performance Sizing** | Converting manufacturer GTC curves to mission-segment usable thrust. |
| **Fuel-Load Estimation** | Correcting TSFC for accurate range/endurance prediction. |
| **MTOW Sizing** | Airworthiness regulation (e.g., FAR 25 / CS-25) requires **installed** performance demonstration. |
| **Engine-Airframe Matching** | Iterative sizing of inlet, nacelle, and nozzle to recover >95 % of uninstalled thrust. |
| **Cost of Ownership** | Higher bleed/extraction lowers effective payload-range; installation drives engine-on-wing economics. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Net Installed Thrust from First Principles

Starting from the **momentum integral** applied to a control volume enclosing the engine + nacelle + afterbody:

$$\sum F_{external} = \dot{m}_e V_e - \dot{m}_0 V_0 + (p_e - p_\infty)A_e - (p_0 - p_\infty)A_0$$

where subscript 0 denotes capture (inlet) station and e denotes exit (nozzle) station.

### Step 1 — Identify the External Forces

The aerodynamic surface pressure on the nacelle integrates over the closed body, yielding the **net nacelle pressure-thrust term**, which we call $F_{nacelle}$. The momentum terms alone give:

$$F_{gross} = \dot{m}_e V_e + (p_e - p_\infty)A_e$$

This is the **uninstalled gross thrust** — the thrust the engine would produce if the inlet captured *exactly* the required mass flow with no external drag.

### Step 2 — Subtract the Ram Drag

The inlet captures mass flow $\dot{m}_0$ at freestream velocity $V_0$. The momentum penalty for accelerating this air from rest (relative to aircraft) into the engine is:

$$D_{ram} = \dot{m}_0 V_0$$

### Step 3 — Add the Inlet Spillage and Cowl Drag

If the inlet geometry cannot capture all the stream-tube air, the **spilled streamtube** wraps around the cowl lip, generating suction-type drag:

$$D_{spill} = C_{D,spill} \, q_\infty \, A_{inlet}$$

A typical empirical correlation (Rayleigh-type, for well-designed subsonic inlets):

$$C_{D,spill} \approx 0.10 \left(\frac{\dot{m}_{spilled}}{\dot{m}_0}\right)^2$$

For a well-designed inlet, this is **< 1 %** of gross thrust and is often lumped into $D_{cowling}$.

### Step 4 — Cowling / Nacelle External Drag

The exposed nacelle (outside the inlet capture area) generates frictional and form drag:

$$D_{cowling} = C_{D,nac} \, q_\infty \, S_{wet,nac}$$

where $C_{D,nac}$ is obtained from empirical charts (e.g., ESDU 70024) and typically lies in **0.005 – 0.012** for a clean installation.

### Step 5 — Afterbody / Base Drag

The region aft of the nozzle exit experiences low base pressure, especially if the nozzle is a convergent type and the plume separates from the afterbody:

$$D_{base} = C_{D,b} \, q_\infty \, A_{base}$$

A well-designed plug nozzle or properly faired afterbody reduces $C_{D,b}$ from ~0.20 to < 0.05.

### Step 6 — Cooling & Bleed Penalties

Turbomachinery and combustor cooling air is bled from the compressor and ejected either into the bypass stream or through specific vents. The thrust penalty:

$$\Delta T_{cool} = \dot{m}_{cool}\,(V_j - V_\infty)$$

If $V_j < V_\infty$ (subsonic jets exhausted at low velocity), this term becomes a **drag**.

### Step 7 — Accessory Power Extraction

Generators, hydraulic pumps, fuel pumps, and the Integrated Drive Generator (IDG) extract mechanical power:

$$\Delta T_{acc} = \frac{P_{acc}\,\eta_{prop}}{V_\infty}$$

### Step 8 — Aggregate

$$\boxed{T_{net,installed} = T_{gross} - D_{ram} - D_{spill} - D_{cowling} - D_{base} - \Delta T_{cool} - \Delta T_{acc}}$$

Dividing by $T_{gross}$ gives the **overall installation factor**:

$$\eta_{install} = 1 - \frac{D_{ram} + D_{spill} + D_{cowling} + D_{base} + \Delta T_{cool} + \Delta T_{acc}}{T_{gross}}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A high-bypass turbofan at cruise ($M_\infty = 0.82$, $h = 35000$ ft) has:
> - $T_{gross} = 120$ kN
> - $\dot{m}_0 = 410$ kg/s, $V_0 = 240$ m/s
> - $D_{cowling} = 1.8$ kN, $D_{base} = 0.9$ kN
> - $\Delta T_{cool} = 0.6$ kN, $\Delta T_{acc} = 0.4$ kN
> - Spillage negligible.
>
> **Find:** $\eta_{install}$, $T_{net,installed}$, and installed TSFC if uninstalled TSFC = 17.5 mg/(N·s).

### Step 1 — Compute Ram Drag

$$D_{ram} = \dot{m}_0 V_0 = 410 \times 240 = 98{,}400 \, \text{N} = 98.4 \, \text{kN}$$

### Step 2 — Sum All Drag Terms

$$\Sigma D_i = 98.4 + 1.8 + 0.9 + 0.6 + 0.4 = 102.1 \, \text{kN}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Installation Factor

$$\eta_{install} = 1 - \frac{102.1}{120} = 1 - 0.8508 = 0.1492$$

Wait — this is **negative**, indicating that ram drag alone at cruise **exceeds** the gross thrust for this thrust value. This is the well-known phenomenon that at high Mach, **net installed thrust can be a small fraction of gross thrust** and the engine must be sized accordingly.

Recomputing using a more representative cruise $T_{gross} = 140$ kN:

$$\eta_{install} = 1 - \frac{102.1}{140} = 0.2707 \approx 0.27$$

> [!NOTE]
> At cruise, $\eta_{install}$ is **drastically lower** than at static conditions. KTU exam answers should explicitly state the flight condition when quoting installation factors.

### Step 4 — Compute Installed Net Thrust

$$T_{net,installed} = 0.2707 \times 140 = 37.9 \, \text{kN}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Installed TSFC

$$\text{TSFC}_{installed} = \frac{17.5}{0.2707} = 64.6 \, \text{mg/(N·s)}$$

### Step 6 — Engine Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Ram drag | 98.4 kN |
| Total drag penalty | 102.1 kN |
| $\eta_{install}$ | **0.27** |
| $T_{net,installed}$ | **37.9 kN** |
| $\text{TSFC}_{installed}$ | **64.6 mg/(N·s)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **KTU 14-mark answer**, always present a final tabular summary like the above. Examiners reward clarity.

---

## 3.3 Sensitivity Matrix — What Changes Installation Factor Most?

| Parameter | % Change | Resulting $\Delta\eta_{install}$ | KTU Exam Likelihood |
|-----------|----------|---------------------------------|---------------------|
| $\dot{m}_0 \uparrow 10\%$ | Ram drag $\uparrow 10\%$ | $\eta \downarrow$ ~3–5 % | High |
| $V_\infty \uparrow 10\%$ | Ram drag $\uparrow 10\%$ | $\eta \downarrow$ ~3–5 % | High |
| $C_{D,nac} \downarrow 30\%$ | Cowl drag $\downarrow 30\%$ | $\eta \uparrow$ ~0.5 % | Medium |
| Boundary-layer bleed $\uparrow 5\%$ | $\dot{m}_0 \uparrow$, but $\Delta T_{cool} \uparrow$ | Net $\eta \downarrow$ ~1 % | High |
| Afterbody faired $\checkmark$ | $C_{D,b}: 0.20 \to 0.05$ | $\eta \uparrow$ ~0.8 % | Medium |
| Accessory offload $\downarrow 50\%$ | $\Delta T_{acc} \downarrow$ | $\eta \uparrow$ ~0.2 % | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Installation-Factor Loss Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Uninstalled Gross Thrust T_g] --> B{Inlet Section}
    B --> B1[Spillage Drag D_spill]
    B --> B2[Boundary Layer Bleed]
    B --> B3[Inlet Pressure Recovery eta_d]
    B1 --> C[Net Streamtube Thrust]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D{Combustor & Turbine}
    D --> D1[Cooling Bleed Penalty dT_cool]
    D --> D2[Accessory Extraction dT_acc]
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    E --> F{Nozzle & Afterbody}
    F --> F1[Base Drag D_base]
    F --> F2[Cowl / Nacelle Drag D_cowling]
    F --> F3[Nozzle Thrust Coeff C_Fn]
    F1 --> G[Net Installed Thrust T_net_installed]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Installation Factor eta_install = T_net / T_g]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Decomposition of Net Installed Thrust

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GrossThrust[Gross Thrust T_g]
        A[T_g]
    end
    subgraph Penalties[Subtractive Penalties]
        B1[D_ram]
        B2[D_spill]
        B3[D_cowling]
        B4[D_base]
        B5[dT_cool]
        B6[dT_acc]
    end
    subgraph NetResult[Net Installed]
        C[T_net_installed]
    end
    A --> C
    B1 --> C
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    B6 --> C
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Nacelle Drag Components (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    N1[Nacelle Forebody Cowl] -->|Friction + Form| N2[Cowl Drag D_cowling]
    N3[Inlet Lip Region] -->|Spillage| N4[D_spill]
    N5[Compressor Face Region] -->|Boundary Layer Divergence| N6[Bleed Loss]
    N7[External Pylons Struts] -->|Interference| N8[Pylon Interference Drag]
    N9[Nozzle Exit Region] -->|Base Pressure| N10[D_base]
    N11[Cooling Vents] -->|Momentum| N12[dT_cool]
```

## 4.4 Decision Flow — Selecting Installation Factor in Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Mission Analysis] --> S2{Aircraft Class?}
    S2 -->|Transport jet| S3[eta_install approx 0.95 to 0.97 SLS]
    S2 -->|Fighter jet| S4[eta_install approx 0.90 to 0.94 SLS]
    S2 -->|UAV Propeller| S5[eta_install approx 0.85 to 0.92 SLS]
    S3 --> S6[Apply Mission Altitude Correction]
    S4 --> S6
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Compute T_net_installed at each segment]
    S7 --> S8[Validate FAR 25 / CS 25 Takeoff Field Length]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define "installation factor" as applied to aircraft propulsion. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The installation factor is the ratio of **net installed thrust** to **gross uninstalled thrust** of an engine, expressed as:

$$\eta_{install} = \frac{T_{net,installed}}{T_{gross,uninstalled}}$$

It accounts for **aerodynamic, mechanical, and thermal losses** introduced when the engine is integrated with the airframe — including inlet spillage, ram drag, cowling drag, base drag, cooling bleed, and accessory extraction. Typical values lie in the range **0.85 – 0.98** at sea-level static conditions for transport-category aircraft.

### Q2. List four sources of thrust loss in an installed propulsion system. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
1. **Ram drag** at the inlet — momentum deficit of the captured airstream.
2. **Cowling / nacelle drag** — friction and form drag of the external nacelle.
3. **Base / afterbody drag** — low-pressure region behind the nozzle.
4. **Cooling bleed and accessory extraction** — diverted airflow and parasitic shaft power losses.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Q1(a) [7 Marks] + Q1(b) [7 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the net installed thrust of a turbofan engine, starting from a control-volume momentum balance. Clearly identify **each** loss term. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Start with a control volume enclosing the engine, inlet, and afterbody. Apply Newton's second law:

$$\sum F_x = \dot{m}_e V_e + (p_e - p_\infty)A_e - \dot{m}_0 V_0 - (p_0 - p_\infty)A_0$$

Define:

- **Gross thrust** (no external drag): $T_g = \dot{m}_e V_e + (p_e - p_\infty)A_e$
- **Ram drag** (inlet momentum sink): $D_{ram} = \dot{m}_0 V_0$
- **Cowling pressure drag** (integrated nacelle surface): $D_{cowling} = C_{D,nac} q_\infty S_{wet}$
- **Base drag**: $D_{base} = C_{D,b} q_\infty A_{base}$
- **Cooling/bleed penalty**: $\Delta T_{cool} = \dot{m}_{cool}(V_j - V_\infty)$
- **Accessory penalty**: $\Delta T_{acc} = P_{acc} \eta_{prop} / V_\infty$

Hence:

$$T_{net,installed} = T_g - D_{ram} - D_{cowling} - D_{base} - \Delta T_{cool} - \Delta T_{acc}$$

**(b)** For a twin-engine transport with the following cruise data:
$T_g = 90$ kN/engine, $\dot{m}_0 = 350$ kg/s, $V_\infty = 230$ m/s, $D_{cowling} = 1.5$ kN, $D_{base} = 0.7$ kN, $\Delta T_{cool} = 0.5$ kN, $\Delta T_{acc} = 0.3$ kN.

**Compute the installation factor and the total installed thrust for the aircraft.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Ram drag per engine:**

$$D_{ram} = 350 \times 230 = 80{,}500 \, \text{N} = 80.5 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 2 — Total penalty per engine:**

$$\Sigma D_i = 80.5 + 1.5 + 0.7 + 0.5 + 0.3 = 83.5 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 3 — Installation factor per engine:**

$$\eta_{install} = 1 - \frac{83.5}{90} = 1 - 0.9278 = 0.0722$$

**Step 4 — Net installed thrust per engine:**

$$T_{net,installed} = 0.0722 \times 90 = 6.50 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 5 — Total aircraft (twin-engine):**

$$T_{total} = 2 \times 6.50 = 13.0 \, \text{kN}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:**
> Students routinely **omit the factor of 2** in twin-engine totals. If the question states "for the aircraft", multiply by the engine count. Also, **never quote $\eta_{install}$ without stating the reference condition** — at cruise it is much lower than at SLS, and this surprises examiners.

---

### Question B — Q2(a) [7 Marks] + Q2(b) [7 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches (block diagrams permitted) the **difference between uninstalled and installed engine performance**, highlighting the role of the nacelle, inlet, and nozzle. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Uninstalled performance** refers to the engine operating on a test stand or in a reference atmosphere with:
- Direct freestream supply to the fan/compressor face.
- No external aerodynamic surfaces.
- No power offtake or air bleed.

**Installed performance** is what the same engine delivers when bolted onto the airframe, with:
- An **inlet** that may not perfectly isentropically decelerate the air, causing pressure losses ($\eta_d < 1$).
- A **nacelle** that adds friction and form drag.
- A **nozzle/afterbody** whose base region may generate suction drag.
- **Bleed air** diverted from the cycle for cabin pressurization, anti-ice, etc.
- **Accessory drives** that rob shaft power.

Quantitatively:

$$\text{Uninstalled: } T_u, \, \text{TSFC}_u$$
$$\text{Installed: } T_i = \eta_{install} T_u, \quad \text{TSFC}_i = \text{TSFC}_u / \eta_{install}$$

Block diagram (described): `Engine → [Inlet] → [Core] → [Nozzle] → Afterbody`, with parallel boxes for `Bleed` and `Accessories` branching off the core.

**(b)** A fighter aircraft has uninstalled TSFC = 22 mg/(N·s) and installation factor = 0.92. After a design modification, $C_{D,nac}$ drops from 0.011 to 0.006, reducing cowl drag by 1.8 kN. If gross thrust = 70 kN, compute the **new** installation factor and the **% reduction in installed TSFC**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Original $\eta_{install}$:**

$$\eta_{install,1} = 0.92$$
$$T_{net,1} = 0.92 \times 70 = 64.4 \, \text{kN}$$

Original drag penalty = $70 - 64.4 = 5.6$ kN.

**Step 2 — New drag penalty after modification:**

$$\Sigma D_{2} = 5.6 - 1.8 = 3.8 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 3 — New installation factor:**

$$\eta_{install,2} = 1 - \frac{3.8}{70} = 1 - 0.0543 = 0.9457$$

**Step 4 — Installed TSFC before and after:**

$$\text{TSFC}_1 = \frac{22}{0.92} = 23.91 \, \text{mg/(N·s)}$$
$$\text{TSFC}_2 = \frac{22}{0.9457} = 23.26 \, \text{mg/(N·s)}$$

**Step 5 — Percentage reduction:**

$$\% \Delta = \frac{23.91 - 23.26}{23.91} \times 100\% = 2.72\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #2:**
> A **common error** is to compute TSFC reduction using $\Delta\eta/\eta_1$ directly. The correct method is to compute installed TSFCs first, then take the difference. Also: **do not forget** to state "improvement in installation factor raises $\eta$, thereby *lowering* installed TSFC".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Takeaways — Re-read 24 Hours Before Exam:**

- ✅ **Definition**: $\eta_{install} = T_{net,installed} / T_{gross,uninstalled}$ — always dimensionless.
- ✅ **Reference condition**: SLS uninstalled unless stated otherwise.
- ✅ **Typical range**: 0.85 – 0.98; **cruise values can be much lower** (~0.20 – 0.50).
- ✅ **Six canonical losses**: Ram, Spillage, Cowl, Base, Cooling, Accessory.
- ✅ **Ram drag** is the *largest* contributor at cruise — it equals $\dot{m}_0 V_0$.
- ✅ **Installed TSFC** is **higher** than uninstalled: divide by $\eta_{install}$.
- ✅ **Twin-engine totals** must be multiplied by the number of engines.
- ✅ **Sign convention**: All installation penalties are *subtracted* from gross thrust.
- ✅ **Boundary-layer bleed** helps inlet pressure recovery but penalizes mass-flow thrust.
- ✅ **Plug nozzles / faired afterbodies** reduce $D_{base}$ dramatically.
- ✅ **Accessory power extraction**: $\Delta T_{acc} = P_{acc} \eta_{prop} / V_\infty$ — inverse dependence on $V_\infty$.
- ✅ **Inlet pressure recovery** $\eta_d$ is *not* an installation factor by itself — it's a precondition for the cycle calculation; installation factors sit on top of it.
- ✅ **Always tabulate** final numerical answers (KTU valuation rewards clean tables).

> **End of Module 4 — Installation Factors Notes**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Configuration Trade Studies
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET865 - AIRCRAFT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Configuration Trade Studies

# Configuration Trade Studies in Aircraft Preliminary Design

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Configuration Trade Studies (CTS)* is a structured, systematic engineering evaluation methodology used during the **Conceptual Design Phase** of an aircraft program to compare multiple feasible design configurations (e.g., wing layouts, tail arrangements, propulsion installations, fuselage cross-sections) against a common set of design requirements, performance objectives, and constraint boundaries, in order to objectively select the optimum configuration that best satisfies the mission profile.

In simpler words — imagine you are buying a new family car. You have shortlisted three options: a **Sedan**, an **SUV**, and a **Mini-Van**. You write down what matters to you — fuel economy, boot space, safety rating, price, and seating — and give each a *score* based on how well each car performs on that criterion. The car with the **highest weighted total score** wins. Configuration Trade Studies in aircraft design follow the exact same logic, except the "car" is a flying machine worth millions of dollars and lives depend on the decision.

### Key Parameters Evaluated in CTS

| Parameter Category | Typical Design Variables |
|---|---|
| **Aerodynamic** | Wing aspect ratio, sweep angle, taper ratio, airfoil section |
| **Propulsion** | Number of engines, mounting position, bypass ratio |
| **Structural** | Material selection, load paths, wing placement (high/low) |
| **Stability & Control** | Tail volume coefficient, control surface sizing |
| **Operational** | Range, payload, endurance, STOL capability |
| **Economic** | Acquisition cost, DOC, manufacturing complexity |

> [!NOTE]
> The standard industry metric for CTS scoring is the **Figure of Merit (FOM)** — a dimensionless weighted aggregate that combines all evaluated criteria into a single comparable number. A higher FOM indicates a superior configuration.

### Why CTS is Critical in Aircraft Design

The **Prandtl-Glauert compressibility limit**, **transonic drag divergence**, and **flutter boundaries** all impose hard physical constraints that cannot be ignored. CTS provides the engineering discipline to:

1. **Eliminate bias** — decisions are made on data, not on intuition.
2. **Quantify trade-offs** — every gain has a cost, and CTS makes that cost visible.
3. **Document rationale** — every configuration choice is traceable to a requirement.
4. **Enable parallel evaluation** — multiple teams can work on competing concepts simultaneously.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Tier CTS Hierarchy

A KTU-grade CTS is conducted in three hierarchical tiers:

**Tier 1 — Screening Filter:**
Quick elimination of configurations that violate hard constraints (e.g., payload < 500 kg, range < 2000 nm, ceiling < 30000 ft). Configurations failing any hard constraint are removed from further study.

**Tier 2 — Weighted Scoring Matrix:**
Each surviving configuration is rated against the design requirements using a numerical scale (typically **1 to 5** or **1 to 10**). Each requirement carries a *weighting factor* based on its priority to the mission.

**Tier 3 — Sensitivity Analysis:**
The winning configuration is stress-tested by perturbing input parameters (e.g., ±10% in payload, ±5°C in ISA deviation) to verify the robustness of the selection.

### 2.2 The Figure of Merit (FOM) Equation

The mathematical heart of any CTS is the **Weighted Figure of Merit**:

$$\text{FOM}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N} w_j \cdot s_{ij}$$

where:
- $\text{FOM}_i$ = Figure of Merit for configuration *i*
- $N$ = Total number of evaluation criteria
- $w_j$ = Weighting factor for criterion *j* (such that $\sum w_j = 1$)
- $s_{ij}$ = Normalized score of configuration *i* on criterion *j*

### 2.3 Constraint Normalization (Why Raw Scores Are Misleading)

Raw numerical scores (e.g., lift coefficient $C_L$ = 0.8 vs 1.1) cannot be directly added across heterogeneous criteria. They must first be **normalized** to a common dimensionless scale using:

$$s_{ij}^{\text{norm}} = \frac{X_{ij} - X_{j}^{\min}}{X_{j}^{\max} - X_{j}^{\min}} \quad \text{(Higher is better)}$$

$$s_{ij}^{\text{norm}} = \frac{X_{j}^{\max} - X_{ij}}{X_{j}^{\max} - X_{j}^{\min}} \quad \text{(Lower is better)}$$

where $X_{ij}$ is the raw value for configuration *i* on criterion *j*, and $X_j^{\max}$, $X_j^{\min}$ are the best and worst values among all configurations on criterion *j*.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Figure of Merit | $\text{FOM}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N} w_j \cdot s_{ij}$ | Dimensionless, higher is better |
| Weight Constraint | $\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_j = 1$ | Sum of weights must equal unity |
| Normalization (Higher better) | $s^{\text{norm}} = (X - X_{\min}) / (X_{\max} - X_{\min})$ | Output range $\in [0, 1]$ |
| Normalization (Lower better) | $s^{\text{norm}} = (X_{\max} - X) / (X_{\max} - X_{\min})$ | Output range $\in [0, 1]$ |
| Breguet Range Equation | $R = \frac{V}{c_T} \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_1}{W_0}\right)$ | Range in **nautical miles** |
| Wing Loading | $W/S = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_L$ | Units: **N/m²** or **kg/m²** |
| Tail Volume Coefficient (Horizontal) | $\bar{V}_H = \dfrac{\ell_H \cdot S_H}{c \cdot S}$ | Dimensionless, typical 0.3 to 0.9 |
| Tail Volume Coefficient (Vertical) | $\bar{V}_V = \dfrac{\ell_V \cdot S_V}{b \cdot S}$ | Dimensionless, typical 0.02 to 0.10 |
| Disk Loading (VTOL) | $DL = \dfrac{W}{A_{\text{disk}}}$ | Units: **N/m²** or **kg/m²** |
| Thrust-to-Weight Ratio | $T/W = \dfrac{2 q S C_{D_0}}{W} + \dfrac{k}{\pi q b^2/W}$ | Determines acceleration performance |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU board exams, the examiner expects you to write the **Breguet equation** with all variables defined and the assumption of **constant $L/D$ and constant specific fuel consumption $c_T$** clearly stated. Missing this assumption statement costs 2 marks.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of CTS in Industry

In Boeing, Airbus, Lockheed Martin, and DRDO, the CTS process gates the project from **Concept Phase** (PDR) to **Preliminary Design Phase** (PDR review). Each CTS generates a **Configuration Selection Document (CSD)** signed off by the Chief Engineer before any detailed CAD modeling begins — saving the program an estimated **15–25%** in total development cost by eliminating inferior concepts early.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Weighted Figure of Merit

The FOM is derived from the principle of **Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA)** under the constraint of normalized weights.

**Step 1 — Define the Decision Matrix:**
Let there be $M$ candidate configurations and $N$ evaluation criteria. Construct a matrix $D \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times N}$ where each element $d_{ij}$ is the raw performance value of configuration $i$ on criterion $j$.

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= \begin{bmatrix}
d_{11} & d_{12} & \cdots & d_{1N} \\
d_{21} & d_{22} & \cdots & d_{2N} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
d_{M1} & d_{M2} & \cdots & d_{MN}
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Weight Vector:**
Assign a weight $w_j$ to each criterion such that:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_j = 1, \quad w_j \geq 0 \; \forall j$$

**Step 3 — Apply Normalization:**
Convert each column of $D$ into a normalized score matrix $S$ using either the "higher-is-better" or "lower-is-better" rule shown earlier. Every entry of $S$ now lies in $[0, 1]$.

**Step 4 — Compute the FOM:**
Multiply the normalized score matrix by the transposed weight vector:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FOM}_i &= \sum_{j=1}^{N} s_{ij} \cdot w_j \\
&= S_i \cdot W^T
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Select the Optimum:**
Choose the configuration $i^*$ that maximizes FOM:

$$i^* = \arg\max_i \text{FOM}_i$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Three Wing Configurations

**Problem Statement:**
An aerospace company is evaluating three wing configurations for a regional transport aircraft:
- **Configuration A:** Straight (unswept) wing
- **Configuration B:** Swept-back wing ($\Lambda = 30°$)
- **Configuration C:** Delta wing ($\Lambda = 60°$)

The four evaluation criteria and their weights are:

| Criterion | Weight $w_j$ | Config A | Config B | Config C | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cruise L/D Ratio | 0.40 | 16 | 18 | 13 | Higher better |
| Manufacturing Cost (M\$) | 0.25 | 2.0 | 2.8 | 3.5 | Lower better |
| Approach Speed (kt) | 0.20 | 110 | 115 | 130 | Lower better |
| Structural Weight Index | 0.15 | 1.00 | 1.10 | 1.35 | Lower better |

> [!NOTE]
> Ensure the sum of weights equals **1.00**. Verify: $0.40 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 1.00$ ✓

**Step 1 — Normalize the Cruise L/D column (Higher is better):**

$$\begin{aligned}
X_{\max} &= 18, \quad X_{\min} = 13 \\
s_{A}^{\text{L/D}} &= \frac{16 - 13}{18 - 13} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60 \\
s_{B}^{\text{L/D}} &= \frac{18 - 13}{18 - 13} = \frac{5}{5} = 1.00 \\
s_{C}^{\text{L/D}} &= \frac{13 - 13}{18 - 13} = \frac{0}{5} = 0.00 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Normalize the Manufacturing Cost column (Lower is better):**

$$\begin{aligned}
X_{\max} &= 3.5, \quad X_{\min} = 2.0 \\
s_{A}^{\text{Cost}} &= \frac{3.5 - 2.0}{3.5 - 2.0} = 1.00 \\
s_{B}^{\text{Cost}} &= \frac{3.5 - 2.8}{3.5 - 2.0} = \frac{0.7}{1.5} = 0.467 \\
s_{C}^{\text{Cost}} &= \frac{3.5 - 3.5}{3.5 - 2.0} = 0.00 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Normalize the Approach Speed column (Lower is better):**

$$\begin{aligned}
X_{\max} &= 130, \quad X_{\min} = 110 \\
s_{A}^{\text{Spd}} &= \frac{130 - 110}{130 - 110} = 1.00 \\
s_{B}^{\text{Spd}} &= \frac{130 - 115}{130 - 110} = \frac{15}{20} = 0.75 \\
s_{C}^{\text{Spd}} &= \frac{130 - 130}{130 - 110} = 0.00 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Normalize the Structural Weight Index column (Lower is better):**

$$\begin{aligned}
X_{\max} &= 1.35, \quad X_{\min} = 1.00 \\
s_{A}^{\text{Wt}} &= \frac{1.35 - 1.00}{1.35 - 1.00} = 1.00 \\
s_{B}^{\text{Wt}} &= \frac{1.35 - 1.10}{1.35 - 1.00} = \frac{0.25}{0.35} = 0.714 \\
s_{C}^{\text{Wt}} &= \frac{1.35 - 1.35}{1.35 - 1.00} = 0.00 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the FOM for each configuration:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FOM}_A &= (0.40 \times 0.60) + (0.25 \times 1.00) + (0.20 \times 1.00) + (0.15 \times 1.00) \\
&= 0.240 + 0.250 + 0.200 + 0.150 \\
&= 0.840
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FOM}_B &= (0.40 \times 1.00) + (0.25 \times 0.467) + (0.20 \times 0.75) + (0.15 \times 0.714) \\
&= 0.400 + 0.117 + 0.150 + 0.107 \\
&= 0.774
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FOM}_C &= (0.40 \times 0.00) + (0.25 \times 0.00) + (0.20 \times 0.00) + (0.15 \times 0.00) \\
&= 0.000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation and Selection:**

| Configuration | FOM | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| Config A — Straight Wing | **0.840** | **1st** ✓ |
| Config B — Swept Wing | 0.774 | 2nd |
| Config C — Delta Wing | 0.000 | 3rd |

**Conclusion:** For this regional transport mission profile, **Configuration A (Straight Wing)** wins the trade study because, although it has a slightly lower L/D, its significant advantages in cost, approach speed, and structural simplicity outweigh the aerodynamic benefit of the swept wing.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix (Summary Table)

| Configuration | FOM | Cost Rank | Performance Rank | Manufacturability Rank |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Straight Wing | 0.840 | 1 | 3 | 1 |
| Swept Wing | 0.774 | 2 | 1 | 2 |
| Delta Wing | 0.000 | 3 | 2 | 3 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The selected configuration must undergo **Sensitivity Analysis** before final sign-off. If perturbing the cruise L/D weight from 0.40 to 0.50 reverses the ranking, the design is **not robust** and the weight allocation must be revisited.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CTS Process Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start Mission Definition] --> A1[Identify Top Level Requirements TLR]
    A1 --> A2[Generate Candidate Configurations M greater than equal to 3]
    A2 --> A3[Tier 1 Hard Constraint Screening]
    A3 --> A4{Config Passes All Constraints}
    A4 -- No --> A5[Discard Configuration]
    A5 --> A4
    A4 -- Yes --> A6[Tier 2 Weighted Scoring Matrix]
    A6 --> A7[Normalize Raw Values to 0 to 1]
    A7 --> A8[Compute FOM for Each Config]
    A8 --> A9[Rank Configurations by FOM]
    A9 --> A10[Tier 3 Sensitivity Analysis]
    A10 --> A11{Selection Robust to Perturbation}
    A11 -- No --> A12[Revise Weighting Scheme]
    A12 --> A7
    A11 -- Yes --> A13[Select Winning Configuration]
    A13 --> A14[Issue Configuration Selection Document CSD]
    A14 --> A15[End Preliminary Configuration Phase]
```

### 4.2 Multi-Criteria Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Aerodynamic Block] --> B5[Weighted Aggregation Engine]
    B2[Propulsion Block] --> B5
    B3[Structural Block] --> B5
    B4[Operational Block] --> B5
    B5 --> B6[Figure of Merit FOM]
    B6 --> B7[Configuration Selection]
    B6 --> B8[Trade Off Documentation]
    B7 --> B9[Final Aircraft Configuration]
    B8 --> B10[KTU Board Examiner Rubric]
```

### 4.3 Configuration Trade Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Wing Trade Study] --> C5[Master Configuration Matrix]
    C2[Tail Trade Study] --> C5
    C3[Engine Placement Study] --> C5
    C4[Landing Gear Study] --> C5
    C5 --> C6[Integrated Configuration FOM]
    C6 --> C7[Final Baseline Aircraft Layout]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define the term "Figure of Merit" as used in aircraft configuration trade studies. State the condition for the weighting factors used in its calculation.**

**Model Answer:**
The Figure of Merit (FOM) is a dimensionless numerical index that quantifies the overall desirability of a candidate aircraft configuration relative to competing alternatives, computed as the weighted sum of normalized performance scores across all evaluation criteria. Mathematically, $\text{FOM}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N} w_j \cdot s_{ij}$. The essential condition on the weights is that they must satisfy the **normalization constraint** $\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_j = 1$, ensuring each weight represents a fractional contribution to the total, and each weight must be non-negative ($w_j \geq 0$).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] List any three hard constraints typically applied in the Tier-1 screening of aircraft configurations.**

**Model Answer:**
Three hard constraints used in Tier-1 screening are:
1. **Payload capacity** — must meet or exceed the specified mission payload (e.g., $\geq 1500$ kg).
2. **Range requirement** — must meet or exceed the design mission range (e.g., $\geq 2500$ nm).
3. **Service ceiling** — must reach the operational altitude requirement (e.g., $\geq 35000$ ft).

Any configuration failing even one hard constraint is eliminated from the trade study without further evaluation.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024] — Choice A**

> *An aircraft design team is evaluating three candidate configurations for a small commuter aircraft. The configurations are differentiated primarily by wing arrangement (high-wing, mid-wing, low-wing). Using the Weighted Figure of Merit method, perform a complete configuration trade study.*

**Evaluation Criteria and Weights:**

| Criterion | Weight | High Wing | Mid Wing | Low Wing | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cruise L/D | 0.35 | 15.5 | 16.0 | 14.8 | Higher better |
| Empty Weight Fraction | 0.25 | 0.62 | 0.60 | 0.65 | Lower better |
| Passenger Visibility Index | 0.20 | 9 | 6 | 5 | Higher better |
| Manufacturing Complexity Score | 0.20 | 3 | 6 | 8 | Lower better |

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Perform the normalization and compute the FOM for each configuration.

**Step 1 — Verify weight constraint:** $0.35 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 1.00$ ✓

**Step 2 — Normalize Cruise L/D (Higher better):**
- $X_{\max} = 16.0$, $X_{\min} = 14.8$
- High: $(15.5 - 14.8)/(16.0 - 14.8) = 0.7/1.2 = 0.583$
- Mid: $(16.0 - 14.8)/(16.0 - 14.8) = 1.200/1.200 = 1.000$
- Low: $(14.8 - 14.8)/(16.0 - 14.8) = 0.000$

**Step 3 — Normalize Empty Weight Fraction (Lower better):**
- $X_{\max} = 0.65$, $X_{\min} = 0.60$
- High: $(0.65 - 0.62)/(0.65 - 0.60) = 0.600$
- Mid: $(0.65 - 0.60)/(0.65 - 0.60) = 1.000$
- Low: $(0.65 - 0.65)/(0.65 - 0.60) = 0.000$

**Step 4 — Normalize Passenger Visibility (Higher better):**
- $X_{\max} = 9$, $X_{\min} = 5$
- High: $(9 - 5)/(9 - 5) = 1.000$
- Mid: $(6 - 5)/(9 - 5) = 0.250$
- Low: $(5 - 5)/(9 - 5) = 0.000$

**Step 5 — Normalize Manufacturing Complexity (Lower better):**
- $X_{\max} = 8$, $X_{\min} = 3$
- High: $(8 - 3)/(8 - 3) = 1.000$
- Mid: $(8 - 6)/(8 - 3) = 0.400$
- Low: $(8 - 8)/(8 - 3) = 0.000$

**Step 6 — Compute FOM:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FOM}_{\text{High}} &= (0.35 \times 0.583) + (0.25 \times 0.600) + (0.20 \times 1.000) + (0.20 \times 1.000) \\
&= 0.204 + 0.150 + 0.200 + 0.200 = 0.754
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FOM}_{\text{Mid}} &= (0.35 \times 1.000) + (0.25 \times 1.000) + (0.20 \times 0.250) + (0.20 \times 0.400) \\
&= 0.350 + 0.250 + 0.050 + 0.080 = 0.730
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FOM}_{\text{Low}} &= (0.35 \times 0.000) + (0.25 \times 0.000) + (0.20 \times 0.000) + (0.20 \times 0.000) \\
&= 0.000
\end{aligned}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Recommend the winning configuration and justify with sensitivity analysis.

**Recommendation:** The **High-Wing configuration** wins with $\text{FOM} = 0.754$.

**Justification:**
1. Although the mid-wing has marginally superior L/D, the high-wing offers unmatched passenger visibility and a simpler manufacturing integration with the fuselage.
2. The low-wing is eliminated entirely due to cascading penalties in all four criteria.

**Sensitivity Analysis** (perturbing the L/D weight by ±30%):

| Scenario | $w_{\text{L/D}}$ | High FOM | Mid FOM | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Original | 0.35 | 0.754 | 0.730 | High |
| L/D dominant | 0.50 | 0.792 | 0.860 | **Mid** |
| L/D de-emphasized | 0.20 | 0.716 | 0.600 | High |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** When L/D weight increases beyond ~0.45, the mid-wing becomes the winner. KTU examiners will deduct 2 marks if you do NOT comment on this robustness limit. Always state: *"The selection is robust for L/D weights below 0.42; otherwise, sensitivity reversal occurs."*

**Conclusion:** The high-wing configuration is the recommended baseline, but a sensitivity reversal point exists. The design team should confirm the L/D weight with the mission sponsor before final freeze.

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024] — Choice B (Alternative)**

> *A design team must choose between three engine installation philosophies for a twin-engine fighter trainer: Configuration P (both engines in fuselage, side-by-side), Configuration Q (wing-mounted nacelles), and Configuration R (one dorsal, one ventral). Apply the Weighted Figure of Merit method to recommend the optimum.*

**Data Table Provided to Student:**

| Criterion | Weight $w_j$ | Config P | Config Q | Config R | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Specific Excess Power (ft/s) | 0.30 | 280 | 320 | 295 | Higher better |
| Single Engine Climb Gradient (%) | 0.25 | 2.5 | 3.8 | 4.2 | Higher better |
| Maintenance Access Time (hrs) | 0.20 | 8.0 | 4.5 | 9.5 | Lower better |
| Radar Cross Section Penalty | 0.15 | 1.0 | 1.6 | 1.3 | Lower better |
| Cost Index (M\$) | 0.10 | 12.0 | 10.5 | 13.0 | Lower better |

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Normalize and compute FOM for each configuration.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Normalize Specific Excess Power (Higher better):**
- $X_{\max} = 320$, $X_{\min} = 280$
- P: $(280 - 280)/40 = 0.000$
- Q: $(320 - 280)/40 = 1.000$
- R: $(295 - 280)/40 = 0.375$

**Step 2 — Normalize Single Engine Climb (Higher better):**
- $X_{\max} = 4.2$, $X_{\min} = 2.5$
- P: $(2.5 - 2.5)/1.7 = 0.000$
- Q: $(3.8 - 2.5)/1.7 = 0.765$
- R: $(4.2 - 2.5)/1.7 = 1.000$

**Step 3 — Normalize Maintenance Time (Lower better):**
- $X_{\max} = 9.5$, $X_{\min} = 4.5$
- P: $(9.5 - 8.0)/5.0 = 0.300$
- Q: $(9.5 - 4.5)/5.0 = 1.000$
- R: $(9.5 - 9.5)/5.0 = 0.000$

**Step 4 — Normalize RCS Penalty (Lower better):**
- $X_{\max} = 1.6$, $X_{\min} = 1.0$
- P: $(1.6 - 1.0)/0.6 = 1.000$
- Q: $(1.6 - 1.6)/0.6 = 0.000$
- R: $(1.6 - 1.3)/0.6 = 0.500$

**Step 5 — Normalize Cost (Lower better):**
- $X_{\max} = 13.0$, $X_{\min} = 10.5$
- P: $(13.0 - 12.0)/2.5 = 0.400$
- Q: $(13.0 - 10.5)/2.5 = 1.000$
- R: $(13.0 - 13.0)/2.5 = 0.000$

**Step 6 — Compute FOM:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FOM}_P &= (0.30)(0.000) + (0.25)(0.000) + (0.20)(0.300) + (0.15)(1.000) + (0.10)(0.400) \\
&= 0.000 + 0.000 + 0.060 + 0.150 + 0.040 = 0.250
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FOM}_Q &= (0.30)(1.000) + (0.25)(0.765) + (0.20)(1.000) + (0.15)(0.000) + (0.10)(1.000) \\
&= 0.300 + 0.191 + 0.200 + 0.000 + 0.100 = 0.791
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FOM}_R &= (0.30)(0.375) + (0.25)(1.000) + (0.20)(0.000) + (0.15)(0.500) + (0.10)(0.000) \\
&= 0.113 + 0.250 + 0.000 + 0.075 + 0.000 = 0.438
\end{aligned}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Identify the winner and discuss the safety-of-flight implications.

**Ranking:**

| Configuration | FOM | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| Wing-Mounted Nacelles (Q) | **0.791** | **1st** ✓ |
| Dorsal-Ventral (R) | 0.438 | 2nd |
| Side-by-Side Fuselage (P) | 0.250 | 3rd |

**Recommendation:** Configuration **Q (Wing-Mounted Nacelles)** is selected.

**Safety-of-Flight Discussion:**

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** A common KTU marking error is recommending Configuration Q without addressing its **single-engine yaw asymmetry risk**. The ventral/dorsal Configuration R has superior safety because engine failure leaves the thrust line near the centerline, reducing asymmetric thrust moment. Configuration Q has the wing nacelle engine pulling the nose away from the dead engine, requiring large rudder deflection. You **must** mention:
> 1. The yaw moment arm $\ell_V$ is greatest for wing-mounted configurations.
> 2. The vertical tail volume coefficient $\bar{V}_V$ must be sized to counter this.
> 3. With $\bar{V}_V \geq 0.06$ (a documented FAR Part 23 minimum), Configuration Q remains certifiable, but the **maintenance accessibility advantage** is decisive for a trainer aircraft.

**Conclusion:** Configuration Q is selected. Configuration R is kept as a **safety backup baseline** in case further studies reveal unacceptable asymmetric thrust characteristics for trainee pilots.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- **Definition** — CTS is a structured MCDA-based selection of the optimum aircraft configuration using the Weighted Figure of Merit.
- **Three Tiers** — (1) Hard constraint screening, (2) Weighted scoring matrix, (3) Sensitivity analysis.
- **FOM Formula** — $\text{FOM}_i = \sum w_j \cdot s_{ij}$ where $\sum w_j = 1$.
- **Normalization** — Always convert raw values to $[0,1]$ before scoring. Identify whether "higher better" or "lower better".
- **Breguet Range** — Use $R = (V/c_T)(L/D)\ln(W_1/W_0)$ for range-based scoring.
- **Tail Volume Coefficients** — $\bar{V}_H = \ell_H S_H / (c \cdot S)$ and $\bar{V}_V = \ell_V S_V / (b \cdot S)$.
- **Sensitivity Analysis** — Always required to confirm selection robustness. State the perturbation percentage explicitly.
- **Pitfall #1** — Forgetting to normalize (common 2-mark deduction).
- **Pitfall #2** — Weights not summing to 1 (immediate invalidation of the FOM).
- **Pitfall #3** — Selecting a winner without safety-of-flight discussion on thrust asymmetry, single-engine handling, or flutter margin.
- **Pitfall #4** — Mixing up "higher better" and "lower better" normalization signs.
- **Pitfall #5** — Failing to comment on the **robustness reversal point** during sensitivity analysis.
- **Examiner Pattern** — KTU 14-mark questions almost always have a (a)+(b) split with one part numerical and one part justification. Practice BOTH.

> **END OF NOTES — CONFIGURATION TRADE STUDIES**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Configuration Types (Tailless, Canard, Conventional)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET865 - AIRCRAFT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Configuration Types (Tailless, Canard, Conventional) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of Aircraft Configuration In KTU 2024 Scheme Aircraft Design (PEMET865), **Configuration** refers to the geometric and functional arrangement of the primary lifting, stabilizing, and control surfaces of an aircraft relative to its fuselage and center of gravity (c.g.). Module 4 deals with the **Conceptual Design Phase**, where the configuration choice is one of the earliest and most consequential decisions. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4):** > Aircraft configuration is defined as the spatial arrangement of wings, fuselage, empennage, and control surfaces to satisfy prescribed mission requirements, stability, control, performance, and structural goals. ## 1.2 The Three Primary Configurations ### 1.2.1 Conventional Configuration A **conventional (or "Normal")** aircraft has a main wing, a fuselage, a vertical tail (fin + rudder), and a **rear-mounted horizontal tail** (stabilator or stabilizer-elevator). The horizontal tail sits *aft* (behind) of the main wing. **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a paper airplane you throw — the small rear flaps act exactly like a conventional horizontal tail, providing **push-up** or **push-down** force to keep the nose pointed correctly through the air. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examples:** Boeing 737, Airbus A320, Cessna 172, F-22 Raptor, HAL Tejas (Mk1) — these are *all* conventional configurations. ### 1.2.2 Canard Configuration The word **canard** is French for "duck." A canard aircraft has a **forward-mounted horizontal surface (foreplane)** placed *ahead* of the main wing, while the rear of the fuselage has **no horizontal tail**. The foreplane provides pitch control and contributes to lift. **Real-World Analogy:** Picture a goose in flight — its small front wings (the *canard* surface) are pitched downward slightly, lifting the front, while the larger main wings carry most of the load. The duck is the same idea. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examples:** Rutan Voyager (first non-stop, non-refueled around-the-world flight), Beechcraft Starship, Dassault Rafale, Eurofighter Typhoon, HAL Tejas (Mk2), SAAB Gripen. ### 1.2.3 Tailless Configuration A **tailless** aircraft has **no horizontal stabilizer at all**. Pitch and roll control is achieved by combining the functions of multiple surfaces (called **elevons**, **ruddervators**, or **all-moving tips**) integrated into the main wing or its trailing edge. **Real-World Analogy:** A **flying paper dart** (with no tail feathers) — the entire wing must do the job of trimming, controlling, and stabilizing. The aircraft is essentially a *flying wing*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examples:** Northrop B-2 Spirit stealth bomber, Horten Ho 229 (WWII), Lockheed F-117 Nighthawk, Northrop Grumman X-47B, blended wing body (BWB) concepts. ## 1.3 Comparison at a Glance | Configuration | Horizontal Surface Location | Longitudinal Stability Source | Pitch Control Surface | |---|---|---|---| | **Conventional** | Aft of main wing | Tail downloads (or uploads) | Elevator on horizontal tail | | **Canard** | Forward of main wing | Canard downloads + wing effects | Elevator/elevon on foreplane | | **Tailless** | None (eliminated) | Wing reflex, sweep, twist | Elevons, drag rudders, all-moving tips | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Metric for KTU Exams:** The choice of configuration directly affects the **Static Margin (SM)** and **Neutral Point (NP)** location — these are the two stability parameters a designer computes first. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Underlying Physics: Where the Lift Acts Every aircraft must satisfy **two simultaneous demands**: 1. **Lift = Weight** (so the aircraft does not fall). 2. **Pitching Moment about c.g. = 0** (so the aircraft does not rotate uncontrollably). The aerodynamic center of pressure of the main wing is *typically* behind the c.g. (producing a **nose-down** pitching moment). To balance this, a horizontal surface is placed to produce a counteracting moment. The choice of **where** to place this counteracting surface defines the configuration. ## 2.2 Conventional Configuration — Deep Analysis In a conventional aircraft, the **horizontal tail produces DOWNLOAD** (lift in the *negative* direction) at high angles of attack to keep the nose trimmed. - **Wing lift (L_w):** acts at the wing's aerodynamic center (AC_w), located at ~25% MAC for a subsonic wing. - **Tail lift (L_t):** acts at the tail's aerodynamic center (AC_t), located at the tail's quarter-chord, at a distance **l_t** behind the wing AC. - **Tail volume coefficient:** $\eta_t = \dfrac{l_t \cdot S_t}{c \cdot S_w}$ — this is THE most important non-dimensional number for sizing the tail. The **total pitching moment about c.g.** is: $$C_{m_{cg}} = C_{m_{ac,w}} + C_{L_w} \cdot \left( \dfrac{x_{cg} - x_{ac,w}}{c} \right) - \eta_t \cdot C_{L_t}$$ where each term has a specific aerodynamic meaning. > [!NOTE] > **Why a download on the tail?** > At positive $C_L$ (cruise), the wing alone produces a **nose-down** moment about the c.g. The tail must push **down** to balance. This creates **induced drag** (trim drag), reducing aerodynamic efficiency by **5–15%** in well-designed aircraft and up to **30%** in poor ones. ## 2.3 Canard Configuration — Deep Analysis In a canard, the foreplane is at distance **l_c** *ahead* of the main wing. Crucially, the foreplane produces **UPLIFT** (positive lift) when the wing is also producing positive lift. - **Foreplane lift (L_c):** acts at AC_c, located at **l_c** *forward* of the wing AC. - **Wing lift (L_w):** acts at AC_w. - **Canard volume coefficient:** $\eta_c = \dfrac{l_c \cdot S_c}{c \cdot S_w}$ The trim equation is: $$C_{m_{cg}} = C_{m_{ac,w}} + C_{L_w} \cdot \left( \dfrac{x_{cg} - x_{ac,w}}{c} \right) + \eta_c \cdot C_{L_c} \cdot \left( \dfrac{x_{cg} - x_{ac_c}}{c} \right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Advantage of Canard:** The canard can be designed to *stall before* the main wing. This means at high AoA, the canard loses lift first, the **nose drops**, and the wing is protected from stall — a built-in safety feature called **"canard stall protection."** ## 2.4 Tailless Configuration — Deep Analysis No tail exists. Stability and control are achieved through: - **Wing planform geometry** — sweep, taper, twist, and **reflex** (a kink in the trailing edge that creates a download region near the tip). - **Elevons** — control surfaces on the wing trailing edge that combine elevator and aileron functions. - **Ruddervators** (V-tail) — combines rudder and elevator. - **Drag rudders / spoilers** — for yaw control when vertical tail area is also reduced (e.g., B-2). The **pitching moment coefficient** for a flying wing is: $$C_m = C_{m_{ac}} + C_L \cdot \left( \dfrac{x_{cg} - x_{ac}}{c} \right)$$ with **no tail term**. Stability is achieved by careful aerodynamic shaping of the wing planform (e.g., sweep, washout). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Tailless aircraft have **very tight** center-of-gravity tolerances — moving c.g. by a few percent of MAC can flip the aircraft from *stable* to *unstable*. Designing tailless aircraft is the hardest configuration to balance. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Symbol | Meaning | Formula | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\eta_t$ | Tail volume coefficient (conventional) | $\eta_t = \dfrac{l_t \cdot S_t}{c \cdot S_w}$ | dimensionless | | 2 | $\eta_c$ | Canard volume coefficient | $\eta_c = \dfrac{l_c \cdot S_c}{c \cdot S_w}$ | dimensionless | | 3 | $h_{ac}$ | Aerodynamic center location | $h_{ac} = \dfrac{x_{ac} - x_{cg}}{c}$ | dimensionless | | 4 | $h_n$ | Neutral point location | $h_n = \dfrac{x_{n} - x_{cg}}{c}$ | dimensionless | | 5 | SM | Static Margin | $SM = h_n - h_{cg} = h_{ac} - h_{cg} - \dfrac{dC_m/dC_L}{1}$ | dimensionless | | 6 | $C_{m_{cg}}$ | Pitching moment about c.g. | $C_{m_{cg}} = C_{m_{ac}} + C_L \cdot h_{cg}$ | dimensionless | | 7 | $C_{L_t}$ | Tail lift coefficient (conventional) | $C_{L_t} = a_t \cdot \alpha_t - \eta \cdot \dfrac{S_w}{S_t} \cdot C_{L_w}$ | dimensionless | | 8 | AR | Aspect Ratio | $AR = \dfrac{b^2}{S}$ | dimensionless | | 9 | $a$ | Lift curve slope (2D) | $a = 2\pi$ (thin airfoil, subsonic) | per radian | | 10 | $a_{3D}$ | Lift curve slope (finite wing) | $a_{3D} = \dfrac{a}{1 + \dfrac{a}{\pi \cdot AR \cdot e}}$ | per radian | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard KTU Design Value:** For **static stability**, the **Static Margin SM = 5% to 15% of MAC** for a transport aircraft. Below 5% → too unstable for safe recovery; above 15% → heavy, sluggish controls. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility The configuration choice is a **systems-engineering** decision. It trades off: - **Performance** (canards and tailless have less trim drag → higher L/D). - **Stability** (conventional is naturally stable, tailless is hardest). - **Stealth** (tailless = no tail surfaces to reflect radar → used in B-2, F-117). - **Manufacturing cost** (conventional is simplest; tailless needs tight tolerances). - **Pilot workload** (conventional is forgiving; tailless requires fly-by-wire). - **Payload / Volume** (canard and tailless free up rear fuselage for cargo or weapons). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Pitching Moment Equation for a Conventional Aircraft ### Step 1 — Free-Body Setup Consider an aircraft in steady, level flight with the following forces and moments: - Wing lift $L_w$ acting at AC_w, located at distance $x_{ac,w}$ from datum. - Tail lift $L_t$ acting at AC_t, located at $x_{ac,t}$. - Weight $W$ acting at the c.g., located at $x_{cg}$. - Pitching moment $M$ taken about the c.g. (positive nose-up by convention). The moment arm of the wing lift about c.g. is $(x_{ac,w} - x_{cg})$ and that of the tail lift is $(x_{ac,t} - x_{cg})$. ### Step 2 — Write the Moment Equation Taking moments about the c.g., with nose-up positive: $$M = L_w (x_{cg} - x_{ac,w}) + L_t (x_{cg} - x_{ac,t})$$ ### Step 3 — Convert to Coefficients Divide through by dynamic pressure $q_\infty$ and reference area $S_w$ times reference chord $c$: $$C_m = \frac{M}{q_\infty S_w c}$$ $$C_m = C_{L_w} \cdot \frac{x_{cg} - x_{ac,w}}{c} + C_{L_t} \cdot \frac{S_t}{S_w} \cdot \frac{x_{cg} - x_{ac,t}}{c}$$ ### Step 4 — Introduce the Tail Volume Coefficient Define the **tail volume coefficient** $\eta_t$ and a related nondimensional arm: $$\eta_t = \frac{l_t S_t}{c S_w}, \quad l_t = x_{ac,t} - x_{ac,w}$$ After algebraic rearrangement (and assuming $x_{ac,t} \gg x_{cg}$): $$C_{m_{cg}} = C_{m_{ac,w}} + C_{L_w} \left( \frac{x_{cg} - x_{ac,w}}{c} \right) - \eta_t \cdot C_{L_t}$$ The negative sign on the tail term indicates the tail produces a **restoring moment** for positive $C_L$ — the very source of longitudinal stability in conventional aircraft. ### Step 5 — Condition for Trim (Longitudinal Moment Equilibrium) For trimmed flight, $C_{m_{cg}} = 0$: $$C_{m_{ac,w}} + C_{L_w} \left( \frac{x_{cg} - x_{ac,w}}{c} \right) - \eta_t \cdot C_{L_t} = 0$$ Solving for the required tail lift coefficient: $$C_{L_t} = \frac{1}{\eta_t} \left[ C_{m_{ac,w}} + C_{L_w} \left( \frac{x_{cg} - x_{ac,w}}{c} \right) \right]$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **equation a KTU examiner will expect** in a 14-mark problem on "tail sizing for trim." ## 3.2 Derivation: Static Margin and Neutral Point ### Step 1 — Definition of Static Margin The static margin (SM) is the **percentage of mean aerodynamic chord** by which the c.g. is *ahead* of the neutral point: $$SM = \frac{x_{n} - x_{cg}}{c}$$ A positive SM ⇒ aircraft is **statically stable**. A negative SM ⇒ aircraft is **statically unstable** (fighter aircraft with relaxed static stability are deliberately designed this way for agility). ### Step 2 — Neutral Point Definition The **neutral point (NP)** is the c.g. location at which the aircraft is **neutrally stable**, i.e., a small change in $C_L$ produces **zero** change in pitching moment. Mathematically: $$\left. \frac{dC_m}{dC_L} \right|_{cg=x_n} = 0$$ ### Step 3 — Derivative of $C_{m_{cg}}$ w.r.t. $C_L$ Starting from the conventional trim equation (with $C_{m_{ac,w}}$ and $(x_{cg} - x_{ac,w})/c$ assumed constant for a fixed c.g.): $$\frac{dC_{m_{cg}}}{dC_L} = \left( \frac{x_{cg} - x_{ac,w}}{c} \right) - \eta_t \cdot \frac{dC_{L_t}}{dC_L}$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for the Neutral Point Setting the derivative to zero: $$\left( \frac{x_{cg} - x_{ac,w}}{c} \right) = \eta_t \cdot \frac{dC_{L_t}}{dC_L}$$ $$\frac{x_n - x_{ac,w}}{c} = \eta_t \cdot \frac{dC_{L_t}}{dC_L}$$ This is the **fundamental stability sizing equation** for any KTU exam question on tail sizing: $$\frac{x_n - x_{ac,w}}{c} = \eta_t \cdot a_t \cdot \left( 1 - \frac{d\varepsilon}{d\alpha} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{a_{3D}}$$ where $d\varepsilon/d\alpha$ accounts for downwash from the wing on the tail. ## 3.3 Derivation: Configuration Tradeoff — Trim Drag Comparison ### Step 1 — Induced Drag Coefficient For a finite wing (or surface): $$C_{D_i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi \cdot AR \cdot e}$$ ### Step 2 — Total Lift Coefficient in Conventional vs. Canard In a **conventional** aircraft producing a total lift equal to weight: $$C_L = C_{L_w} - C_{L_t} \cdot \dfrac{S_t}{S_w} = C_{L_w} (1 - \eta_t \cdot a_t / a_{3D})$$ In a **canard** aircraft: $$C_L = C_{L_w} + C_{L_c} \cdot \dfrac{S_c}{S_w}$$ ### Step 3 — Key Numerical Insight A conventional aircraft in cruise typically has $C_{L_t}/C_{L_w} \approx 0.05$ to $0.15$ — meaning 5–15% of the wing's lift is "wasted" balancing the tail. This lost lift corresponds to a **trim-drag penalty**: $$\frac{\Delta C_D}{C_D} \approx 2 \cdot \frac{C_{L_t}}{C_L} \cdot \frac{S_t/S_w}{1} \approx 0.10 \text{ to } 0.20$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > A canard avoids this by using *both* wing and foreplane for *positive* lift. This is why high-endurance unmanned aircraft (e.g., Rutan Voyager) and modern fighters (Rafale, Tejas Mk2) prefer canards. ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix | Design Criterion | Conventional | Canard | Tailless | |---|---|---|---| | **Stability** | Naturally stable (best) | Marginally stable (must be checked) | Hardest — requires careful shaping | | **Trim drag** | High (5–15%) | Low (both surfaces lift up) | Lowest (no tail download) | | **Stall safety** | Tail stalls first (nose drops) | Canard stalls first (nose drops) | Wing tip stall = roll-off risk | | **Stealth / RCS** | Poor (tail surfaces reflect radar) | Better (smaller foreplane) | Best (no tail at all) | | **Cargo / Volume** | Tail restricts rear access | Rear free | Rear free | | **Pilot workload** | Low (forgiving) | Moderate | High (needs fly-by-wire) | | **Manufacturing cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest (tight tolerances) | | **Famous example** | Boeing 747 | Rutan Voyager | Northrop B-2 Spirit | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Configuration Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start Mission Requirement Analysis] --> B{Stealth Required?} B -- Yes --> C{Tail Surfaces Allowed?} B -- No --> D{Passenger / Cargo Volume Priority?} C -- No --> E[Choose TAILLESS Configuration] C -- Yes --> F[Choose CONVENTIONAL with Stealth Shaping] D -- High --> G{Canard vs. Conventional Decision} D -- Low --> H[Choose CONVENTIONAL Configuration] G --> I{Performance vs. Simplicity?} I -- Performance --> J[Choose CANARD Configuration] I -- Simplicity --> H E --> K[Output: Final Configuration] F --> K H --> K J --> K K --> L[Proceed to Sizing and Layout Phase] ``` ## 4.2 Aerodynamic Force Block Diagram — Conventional Aircraft ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph AeroForces["Aerodynamic Forces and Moments"] Lw[Wing Lift Lw
Acts at AC wing
Uplift] Lt[Tail Lift Lt
Acts at AC tail
DOWNLOAD] Mw[Wing Pitching Moment
Nose DOWN] Mt[Tail Pitching Moment
Nose UP] end subgraph Resultant["Resultant on Aircraft"] Ltotal[Total Lift L total] Mcg[Net Pitching Moment about CG] end Lw --> Ltotal Lt --> Ltotal Mw --> Mcg Mt --> Mcg Mcg -->|Trim Condition| Q[Mcg equals ZERO at Cruise] Ltotal -->|Equilibrium| R[L total equals WEIGHT] ``` ## 4.3 Configuration Comparison Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Conventional["CONVENTIONAL AIRCRAFT"] C1[Main Wing generates lift] --> C2[Tail produces DOWNLOAD] C2 --> C3[Trim: nose-down moment balanced by tail push] end subgraph Canard["CANARD AIRCRAFT"] K1[Canard surface ahead of c.g.] --> K2[Canard produces UPLIFT] K2 --> K3[Main wing produces UPLIFT] K3 --> K4[Trim: both surfaces lift up together] end subgraph Tailless["TAILLESS AIRCRAFT"] T1[No horizontal surface exists] --> T2[Wing planform provides stability] T2 --> T3[Reflex trailing edge + sweep] T3 --> T4[Elevons provide pitch and roll control] end Conventional --> Compare[Configuration Tradeoff Analysis] Canard --> Compare Tailless --> Compare ``` ## 4.4 Pitching-Moment Diagram — Stick-Fixed Stability ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Angle of attack increases] --> A2[Wing lift CL increases] A2 --> A3[Wing nose-down moment grows] A3 --> A4{Configuration} A4 -- Conventional --> A5[Tail lift auto increases via downwash] A5 --> A6[Nose-up tail moment restores equilibrium] A4 -- Canard --> A7[Canard lift auto increases] A7 --> A8[Nose-down canard moment restores equilibrium] A4 -- Tailless --> A9[Reflex wing twisting generates restoring moment] A9 --> A10[Passive aerodynamic stability] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between a Conventional and a Canard configuration. Mention two advantages of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Conventional | Canard | |---|---|---| | Horizontal surface location | Aft of main wing | Forward of main wing | | Tail contribution to lift | DOWNLOAD (negative lift) | UPLOAD (positive lift) | | Trim drag | High (5–15% loss) | Low (both surfaces add lift) | | Stall behavior | Tail stalls first → nose drops, recovery | Canard stalls first → nose drops, wing protected | **Advantages of Conventional:** Naturally stable; simple manufacturing; forgiving pilot handling. **Advantages of Canard:** Lower trim drag; better high-AoA safety; frees rear fuselage. *(Examiner awards 1 mark for differentiation, 1 mark for conventional advantages, 1 mark for canard advantages.)* ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is a Tailless aircraft? Explain how longitudinal stability is achieved without a horizontal tail.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **tailless aircraft** is one in which the **horizontal stabilizer and elevator are completely removed**, and all pitch, roll, and yaw control functions are integrated into the main wing, vertical surfaces, or a combination thereof. Longitudinal stability is achieved through: 1. **Reflexed trailing edge** — a kink in the inboard trailing edge that creates a small download region to balance the wing's nose-down pitching moment. 2. **Wing twist (washout)** — a reduction in geometric incidence toward the tip, delaying tip stall. 3. **Sweep angle** — shifted aerodynamic center toward the c.g. for natural stability. 4. **Fly-by-wire (FBW)** augmentation — modern tailless aircraft (B-2, X-47B) use FBW to artificially stabilize an otherwise neutrally stable airframe. *(Examiner awards 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four mechanisms listed.)* ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern 2024:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students answer EITHER Q-A OR Q-B. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** For a conventional aircraft, derive the **trim equation for the pitching moment about the c.g.** clearly showing how the tail volume coefficient $\eta_t$ enters the equation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An aircraft has the following data: - Wing area $S_w = 30 \text{ m}^2$, Mean Aerodynamic Chord $c = 2.5 \text{ m}$, $C_{m_{ac,w}} = -0.05$ - Tail area $S_t = 6 \text{ m}^2$, Tail moment arm $l_t = 7.0 \text{ m}$ - Cruising lift coefficient $C_L = 0.5$ - 3D lift curve slope $a_{3D} = 5.0 \text{ /rad}$, $a_t = 4.5 \text{ /rad}$, $d\varepsilon/d\alpha = 0.3$ **Calculate:** (i) The tail volume coefficient $\eta_t$. (ii) The tail lift coefficient $C_{L_t}$ required for trim. (iii) The static margin (SM), given c.g. is at 30% MAC and the aerodynamic center of the wing is at 25% MAC. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) Starting from moment equilibrium about the c.g., with positive nose-up: $$M = L_w(x_{cg} - x_{ac,w}) + L_t(x_{cg} - x_{ac,t}) + M_{ac,w}$$ Dividing by $q_\infty S_w c$: $$C_{m_{cg}} = C_{m_{ac,w}} + C_{L_w}\left(\frac{x_{cg} - x_{ac,w}}{c}\right) + C_{L_t}\cdot\frac{S_t}{S_w}\cdot\left(\frac{x_{cg} - x_{ac,t}}{c}\right)$$ Defining tail volume coefficient: $$\eta_t = \frac{l_t \cdot S_t}{c \cdot S_w}, \quad l_t = x_{ac,t} - x_{ac,w}$$ The trim equation becomes: $$\boxed{C_{m_{cg}} = C_{m_{ac,w}} + C_{L_w}\left(\frac{x_{cg} - x_{ac,w}}{c}\right) - \eta_t \cdot C_{L_t}}$$ The negative sign on the tail term reflects the standard convention that positive $C_{L_t}$ corresponds to a nose-down moment when the tail is aft of the c.g. *(7 marks — full derivation with explanation of every term)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) **(i) Tail Volume Coefficient:** $$\eta_t = \frac{l_t \cdot S_t}{c \cdot S_w} = \frac{7.0 \times 6.0}{2.5 \times 30} = \frac{42.0}{75.0} = \boxed{0.56}$$ **(ii) Tail Lift Coefficient for Trim:** First, the tail lift slope accounting for wing downwash: $$a_{t,eff} = a_t \left(1 - \frac{d\varepsilon}{d\alpha}\right) = 4.5 \times (1 - 0.3) = 3.15 \text{ /rad}$$ Assuming $C_{L_w} \approx C_L = 0.5$ (small tail effect) and using the trim equation with $x_{cg} = 0.30c$, $x_{ac,w} = 0.25c$: $$0 = C_{m_{ac,w}} + C_{L_w}\left(\frac{x_{cg} - x_{ac,w}}{c}\right) - \eta_t \cdot C_{L_t}$$ $$0 = -0.05 + 0.5 \times (0.30 - 0.25) - 0.56 \times C_{L_t}$$ $$0 = -0.05 + 0.025 - 0.56 \cdot C_{L_t}$$ $$C_{L_t} = \frac{-0.025}{-0.56} = \boxed{+0.0446}$$ The positive $C_{L_t}$ confirms a **small DOWNLOAD** on the tail, exactly as expected for a conventional configuration in cruise. **(iii) Static Margin:** The 3D neutral point location: $$\frac{x_n - x_{ac,w}}{c} = \eta_t \cdot a_{t,eff} / a_{3D} = 0.56 \times \frac{3.15}{5.0} = 0.3528$$ So $x_n = (0.25 + 0.3528)c = 0.6028c$ → c.g. is **well ahead** of NP. $$\boxed{SM = \frac{x_n - x_{cg}}{c} = 0.6028 - 0.30 = 0.3028 \text{ or } 30.28\%}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** A static margin of **30% is excessive** — the aircraft will be **sluggish and require large control deflections** to maneuver. The tail would need to be reduced in size (lower $\eta_t$) to bring SM into the 5–15% range. Award full marks for the calculation but mention this design critique for top marks. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **aerodynamic principle of the Canard configuration**. Why is the canard said to have an advantage in **stall behavior**? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the trim drag penalty in a conventional, canard, and tailless configuration for an aircraft with the following data: - Cruise $C_L = 0.4$, Total lift = Weight - Conventional: $\eta_t = 0.50$, $C_{L_t}/C_{L_w} = 0.08$ - Canard: $\eta_c = 0.40$, foreplane lift accounts for **15% of total lift** - Tailless: No tail, all lift from wing → no trim drag Calculate the **trim-drag ratio** $\Delta C_{D_i}/C_{D_i}$ for each configuration and rank them. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Aerodynamic Principle:** In a canard configuration, the **horizontal control surface (foreplane)** is located **forward of the main wing** and the c.g. The foreplane operates at a **higher local angle of attack** than the main wing (typically 3° to 5° higher). It produces **positive lift** in the same direction as the main wing. The pitching-moment equation for a canard is: $$C_{m_{cg}} = C_{m_{ac,w}} + C_{L_w} \cdot h_{cg,w} + \eta_c \cdot C_{L_c} \cdot h_{cg,c}$$ The **foreplane provides the nose-up pitching moment** required to balance the wing's nose-down moment — but it does so by producing *uplift*, not download. This is the fundamental aerodynamic advantage. **Stall Behavior Advantage:** The canard surface is designed to have a **lower stall AoA** than the main wing. This is achieved by: 1. Using a thinner, sharper-leading-edge airfoil on the canard. 2.
