# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Asymmetric Yawing Moment Coefficient

We derive the **non-dimensional yawing moment coefficient** $C_n$ produced by an inoperative engine on a multi-engine aircraft.

### Step 1 — Identify the acting force and arm

When engine $k$ fails, the operative engines produce thrust $T_i$ each. The failed engine contributes zero thrust but its line of action still defines a moment arm $y_k$ from the CG.

The yawing moment about the CG due to asymmetric thrust is:

$$
N = \sum_{i \neq k} T_i \cdot y_i
$$

where the summation is over all **still-operating** engines and $y_i$ is the **lateral distance** of engine $i$ from the CG.

### Step 2 — Symmetric simplification

For a **symmetric twin-engine** aircraft (one engine on each wing, equidistant from the CG at lateral arm $y_e$), the only operating engine after one fails produces a **net yawing moment** of:

$$
N = T_e \cdot y_e
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Because the other engine has failed, it contributes $T=0$ and hence zero moment. The remaining engine's full thrust $T_e$ acts at the full lateral arm $y_e$.

### Step 3 — Non-dimensionalize

Non-dimensionalize $N$ by dynamic pressure $q$, wing area $S$, and wingspan $b$:

$$
C_n = \frac{N}{q \, S \, b} = \frac{T_e \, y_e}{q \, S \, b}
$$

### Step 4 — Introduce the **asymmetric thrust parameter** $\mu$

A common KTU exam form introduces the parameter:

$$
\mu \triangleq \frac{T_e \, y_e}{q \, S \, b^2}
$$

This is sometimes written as $\mu = \dfrac{T_e \, y_e}{q \, S \, b \cdot b}$ to keep $b^2$ in the denominator (no vertical pipes). The two expressions differ by one factor of $b$ — verify which form your KTU valuation key uses.

### Step 5 — Solve for the moment arm $y_e$

Sometimes the KTU exam inverts the question and asks: *given $V_{MC}$, $C_{n_\beta}$, and design constants, what maximum $y_e$ is acceptable?*

We solve the relation:

$$
V_{MC} = \sqrt{ \frac{2 \, T_e \, y_e}{\rho \, S \, b \, C_{n_{\delta_r}}} }
$$

for $y_e$:

$$
y_e = \frac{ \rho \, S \, b \, C_{n_{\delta_r}} \, V_{MC}^2 }{ 2 \, T_e }
$$

### Step 6 — Numerical sanity check (worked example)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example (Sea level, twin turboprop)**
> Given:
> - $T_e = 12{,}000$ N (one engine)
> - $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m$^3$
> - $S = 30$ m$^2$
> - $b = 16$ m
> - $C_{n_{\delta_r}} = 0.0012$ rad$^{-1}$ (per deg of rudder)
> - $V_{MC} = 70$ m/s (≈ 136 kt)

Compute $y_e$:

$$
y_e = \frac{(1.225)(30)(16)(0.0012)(70)^2}{2 \cdot 12000}
$$

### Step 7 — Expanded calculation (every arithmetic step shown)

$$
y_e = \frac{1.225 \times 30 \times 16 \times 0.0012 \times 4900}{24000}
$$

Compute the numerator term by term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.225 \times 30 &= 36.75 \\
36.75 \times 16 &= 588.0 \\
588.0 \times 0.0012 &= 0.7056 \\
0.7056 \times 4900 &= 3457.44
\end{aligned}
$$

Divide by denominator:

$$
y_e = \frac{3457.44}{24000} \approx 0.1441 \text{ m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A maximum lateral engine placement of **≈ 14.4 cm** from the CG is required to maintain $V_{MC}$ of 70 m/s. This is far smaller than a real twin-engine wing span of $b/2 \approx 8$ m — meaning that $V_{MC}$ must be **relaxed** (i.e., larger) or thrust **reduced** (smaller $T_e$) to permit realistic engine placement.

### Step 8 — Inverted problem (design implication)

If the actual engine placement is $y_e = 4.0$ m (typical outboard nacelle on a regional turboprop), what is the resulting $V_{MC}$?

$$
V_{MC} = \sqrt{ \frac{2 \cdot T_e \cdot y_e}{\rho \, S \, b \, C_{n_{\delta_r}}} }
$$

$$
V_{MC} = \sqrt{ \frac{2 \times 12000 \times 4.0}{1.225 \times 30 \times 16 \times 0.0012} }
$$

### Step 9 — Continued calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator:} \quad 2 \times 12000 \times 4.0 &= 96000 \\
\text{Denominator:} \quad 1.225 \times 30 \times 16 \times 0.0012 &= 0.7056 \\
\text{Ratio:} \quad \frac{96000}{0.7056} &\approx 136{,}054 \\
V_{MC} &= \sqrt{136{,}054} \approx 369 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** $V_{MC} = 369$ m/s is **faster than any practical takeoff speed** — meaning with these inputs, the design is uncontrollable on engine-out. Either reduce $y_e$, increase $C_{n_{\delta_r}}$ (bigger rudder), or limit maximum thrust at $V_{MC}$ conditions.

## 3.2 Derivation of Critical Engine Definition (FAR 25)

### Step 1 — State the governing yawing moment

After one engine fails on a multi-engine aircraft, the **net yawing moment** about the CG is:

$$
N = T_e \cdot y_e \quad \text{(assuming the failed engine's thrust is zero)}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute for each engine

For an engine at lateral distance $y_i$ from CG with operating thrust $T_i$:

$$
N_i = T_i \cdot y_i
$$

### Step 3 — Identify maximum

The **critical engine** is the one whose failure gives the **largest** $|N_i|$:

$$
i^{*} = \arg\max_{i} \mid T_i \, y_i \mid
$$

### Step 4 — Consequence for placement

- For a **symmetric twin** (Boeing 737), both engines are equidistant and equal thrust ⇒ either is critical.
- For a **tri-jet** (Boeing 727), the **tail engine** (if centerline) has $y_{tail}=0$ ⇒ not critical. The wing engines are critical ⇒ failure of **either wing engine** is the certification case.
- For **asymmetric layouts** (e.g., one engine on wing + one on tail), the engine with largest $y$ is critical.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Single vs Multi Engine

| Design Driver | Single Engine | Twin Wing | Twin Fuselage (Push-Pull) | Tri-Jet (Wing + Tail) | Quad (Wing + Wing) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{MC}$ consideration | None | **High** | **None** (thrust on CG) | **High** for wing engine | Moderate |
| Thrust line offset $y_e$ | 0 (on axis) | Large (wing) | 0 (on axis) | Tail = 0, Wing = large | Moderate |
| Critical engine | None | Either | None | Either wing engine | Symmetric |
| Weight penalty | 1.0× | ~1.15× | ~1.20× | ~1.30× | ~1.40× |
| Drag penalty (nacelle) | 1× | 2× | 2× | 3× | 4× |
| Cabin noise | Front (tractor) or rear (pusher) | Side | Side | Side + center | Side |
| Maintenance access | Easy | Easy | Difficult | Moderate | Easy |
| Certification cost | Low | High | Medium | Very High | High |
| Failure survival | None | 50% thrust | 50% thrust | 33% or 67% thrust | 50% thrust |
| Example | Cessna 172 | A320 | Cessna 337 | B-727 | B-747 |

## 3.4 Derivation of Pitching Moment from Tractor/Pusher Offset

### Step 1 — Establish thrust vector and CG separation

For a tractor engine with thrust line **below** the CG by vertical distance $z_e < 0$ (below CG), the thrust produces a **nose-up pitching moment** if the thrust line is **above** the CG, and **nose-down** if below.

### Step 2 — General formula

$$
M_T = T_e \cdot z_e
$$

where $z_e$ is the **vertical distance** from CG to thrust line (positive upward).

### Step 3 — Stability implication

For tractor propellers mounted **above the wing**, the moment is nose-up; the horizontal tail must trim downward, increasing download and drag. For tractor propellers mounted on the **nose** (e.g., single-engine Cessna), thrust is approximately on the CG axis ⇒ $z_e \approx 0$ ⇒ $M_T \approx 0$.

### Step 4 — Pusher consideration

For a pusher propeller **behind** the CG, the moment is typically nose-up due to engine weight aft of CG; thrust itself is along CG axis, but the **thrust line bend** through a pusher on the rear fuselage produces a **nose-down** moment as power changes (P-factor analog).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the term "critical engine" as applicable to a multi-engine aircraft. State, with justification, which engine is critical in the Boeing 727 Tri-Jet configuration.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** A *critical engine* is the engine whose failure produces the **largest adverse yawing moment** about the aircraft center of gravity. Mathematically, for engine $i$ with thrust $T_i$ and lateral arm $y_i$, the critical engine satisfies:
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> i^{*} = \arg\max_{i} \mid T_i \, y_i \mid
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Application to Boeing 727 (1 mark):** In the B-727 tri-jet, the **centerline tail engine** (engine #2) is on the thrust axis (its lateral arm $y_{tail} \approx 0$). The two wing engines (engines #1 and #3) are mounted symmetrically with large lateral arms. Hence, **either wing engine** is the **critical engine** — its failure produces the largest yawing moment, which the rudder must counteract.

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Compare tractor and pusher propeller configurations for a single-engine aircraft. List two advantages and two disadvantages of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Configuration | Advantages (any 2) | Disadvantages (any 2) |
|---|---|---|
| **Tractor (front)** | 1. Propeller slipstream cools engine. 2. Cleaner wing (no pusher interference). 3. Simple nose-mount structure. | 1. Forward visibility obstructed. 2. Propeller arc danger to ground crew. 3. Cabin noise from propeller. |
| **Pusher (rear)** | 1. Unobstructed forward view. 2. Lower cabin noise. 3. Can use short-coupled wings. | 1. Deep-stall risk on aft-mounted engines (T-tail). 2. Wing interference with propeller slipstream. 3. Cooling less effective. |

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE rules, Module 4 offers **internal choice** between two 14-mark questions. Both Question A and Question B are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** *Explain with neat sketches the different engine placement configurations used in modern multi-engine aircraft. Discuss the relative merits and demerits of wing-mounted vs tail-mounted engine configurations. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *For a twin-engine commuter aircraft, the following data are given at takeoff (sea-level standard):*
- *Maximum takeoff thrust per engine: $T_e = 18{,}000$ N*
- *Lateral distance of each engine from CG: $y_e = 4.5$ m*
- *Wing area: $S = 45$ m$^2$*
- *Wingspan: $b = 22$ m*
- *Rudder control derivative: $C_{n_{\delta_r}} = 0.0015$ rad$^{-1}$*
- *Air density: $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m$^3$*

*Calculate the minimum control speed $V_{MC}$ and comment on whether the design satisfies FAR-23 requirements (assume $V_S = 38$ m/s). (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**(Award full 7 marks for the following structured answer.)**

**1. Engine placement configurations (4 marks):**

- **Wing-mounted twin (e.g., B-737, A-320, Beech Baron 58):** Two engines on pylons outboard on each wing, equidistant from fuselage. Symmetric placement ⇒ equal moment arms.
- **Fuselage-side parallel (e.g., Cessna 337):** Engines mounted on fuselage sides, tractor + pusher or two tractors. Small moment arm ⇒ easier directional control.
- **Tail-mounted (e.g., MD-80, DC-10):** Engines on rear fuselage, often with an additional wing engine. Used for high-bypass turbofans on narrow-body airliners.
- **Over-wing (e.g., B-52, B-36):** Engines mounted above wing on pylons. Wing bending moment relief.

**2. Wing-mounted vs Tail-mounted comparison (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Wing-mounted | Tail-mounted |
|---|---|---|
| Cabin noise | Higher (under cabin) | Lower (behind cabin) |
| Maintenance | Easy access via ground | Difficult (tail high) |
| Asymmetric thrust | Moderate arm | Largest arm (critical engine) |
| Ground clearance | Excellent | Marginal for large fans |
| Wing bending relief | Yes | No |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Write the $V_{MC}$ formula (1 mark):**

$$
V_{MC} = \sqrt{ \frac{2 \, T_e \, y_e}{\rho \, S \, b \, C_{n_{\delta_r}}} }
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given data (1 mark):**

$$
V_{MC} = \sqrt{ \frac{2 \times 18000 \times 4.5}{1.225 \times 45 \times 22 \times 0.0015} }
$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator (1 mark):**

$$
2 \times 18000 \times 4.5 = 162{,}000 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.225 \times 45 &= 55.125 \\
55.125 \times 22 &= 1212.75 \\
1212.75 \times 0.0015 &= 1.819125
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Form the ratio (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{162{,}000}{1.819125} \approx 89{,}053
$$

**Step 6 — Take square root (1 mark):**

$$
V_{MC} = \sqrt{89{,}053} \approx 298.4 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 7 — Comment on FAR-23 compliance (1 mark):**

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall Alert:** Students commonly compare $V_{MC}$ with $V_S$ alone. The correct FAR-23 rule is:
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> V_{MC} \leq 1.2 \, V_S = 1.2 \times 38 = 45.6 \text{ m/s}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> Since the calculated $V_{MC} = 298.4$ m/s $\gg 45.6$ m/s, **the design FAILS FAR-23** in this configuration. The student must conclude that the design is not certifiable — either reduce $y_e$, reduce $T_e$ at $V_{MC}$ conditions, or increase rudder authority.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** *Explain the concept of "minimum control speed" $V_{MC}$. Derive an expression for $V_{MC}$ in terms of thrust, moment arm, and rudder control power. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A twin-engine business jet has the following data:*
- *Engine thrust: $T_e = 14{,}000$ N per engine*
- *Lateral engine arm: $y_e = 3.8$ m*
- *Wing area: $S = 38$ m$^2$*
- *Wingspan: $b = 18$ m*
- *Rudder control derivative: $C_{n_{\delta_r}} = 0.0018$ rad$^{-1}$*
- *Air density: $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m$^3$*

*Determine the maximum allowable lateral engine arm $y_e$ if the $V_{MC}$ must not exceed $1.2 \, V_S$, with $V_S = 42$ m/s. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**1. Concept of $V_{MC}$ (3 marks):**

> **Definition:** $V_{MC}$ is the **minimum airspeed** at which directional control can be maintained (using rudder alone, no aileron) after the **critical engine** has failed, with the **operative engine at takeoff thrust**, and the aircraft in the takeoff configuration (gear down, flaps takeoff).

**2. Free-body sketch and derivation (4 marks):**

After engine failure, the operative engine produces thrust $T_e$ at lateral arm $y_e$, generating a yawing moment:

$$
N = T_e \cdot y_e
$$

The rudder must counter this with its own yawing moment $N_R$:

$$
N_R = C_{n_{\delta_r}} \cdot \delta_r \cdot q \cdot S \cdot b
$$

For balance, set $N = N_R$:

$$
T_e \, y_e = C_{n_{\delta_r}} \, \delta_{r,max} \, q \, S \, b
$$

At $V = V_{MC}$, dynamic pressure is $q = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{MC}^2$. Solving:

$$
V_{MC}^2 = \frac{2 \, T_e \, y_e}{\rho \, S \, b \, C_{n_{\delta_r}} \, \delta_{r,max}}
$$

For **$\delta_{r,max} = 1$ (full rudder)**, the simplified KTU form is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{MC} = \sqrt{ \frac{2 \, T_e \, y_e}{\rho \, S \, b \, C_{n_{\delta_r}}} }
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute the design $V_{MC}$ limit (1 mark):**

$$
V_{MC} = 1.2 \, V_S = 1.2 \times 42 = 50.4 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve the $V_{MC}$ formula for $y_e$ (1 mark):**

$$
y_e = \frac{ \rho \, S \, b \, C_{n_{\delta_r}} \, V_{MC}^2 }{ 2 \, T_e }
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $V_{MC}^2$ (1 mark):**

$$
V_{MC}^2 = (50.4)^2 = 2540.16 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute all data (1 mark):**

$$
y_e = \frac{1.225 \times 38 \times 18 \times 0.0018 \times 2540.16}{2 \times 14000}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the numerator stepwise (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.225 \times 38 &= 46.55 \\
46.55 \times 18 &= 837.9 \\
837.9 \times 0.0018 &= 1.50822 \\
1.50822 \times 2540.16 &\approx 3831.06
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Divide by denominator (1 mark):**

$$
y_e = \frac{3831.06}{28000} \approx 0.1368 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 7 — Comment on the result (1 mark):**

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall Alert:** A student may incorrectly conclude $y_e \approx 0.14$ m is feasible and stop. **But** this is **unrealistically small** — no real engine can be mounted that close to the CG without structural interference. The correct engineering conclusion is that **the design is impractical** in this configuration; the student should recommend **increasing rudder authority** (bigger vertical tail) or **reducing $V_S$** (larger wing area or higher $C_{L,max}$) to permit a realistic $y_e \geq 2$ m.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout (Critical for Marks)**
> 
> 1. **Most-failed part:** Students confuse $y_e$ (lateral arm for yawing moment) with $x_e$ (longitudinal arm for pitching moment). The engine placement for $V_{MC}$ involves the **lateral** arm, **not** longitudinal.
> 2. **Second-most-failed part:** Forgetting to non-dimensionalize $N$ properly. Always divide by $q \, S \, b$ to get $C_n$.
> 3. **Third-most-failed part:** Using $V_S$ (single-engine stall speed) when FAR-25 actually uses $V_{S1}$ (stall speed in takeoff configuration). For FAR-23, $1.2 V_S$ is acceptable.
> 4. **Critical-engine definition:** Many students write "the engine that produces the most thrust" — **wrong**. It is the engine whose **failure** produces the most adverse yawing moment, factoring in **both** $T$ and $y$.
> 5. **Distributed propulsion:** If asked about eVTOL (e.g., Lilium Jet), students often forget that each rotor/prop is **an independent thrust line**, multiplying the asymmetric-thrust considerations. State this explicitly.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Engine Placements (Module 4)**
>
> - ☐ **Engine placement** = choice of thrust-line location relative to airframe (longitudinal, lateral, vertical).
> - ☐ **Tractor** = prop in front, pulling; **Pusher** = prop behind, pushing.
> - ☐ **Single-engine placements:** nose tractor, aft pusher, mid-fuselage inline, wing nacelle.
> - ☐ **Multi-engine placements:** wing twin, fuselage twin, push-pull tandem, tri-jet (wing + tail), quad (wing or distributed).
> - ☐ **Critical engine** = engine whose failure gives largest yawing moment $N_i = T_i y_i$. **Not** the one with most thrust.
> - ☐ **$V_{MC}$ formula:** $V_{MC} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 T_e y_e}{\rho S b C_{n_{\delta_r}}}}$.
> - ☐ **FAR-23 limit:** $V_{MC} \leq 1.2 V_S$; **FAR-25 limit:** $V_{MC} \leq 1.13 V_{S1}$ at sea level.
> - ☐ **Symmetric twin (B-737):** both engines equidistant ⇒ either is critical.
> - ☐ **Tri-jet (B-727):** tail engine on CG ⇒ wing engines are critical.
> - ☐ **Push-pull (Cessna 337):** both engines on CG axis ⇒ **no asymmetric thrust** on engine-out.
> - ☐ **Tail-mounted engines (DC-10, MD-11):** largest $y_e$ ⇒ largest $V_{MC}$ ⇒ requires large rudder.
> - ☐ **Distributed propulsion (B-2, eVTOL):** many small thrust lines ⇒ redundancy and low radar signature but integration complexity.
> - ☐ **Pitching moment** from thrust offset: $M_T = T_e z_e$, where $z_e$ is **vertical** offset from CG.
> - ☐ **Power loading** $W/P$ and **thrust-to-weight** $T/W$ are key non-dimensional figures of merit.
> - ☐ **Propulsive efficiency** $\eta_{prop,max} \approx 0.85$ (Betz limit) for tractor.
> - ☐ **Always non-dimensionalize** yawing moments using $C_n = \dfrac{N}{q S b}$.
> - ☐ **Engine-out** scenarios must be considered for **all multi-engine** aircraft during certification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Landing Gear Type and Placement (Tail Dragger, Tricycle, Tandem)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET865 - AIRCRAFT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Landing Gear Type and Placement (Tail Dragger, Tricycle, Tandem)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Landing Gear Configuration?

In aircraft design, the **landing gear configuration** refers to the geometric arrangement of the wheels (and supporting struts) relative to the aircraft's **Center of Gravity (CG)**, the ground line, and the fuselage reference axis. The choice of configuration fundamentally governs ground-handling behavior, takeoff/landing rotation dynamics, pilot visibility, structural weight, and crosswind limitations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMET865 – Module 4):**
> *"Landing gear layout deals with the type (tail-wheel, nose-wheel, tandem, multi-wheel) and the placement (longitudinal and lateral) of the landing gears beneath the aircraft such that ground stability, structural load transfer, and operational safety are satisfied throughout taxi, take-off, and landing."*

## 1.2 The Three Primary Configurations (KTU Focus)

| Configuration | Alternate Name | Wheel Position |
|---------------|---------------|----------------|
| **Tail Dragger** | Conventional Gear | Two main wheels *forward* of CG + small tailwheel at rear |
| **Tricycle** | Nose-Wheel Gear (NWG) | Two main wheels *aft* of CG + nose wheel forward |
| **Tandem** | Bicycle / Inline Gear | Two main wheels aligned along the fuselage centerline |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Chair and the Stool"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> Imagine you sitting on a three-legged stool with one leg significantly shorter than the others.
> - A **Tail Dragger** behaves like a person leaning forward on a chair balanced on its front two legs — the rear (tail) is light, and the front must hold almost everything. If you push from behind, it tips backward (a famous phenomenon called the *"ground loop"*).
> - A **Tricycle** is like a normal office chair with casters: stable, balanced, and easy to push in any direction. The nose wheel acts like a guide.
> - A **Tandem** is like a single-track motorcycle wheel arrangement — perfect for an aircraft with a very narrow fuselage (e.g., bombers with a deep weapons bay) but highly prone to lateral tipping.

This single intuition explains why almost every modern transport, fighter, and trainer aircraft today uses the **tricycle** layout — it is the most forgiving and intuitive to handle on the ground.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

- It is a **Module 4 focal topic** under *Configuration Layout* of aircraft design.
- Direct 3-mark questions appear on configuration identification, advantages, and disadvantages.
- 14-mark questions (with sub-parts) frequently combine landing gear placement with **static/dynamic ground stability analysis** and **weight distribution on wheels**.
- Crosses over into **structures** (load paths into fuselage), **aerodynamics** (rotation drag, crosswind), and **propulsion** (propeller ground clearance).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Tail Dragger (Conventional / Tail-Wheel Gear)

### 2.1.1 Geometry
The two main wheels are positioned **forward** of the CG, while a small, often non-steerable tailwheel supports the rear. The aircraft therefore sits on the ground with its **longitudinal axis tilted nose-up** — the *tail-low* attitude.

**Key Geometric Parameters:**
- $b$ = longitudinal distance between main wheels and tailwheel (total wheelbase)
- $b_m$ = distance from CG to main wheels (CG lies *behind* the mains → $b_m > 0$ toward tail)
- $b_t$ = distance from CG to tailwheel
- $h$ = height of CG above ground

### 2.1.2 Ground Stability Analysis

**Static Tip-Back Condition:** The aircraft will tip backward (prop strike) if the line of gravity passes behind the main wheels.

$$\tan\phi_{\text{crit}} = \frac{b_m}{h}$$

where $\phi_{\text{crit}}$ is the maximum rearward tip-back angle. Beyond this, the aircraft rotates uncontrollably about the main wheels.

**Nose-Over (Forward Tip) Condition:** Conversely, with brakes locked hard, the aircraft can somersault forward.

$$\tan\theta_{\text{crit}} = \frac{b_t}{h}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb (Tail Dragger):** Always keep $h/b_m$ small (i.e., CG low relative to wheelbase ahead of CG) to prevent nose-over during braking.

### 2.1.3 Why Pilots Dislike It — *Ground Loop*
When taxiing in a crosswind or with asymmetric thrust, the CG (located *behind* the mains) produces an overturning moment. Any yaw is amplified because the contact patches of the mains act as a **pivot**, swinging the tail in the opposite direction — the aircraft literally *loops* on the ground.

### 2.1.4 Advantages
- Larger **propeller clearance** (useful for tail-draggers with long, ground-scraping propellers of early piston aircraft, e.g., DC-3, P-51 Mustang).
- Greater **main-wheel steering authority** because they are near the CG.
- Simpler, lighter structure (saves weight and cost).
- Suits **soft/rough field** operations because the tailwheel is light.

### 2.1.5 Disadvantages
- **Poor forward visibility** on the ground (tail-low attitude blocks forward view).
- Highly susceptible to **ground looping**.
- Tailwheel typically non-steerable ⇒ tight turns require **differential braking**.
- Pilot workload during crosswind landings is extreme.

---

## 2.2 The Tricycle Gear (Nose-Wheel Gear — NWG)

### 2.2.1 Geometry
Two main wheels **aft** of the CG, plus a steerable nose wheel forward. The aircraft sits **level** (or slightly nose-up) on the ground — *tail-high attitude*.

**Key Geometric Parameters:**
- $b_n$ = distance from CG to nose wheel
- $b_m$ = distance from CG to main wheels
- $b = b_n + b_m$ = total wheelbase
- $t$ = track (lateral distance between main wheels)
- $h$ = height of CG

### 2.2.2 The Cornerstone KTU Condition — Directional Ground Stability

For an aircraft to be **directionally (yaw) stable on the ground**, the CG must lie **ahead** of the line joining the two main-wheel contact patches. This is universally stated as:

$$\boxed{\,e > 0\,}$$

where $e$ is the **longitudinal eccentricity** measured from CG to main-wheel axle (positive when CG is *forward* of mains).

Equivalently:

$$\frac{b_n}{b_m} > 0 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad b_n > 0$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is the **#1 cause of loss of marks** in KTU paper valuation — students write "$b_n < b_m$" without stating the positive eccentricity condition. Always write it in terms of CG being **ahead of mains**.

### 2.2.3 Lateral Static Stability — Tip-Over
The aircraft tips laterally when the resultant weight passes outside the outer main wheel:

$$\tan\phi_{\text{lateral}} = \frac{t/2}{h}$$

A wider track $t$ and lower CG $h$ raise the tip-over threshold.

### 2.2.4 Load Distribution on Wheels (Static)
With aircraft weight $W$ resting on three points and the CG eccentricity known:

$$N_n = W \cdot \frac{b_m}{b_n + b_m}$$

$$N_{m,\text{each}} = \frac{W}{2} \cdot \frac{b_n}{b_n + b_m}$$

where $N_n$ is the load on the nose wheel and $N_{m,\text{each}}$ is the load on each main wheel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of 80/20 for typical tricycle:** Approximately **80% of W rests on mains** and **20% on the nose wheel** at rest. During braking this nose load grows.

### 2.2.5 Advantages
- **Excellent ground stability** — natural resistance to ground loop.
- **Superior forward visibility** for the pilot.
- **Predictable braking** — straight-line deceleration.
- Steerable nose wheel simplifies taxi.
- Cleaner rotation for takeoff (rotate about mains).

### 2.2.6 Disadvantages
- **Nose-wheel shimmy** (dynamic instability of the unsprung nose wheel) — mitigated by shimmy dampers.
- **Propeller/Engine ground clearance** reduced → low-slung engines risk foreign-object damage (FOD).
- **Heavier** and **more complex** than tail draggers.
- Heavier structural load on forward fuselage.

---

## 2.3 The Tandem Gear (Bicycle / Inline Gear)

### 2.3.1 Geometry
Main wheels are aligned **along the longitudinal axis** of the fuselage, one forward, one aft of CG. Used on **bombers** (B-52, B-1, Vigilante) and **high-speed interceptor/fighter prototypes** (SR-71 originally used bicycle gear with outrigger wing-tip pods). Modern fighters use tandem variants with **outrigger** or **multi-wheel** arrangements.

### 2.3.2 Stability Conditions
Since there is no lateral track, the configuration is **inherently unstable laterally**. Outrigger wheels or wing-tip support pods must be deployed low-speed (often during taxi only) or permanently to provide lateral stability.

### 2.3.3 Advantages
- Fits inside a **narrow, slender fuselage** (preserves internal volume for fuel, payload, weapons bay).
- Eliminates wing/fuselage bending moment due to symmetric gear load transfer to the fuselage.
- Suitable for **carrier-based aircraft** (e.g., F/A-18) where wing-fold mechanisms conflict with main gear placement.

### 2.3.4 Disadvantages
- **No lateral stability** without auxiliary outriggers.
- Heavy crosswind behavior.
- Complex deployment/retraction of outriggers.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Quick Reference)

| # | Formula / Condition | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $e > 0$ (CG ahead of main gear) | Directional ground stability for **tricycle** |
| 2 | $e < 0$ (CG behind main gear) | **Tail dragger** — prone to ground loop |
| 3 | $\tan\phi_{\text{tip}} = b/h$ | Tip-over angle (longitudinal) |
| 4 | $\tan\phi_{\text{lateral}} = (t/2)/h$ | Lateral tip-over angle |
| 5 | $N_n = W \cdot b_m/(b_n + b_m)$ | Nose-wheel load (tricycle) |
| 6 | $N_m = W \cdot b_n/(b_n + b_m)$ | Total main-wheel load (tricycle) |
| 7 | $\tau = \frac{I_{yy}}{m \cdot b \cdot h}$ | Ground resonance/shimmy screening parameter |
| 8 | $\delta_{TO} = \arctan\!\big(\tfrac{V_{LOF}^2}{g \cdot R}\big)$ | Rotation clearance angle |
| 9 | $H_{CG} \le \frac{t}{2}\tan\phi_{\text{limit}}$ | CG-height lateral stability bound |
| 10 | $\eta_{brake} = \frac{\mu W}{F_{brake,\max}}$ | Anti-skid braking efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> The absolute-value bars in formulas above are written as `\vert` to ensure Markdown compatibility — never as `|`.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The choice of landing gear configuration drives:
1. **Airworthiness certification** under FAR Part 23 / Part 25 / CS-23 / CS-25 (EASA), which require demonstration of ground stability in crosswinds up to **20 kt for utility** and **30 kt for transport category**.
2. **Maintenance cost** — tricycle shimmy dampers, hydraulic actuators, and anti-skid brakes constitute ~**6% of operating cost** of transport aircraft.
3. **Passenger experience** — nose-wheel steering eliminates the jarring tailwag of tail-draggers.
4. **Field performance** — tail-draggers handle unimproved grass strips far better than tricycle aircraft.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Static Load Distribution on Tricycle Gear

**Problem Statement (Classic KTU numerical):**
> A transport aircraft has the following data: $W = 60{,}000$ N, $b_n = 4$ m (CG to nose wheel), $b_m = 6$ m (CG to main wheels). Find the load on the nose wheel and on each main wheel.

### Step 1 — Free Body Diagram Setup

Place the aircraft on three contact points (nose wheel $N_n$ and two main wheels $N_m$). Treat the aircraft as a rigid body in equilibrium under weight $W$ acting at the CG.

### Step 2 — Vertical Force Equilibrium

$$N_n + 2 N_m = W$$

### Step 3 — Moment Equilibrium About the Main Wheels

Taking moments about the main-wheel axle (eliminates $N_m$):

$$\begin{aligned}
N_n \cdot b_m - W \cdot b_m &= 0 \\
\Rightarrow N_n &= W \cdot \frac{b_m}{b_n + b_m}
\end{aligned}$$

> Here we used the fact that the weight acts at a distance $b_m$ *behind* the main wheel (CG is ahead of mains in tricycle) → moment arm = $b_m$. The nose-wheel reaction acts at a distance $b_n + b_m$ *in front of* the mains → moment arm = $b_n + b_m$, multiplied by the nose-wheel force which produces a balancing moment.

### Step 4 — Substitute Numbers

$$\begin{aligned}
N_n &= 60{,}000 \times \frac{6}{4+6} \\
&= 60{,}000 \times 0.6 \\
&= 36{,}000\ \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Main-Wheel Load (Total)

$$2 N_m = W - N_n = 60{,}000 - 36{,}000 = 24{,}000\ \text{N}$$

$$\therefore N_m = 12{,}000\ \text{N per wheel}$$

### Step 6 — Verification — Moment Equilibrium About Nose Wheel

$$\begin{aligned}
2 N_m \cdot (b_n + b_m) - W \cdot b_n &= 0 \\
24{,}000 \times 10 - 60{,}000 \times 4 &= 240{,}000 - 240{,}000 = 0 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** Nose wheel carries **60%** of $W$, each main wheel **20%**. (Higher than the typical 20/80 because CG is far forward in this hypothetical — useful for illustrating how forward CG shifts load to the nose wheel.)

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Ground Tip-Over Angle for a Tricycle Aircraft

**Problem Statement:**
> Find the maximum nose-up rotation angle before tail strike for a tricycle aircraft with $b_n = 3$ m, $b_m = 5$ m, $h = 1.6$ m, and CG height $h_{CG} = 1.2$ m.

### Step 1 — Identify Pivot Point
At rotation, the aircraft pivots about the **main wheels**. The CG describes a circular arc of radius $\sqrt{b_m^{\,2} + h_{CG}^{\,2}}$.

### Step 2 — Geometry of Tip-Back
Just before tail strike, the line CG–Main-Wheel makes angle $\phi$ with the ground:

$$\tan\phi = \frac{h_{CG}}{b_m}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute

$$\begin{aligned}
\tan\phi &= \frac{1.2}{5} = 0.24 \\
\phi &= \arctan(0.24) \\
\phi &= 13.5^{\circ}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Tail Strike Condition
The tail strikes ground when the fuselage underside angle equals the rotation angle. Fuselage clearance angle $\alpha_{clear}$ is given by:

$$\alpha_{clear} = \phi + \arctan\!\left(\frac{r_{tail} - h_{CG}}{L_{tail}}\right)$$

where $r_{tail}$ is the tail-wheel/rump height and $L_{tail}$ the CG-to-tail-base distance. The aircraft must complete rotation (typically $\sim 8°-12°$ for transport jets) **before** $\phi$ is reached.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Comparison Matrix — Configurations at a Glance

| Design Driver | Tail Dragger | Tricycle | Tandem |
|---------------|-------------|----------|--------|
| CG Position | Ahead of mains by design | Behind nose wheel | Midway between two gears |
| $e$ | Negative | **Positive** | None laterally |
| Tip-Back Risk | **High** (prop strike) | Low | Low |
| Ground Loop Risk | **Very High** | Very Low | Moderate |
| Pilot Forward Visibility | **Poor** | Excellent | Fair |
| Propeller Ground Clearance | **Excellent** | Limited | Limited (mid-fuselage) |
| Crosswind Tolerance | Low (~15 kt) | **High (~30+ kt)** | Low–Moderate |
| Structural Weight Penalty | Low | Moderate | High (outrigger) |
| Crosswind Certification Limit (FAR 23) | 20 kt | **30 kt** | 20 kt |
| Typical Applications | Vintage, bushplanes, gliders | All modern jets & airliners | Bombers, carrier aircraft |
| Example Aircraft | DC-3, P-51, J-3 Cub | B-737, A-320, F-16 | B-52, B-1, XB-70, Vigilante |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Note: Ground Resonance (Shimmy) Screening

For a tricycle aircraft, an initial screening check on the ground-resonance mode uses:

$$\tau_{gr} = \frac{I_{y,\text{taxi}}}{m \, b \, h} \quad\text{should be} > \tau_{\min}$$

where $I_{y,\text{taxi}}$ is the yawing moment of inertia about the CG with landing gear extended, $m$ the mass, $b$ the wheelbase, and $h$ the CG height. This is the **Mil-Std-8A / JAS-23** guidance used by KTU-aligned examiners.

---

## 3.5 KTU Numerical Worked Example (Exam-Style)

**Question:**
> An aircraft weighing 45 kN has the CG located 1.5 m ahead of the main-wheel axle. The nose wheel is 3.5 m ahead of the main-wheel axle. Determine (a) the load on each wheel, (b) the maximum permissible runway slope before the aircraft starts to tip backward.

### Solution

**(a) Wheel Loads**

With $b_n = 1.5 + 3.5 = 5.0$ m, $b_m = 3.5$ m (note: CG is *between* nose wheel and mains here, so $b_n$ and $b_m$ measured consistently):

$$\begin{aligned}
N_n &= W \cdot \frac{b_m}{b_n + b_m} = 45{,}000 \times \frac{3.5}{5.0 + 3.5} \\
&= 45{,}000 \times \frac{3.5}{8.5} = 18{,}529\ \text{N} \\
2 N_m &= 45{,}000 - 18{,}529 = 26{,}471\ \text{N} \\
N_m &= 13{,}235\ \text{N per wheel}
\end{aligned}$$

**(b) Tipping Slope**

For tip-back, the line of gravity must pass through the main-wheel axle:

$$\tan\theta_{\text{slope}} = \frac{b_m}{h}$$

Take $h = 1.4$ m (typical light aircraft CG height):

$$\begin{aligned}
\tan\theta &= \frac{3.5}{1.4} = 2.5 \\
\theta &= \arctan(2.5) = 68.2^{\circ}
\end{aligned}$$

This is essentially **vertical** — i.e., the aircraft would never tip backward in normal operation. (Had the CG been behind the mains, the tip-back angle would be small and dangerous — the classic tail-dragger problem.)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Schematic — Three Landing Gear Configurations (Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TD["TAIL DRAGGER - Top View"]
        noseTD["NOSE / ENGINE"]:::nose
        mwL["MAIN WHEEL L"]:::mw
        mwR["MAIN WHEEL R"]:::mw
        tw["TAIL WHEEL"]:::tail
        cgTD["CG - behind mains"]:::cg
        noseTD --> mwL
        noseTD --> mwR
        mwL --> tw
        mwR --> tw
    end

    subgraph TRI["TRICYCLE - Top View"]
        nw["NOSE WHEEL"]:::nose
        cgTRI["CG - ahead of mains"]:::cg
        mwL2["MAIN WHEEL L"]:::mw
        mwR2["MAIN WHEEL R"]:::mw
        tailTRI["TAIL / AFT FUSELAGE"]:::tail
        nw --> cgTRI
        cgTRI --> mwL2
        cgTRI --> mwR2
        mwL2 --> tailTRI
        mwR2 --> tailTRI
    end

    subgraph TAN["TANDEM - Top View"]
        fwd["FWD GEAR"]:::mw
        cgTAN["CG - between gears"]:::cg
        aft["AFT GEAR"]:::mw
        outL["OUTRIGGER L"]:::tail
        outR["OUTRIGGER R"]:::tail
        fwd --> cgTAN
        cgTAN --> aft
        fwd -.-> outL
        fwd -.-> outR
        aft -.-> outL
        aft -.-> outR
    end

    classDef nose fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mw fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef tail fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cg fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Ground Stability Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Define Aircraft Role and Mission"]:::input
    B["Identify CG Envelope from Loading Diagram"]:::input
    C{"Is Forward CG Limit Close to Nose?"}:::decision
    D["Use TAIL DRAGGER"]:::choice
    E["Use TRICYCLE"]:::choice
    F{"Is Wing Span Wide Enough for Lateral Track?"}:::decision
    G["Use TANDEM with Outriggers"]:::choice
    H["Use Conventional TRICYCLE or MULTI-WHEEL"]:::choice
    I["Verify Tip-Over Stability tan phi = t slash 2h"]:::verify
    J["Verify e greater than 0 for Directional Stability"]:::verify
    K["Final Selection - Configuration Locked"]:::output

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C -- "Yes - tail prop clearance critical" --> D
    C -- "No" --> E
    E --> F
    F -- "No - slender fuselage e.g. bomber" --> G
    F -- "Yes" --> H
    D --> I
    E --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J
    J --> K

    classDef input fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef decision fill:#ffd700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef choice fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef verify fill:#bcbd22,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef output fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

```mermaid
    E["Vertical from CG"]:::vert
    F["Horizontal b sub m"]:::hor

    A --- B
    B --- C
    A --- E
    A --- F
    F --- B

    classDef cg fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef mw fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff

```

## 4.4 Architecture Flow — Landing Gear Selection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M["Mission Profile"]:::step
    P["Payload Range"]:::step
    C["CG Envelope"]:::step
    F["Field Length Requirements"]:::step
    XW["Crosswind Limit per Certification"]:::step
    G1["Configuration Options Generated"]:::step
    EVA["Static Stability Evaluation"]:::step
    DYN["Dynamic Stability Evaluation - Shimmy"]:::step
    WGT["Weight Penalty Estimation"]:::step
    COST["Cost Benefit"]:::step
    SEL["Final Configuration Selected"]:::output

    M --> P --> C --> F --> XW --> G1 --> EVA --> DYN --> WGT --> COST --> SEL

    classDef step fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef output fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between a *tail-dragger* and a *tricycle* landing gear configuration with respect to the position of the Center of Gravity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- In a **tail-dragger**, the two main wheels are positioned *forward* of the CG, with a small tailwheel at the rear. The CG therefore lies **behind** the main-wheel line — leading to a *negative eccentricity* ($e < 0$).
- In a **tricycle**, the two main wheels lie *aft* of the CG and a nose wheel is forward — the CG lies **ahead** of the main-wheel line, giving a *positive eccentricity* ($e > 0$).
- As a consequence, the tail-dragger is **ground-unstable** and prone to ground loops, whereas the tricycle is **ground-stable** directionally.
- Visually, the tail-dragger sits tail-low on the ground, while the tricycle sits level or slightly nose-up.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper]**
**List any three advantages of a tricycle landing gear over a conventional tail-wheel type.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Superior ground stability** because the CG is forward of the main-wheel line ($e > 0$), preventing ground looping.
2. **Excellent forward visibility** for the pilot on the ground (no tail-low attitude).
3. **Predictable braking** with anti-skid systems because weight is well-distributed and deceleration is straight-line.
4. *(bonus)* Reduced pilot workload during crosswind taxi and landing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper]**

> **(a)** Explain the concept of landing gear placement for an aircraft. With neat sketches, describe the three major types — tail dragger, tricycle, and tandem — highlighting CG position in each case. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A small aircraft of mass 1,500 kg is at rest on level ground with a tricycle landing gear. The distances are: $b_n = 2.0$ m (CG to nose wheel) and $b_m = 2.5$ m (CG to main wheels). The CG is at a height $h = 1.2$ m above the ground and the track width $t = 2.4$ m.
>
> Compute:
> (i) The vertical load on each main wheel and the nose wheel. **(3 marks)**
> (ii) The maximum lateral tip-over angle. **(2 marks)**
> (iii) The maximum longitudinal slope on which the aircraft can be parked without tipping backward. **(2 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A**

**(a) Conceptual Description (7 marks):**

Landing gear placement is the longitudinal and lateral positioning of wheels relative to the aircraft's CG, dictated by **stability**, **structural**, **operational** and **aerodynamic** considerations.

- **Tail Dragger:** Main wheels *forward* of CG, tailwheel at the rear. Aircraft sits tail-low. CG behind mains → $e < 0$ → directionally unstable on the ground. Propeller ground clearance is excellent.
- **Tricycle:** Nose wheel forward of CG, main wheels behind. Aircraft sits level/tail-high. CG ahead of mains → $e > 0$ → directionally stable. Excellent visibility, predictable braking.
- **Tandem:** Two main wheels inline along fuselage centerline. Used when fuselage is narrow (bombers) or for carrier compatibility. Lateral stability obtained via outriggers/wing-tip supports.

*(Include neat top-view sketches for each — refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for diagrams.)*

---

**(b)(i) Vertical Load on Each Wheel (3 marks):**

Total weight: $W = m \cdot g = 1500 \times 9.81 = 14{,}715$ N

$$\begin{aligned}
N_n &= W \cdot \frac{b_m}{b_n + b_m} \\
&= 14{,}715 \times \frac{2.5}{2.0 + 2.5} \\
&= 14{,}715 \times \frac{2.5}{4.5} \\
&= 14{,}715 \times 0.5556 \\
&= 8{,}175\ \text{N (nose wheel)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
2 N_m &= W - N_n = 14{,}715 - 8{,}175 = 6{,}540\ \text{N} \\
N_m &= 3{,}270\ \text{N per main wheel}
\end{aligned}$$

---

**(b)(ii) Maximum Lateral Tip-Over Angle (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tan\phi_{\text{lat}} &= \frac{t/2}{h} = \frac{1.2}{1.2} = 1.0 \\
\phi_{\text{lat}} &= \arctan(1.0) = 45^{\circ}
\end{aligned}$$

---

**(b)(iii) Maximum Longitudinal Slope (2 marks):**

Tip-back occurs when the line of gravity passes through the main-wheel contact point. With CG ahead of mains by $b_m = 2.5$ m, on a *negative* (downhill-rearward) slope the CG vector still lies ahead of mains.

$$\begin{aligned}
\tan\theta_{\text{slope}} &= \frac{b_m}{h} = \frac{2.5}{1.2} = 2.083 \\
\theta_{\text{slope}} &= \arctan(2.083) = 64.4^{\circ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The aircraft can be parked on slopes up to **64.4°** without tipping backward — essentially any realistic airport gradient.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Model Paper]**

> **(a)** Discuss in detail the merits and demerits of **tail-dragger** and **tricycle** landing gear layouts. Why has the aviation industry shifted predominantly to tricycle gear for modern aircraft? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A twin-engine transport aircraft has the following ground-load data at Maximum Take-Off Weight (MTOW): Total weight $W = 200$ kN, $b_n = 7.0$ m, $b_m = 2.5$ m, $h = 2.0$ m, $t = 5.5$ m.
>
> Determine:
> (i) The nose-wheel reaction and main-wheel reaction (each side). **(4 marks)**
> (ii) The longitudinal and lateral tip-over angles. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B**

**(a) Merits and Demerits (7 marks):**

*Tail Dragger — Merits:*
- Excellent propeller ground clearance (suited to large piston-engine aircraft).
- Heavy main wheels near CG → powerful steering through differential braking.
- Lighter structure, lower cost.
- Suited to soft/unprepared airfields (tailwheel is light and minimizes drag during taxi).

*Tail Dragger — Demerits:*
- Poor forward visibility (tail-low).
- Highly prone to **ground looping** under crosswind or asymmetric power.
- High pilot workload.
- Non-steerable tailwheel → tight turns require differential braking.

*Tricycle — Merits:*
- CG ahead of mains → $e > 0$ → ground stability.
- Excellent pilot visibility.
- Steerable nose wheel simplifies ground handling.
- Predictable braking performance.

*Tricycle — Demerits:*
- Limited propeller/engine ground clearance (FOD risk).
- Heavier structure (nose gear + steering + hydraulics).
- Nose-wheel shimmy requires dampers.

*Why industry shifted:*
- Higher crosswind operating limits (30 kt vs ~15 kt).
- Pilot training cost reduction.
- Passenger comfort and safety.
- Suitability for jet engines mounted low on the wing.

---

**(b)(i) Wheel Reactions (4 marks):**

$W = 200{,}000$ N, $b_n = 7.0$ m, $b_m = 2.5$ m.

$$\begin{aligned}
N_n &= W \cdot \frac{b_m}{b_n + b_m} \\
&= 200{,}000 \times \frac{2.5}{9.5} \\
&= 200{,}000 \times 0.2632 \\
&= 52{,}632\ \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
2 N_m &= 200{,}000 - 52{,}632 = 147{,}368\ \text{N} \\
N_m &= 73{,}684\ \text{N per side}
\end{aligned}$$

---

**(b)(ii) Tip-Over Angles (3 marks):**

Longitudinal tip-back:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tan\phi_{\text{long}} &= \frac{b_m}{h} = \frac{2.5}{2.0} = 1.25 \\
\phi_{\text{long}} &= \arctan(1.25) = 51.3^{\circ}
\end{aligned}$$

Lateral tip-over:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tan\phi_{\text{lat}} &= \frac{t/2}{h} = \frac{2.75}{2.0} = 1.375 \\
\phi_{\text{lat}} &= \arctan(1.375) = 53.97^{\circ}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Pitfalls (where students lose 1–3 marks):**
> 1. **Writing only $b_n < b_m$** instead of explicitly stating **$e > 0$** (CG ahead of mains) for tricycle ground stability.
> 2. **Forgetting to halve the main-wheel load** when reporting "load on each main wheel".
> 3. **Mixing up tip-back and tip-forward** — tip-back angle uses $b_m/h$ for tricycle; tip-forward uses $b_n/h$ for nose-over.
> 4. **Confusing track $t$ with wheelbase $b$** — track is *lateral*, wheelbase is *longitudinal*.
> 5. **Omitting units** in numerical answers — $m$ vs $mm$ vs $ft$ frequently mis-marked.
> 6. **Not drawing the free-body diagram** before writing equilibrium equations — examiners specifically look for it.
> 7. **Tail-dragger question mark** — many students write advantages but forget the **propeller clearance** advantage (a 2-marker in its own right).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Summary — Must-Remember for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **Three configurations:** Tail Dragger (CG behind mains), Tricycle (CG ahead of mains), Tandem (inline + outriggers).
- **Ground stability condition (Tricycle):** $e > 0$ — CG must be ahead of the main-wheel line.
- **Tip-over longitudinal angle:** $\tan\phi = b/h$ (use $b_m$ for tricycle tip-back).
- **Tip-over lateral angle:** $\tan\phi = (t/2)/h$.
- **Wheel load formulas:** $N_n = W \cdot b_m/(b_n + b_m)$, $N_m = W \cdot b_n/[2(b_n + b_m)]$.
- **Tricycle advantages:** Visibility, ground stability, predictable braking, crosswind tolerance.
- **Tricycle disadvantages:** Prop clearance, nose-wheel shimmy, heavier structure.
- **Tail-dragger prime hazard:** Ground loop.
- **Tandem prime hazard:** Lateral instability — requires outriggers.
- **Cert crosswind limit:** 20 kt (Tail dragger / Tandem), **30 kt (Tricycle)** under FAR/CS-23 utility.
- **Typical split:** Tricycle ≈ 80% on mains, 20% on nose (at rest).
- **Braking on tricycle:** Forward CG shifts weight to nose wheel, increasing nose-wheel loading during braking.
- **Carrier aircraft** (e.g., F/A-18) use tandem-like arrangement with canted outriggers.
- **Bicycle gear** used in supersonic bombers (B-1, B-52) — fuselage bent only in vertical plane, not lateral.
- **Landing gear weight fraction:** Roughly **3–6% of MTOW** for transport jets.
- **Shimmy check:** Always perform dynamic stability check for steerable nose wheel above 60 kt taxi speed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Packaging for Storage/Transport
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET865 - AIRCRAFT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Packaging for Storage/Transport

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aircraft Packaging for Storage/Transport** is the systematic engineering discipline concerned with the **preservation, protection, containerization, and documentation** of airframes, engines, avionics, and ancillary subsystems to maintain airworthiness and prevent degradation (corrosion, contamination, structural fatigue, moisture ingress) during periods of inactivity, transit, or shipment via surface or aerial means, in compliance with regulatory frameworks such as **MIL-STD-2073**, **ATA Specification 300**, **SAE ARP 1281**, and **ISO 11666**.

The discipline is broadly classified into three operational categories:

| Category | Definition | Typical Duration |
|----------|------------|------------------|
| **Short-Term Storage** | Routine parking between flights | Hours – 7 days |
| **Intermediate Storage** | Scheduled lay-up (e.g., seasonal fleets) | 7 days – 90 days |
| **Long-Term Storage / Type II Preservation** | Extended preservation exceeding 90 days | 3 months – 5 years |
| **Transportation Packaging** | Transit preparation via road/rail/sea/air | Days – weeks |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Time Capsule Aircraft"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you own a **classic 1965 vintage roadster** that must survive a **2-year overseas sea voyage** inside a humid container. You cannot simply park it. You must drain the fluids, fog the engine cylinders with protective oil, plug every opening against salt air, seal moisture-vulnerable parts in **barrier film + desiccants**, elevate the tires off the deck, and label the crate with shock/tilt indicators. Aircraft packaging follows the **identical logic** — except the "classic car" weighs **75,000 kg**, costs **\$80 million**, and contains fly-by-wire computers that are sensitive to **electrostatic discharge (ESD)** down to **100 V**.

The core engineering challenge is **maintaining the as-manufactured condition** while the asset is exposed to three principal degradation vectors:

$$\vec{D}_{total} = \vec{D}_{corrosion} + \vec{D}_{mechanical} + \vec{D}_{environmental}$$

where $\vec{D}_{corrosion}$ represents electrochemical oxidation, $\vec{D}_{mechanical}$ represents shock/vibration damage during transport, and $\vec{D}_{environmental}$ represents humidity, temperature cycling, UV, and contamination effects.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis):**
> - **Standard Preservation Humidity: < 35 % Relative Humidity (RH)**
> - **Standard Storage Temperature Range: -40 °C to +50 °C** (per **MIL-STD-2073-1D**)
> - **Maximum Allowable SHock during Transport: 4g peak** (per **ATA Spec 300 Category III**)
> - **Engine Preservation Fogging Oil Viscosity: ISO VG 32 to VG 100** (per **MIL-PRF-21260**)
> - **Desiccant Type: Silica Gel (Type 4 indicating) per MIL-D-3464**
> - **Standard Crate Drop Test Height: 305 mm (12 in) for Category III** per **ASTM D4169**

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Aircraft Design

In the **Aircraft Design lifecycle (NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 Scheme)**, packaging is treated as a **Design for Maintainability (DfM)** and **Design for Logistics (DfL)** sub-discipline. Modern KTU Module 4 expects students to appreciate that **logistics costs constitute 6–10 % of the Total Life Cycle Cost (LCC)** of an airframe — making packaging a **non-trivial design parameter**, not an afterthought.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The 5 Pillars of Aircraft Packaging

The KTU 2024 syllabus structures the discipline around **five interlocking pillars**:

### Pillar 1 — Preservation (Chemical & Physical)

The objective is to **isolate** the airframe from oxygen, moisture, and contaminants. This is achieved through:

- **Dry Preservation System (DPS):** Engine oil drained, replaced with **preservative oil** meeting **MIL-PRF-21260 Grade 2/3**, then the cylinders are **fogged** using a pressurized atomizer (typically at **35–70 kPa**) through the spark-plug or igniter ports.
- **Wet Preservation System (WPS):** Preservative oil is **left in the lubrication system**, and the engine is **hand-turned every 30 days** to prevent oil migration away from bearing surfaces.
- **Hybrid / "Pickled" Preservation:** Combines WPS with **VPI (Vapor Phase Inhibitor) / VCI (Volatile Corrosion Inhibitor) emitters** placed in fuel tanks and cavities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Depreservation** is the symmetric reverse process — flushing preservative oils, performing oil changes, inspecting magnetic chip detectors, and conducting ground runs before flight clearance.

### Pillar 2 — Environmental Control (Humidity & Temperature)

The **Clapeyron / Antoine Equation** governs the saturation vapor pressure of water over the operating temperature range, which directly determines **drying/dehumidification requirements**:

$$\log_{10}(P_{sat}) = A - \frac{B}{C + T}$$

where:
- $P_{sat}$ = saturation vapor pressure (mmHg)
- $T$ = temperature (°C)
- $A, B, C$ = Antoine constants (for water, **$A=8.07131$**, **$B=1730.63$**, **$C=233.426$** for $1 < T < 100$ °C)

The **absolute humidity ratio** of air inside a sealed container:

$$\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}$$

where $P_v$ is the partial pressure of water vapor. Maintaining $\omega < 0.008$ kg water/kg dry air prevents **filiform corrosion** on aluminum alloys (2024-T3, 7075-T6).

### Pillar 3 — Mechanical Protection (Shock, Vibration, Static Load)

Three transport loading profiles are standardized:

| Profile | Application | Peak $g$ | Frequency Range |
|---------|-------------|----------|-----------------|
| **Loose Cargo** (ATA Cat I) | Spare parts | 2g | 1–10 Hz |
| **Palletized** (ATA Cat II) | Subassemblies | 3g | 1–30 Hz |
| **Containerized Aircraft** (ATA Cat III) | Whole airframes | **4g** | 1–50 Hz |

The **shock response spectrum (SRS)** must remain below the **fragility curve** of the airframe. The natural frequency of a packaged airframe approximated as a single-DOF mass-spring:

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

where $k$ is the effective stiffness of the transport cradle and $m$ is the supported mass. Typical $f_n$ for a transport cradle: **2–6 Hz** (well below the airframe's structural modes of 10–25 Hz, providing isolation).

### Pillar 4 — Containerization (ATA Spec 300 / MIL-STD-2073)

Three container categories:

1. **ATA Category I:** Loose cargo, basic weather-resistant box.
2. **ATA Category II:** Palletized, requires humidity-controlled bag + 4 mil barrier film.
3. **ATA Category III:** Hard-shell, hermetically sealed, equipped with shock/tilt indicators, desiccants, VCI emitters, and temperature loggers.

### Pillar 5 — Documentation & Marking

Every package must carry:
- **NSN (National Stock Number)**
- **Aircraft Serial Number & Applicable TCDS reference**
- **Hazardous Material Declarations** (per **IATA DGR**)
- **Center of Gravity (CG) markers**
- **"THIS SIDE UP" arrows** per **ISO 780**
- **CooL / Keep Dry labels** per **ASTM D5445**

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Summary** — Memorize for KTU ESE.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Units | Application |
|---|---------------------|--------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Antoine Equation | $\log_{10}(P_{sat}) = A - \frac{B}{C+T}$ | mmHg, °C | Saturation vapor pressure |
| 2 | Humidity Ratio | $\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_{atm}-P_v}$ | kg/kg | Absolute humidity |
| 3 | Natural Frequency | $f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ | Hz | Cradle isolation design |
| 4 | Transmission Ratio (Vibration Isolation) | $T = \left\vert \frac{1}{1-(f/f_n)^2} \right\vert$ | dimensionless | Shock isolation efficiency |
| 5 | Desiccant Capacity | $M_{des} = \frac{m_{air} \cdot (\omega_i - \omega_f)}{\eta_{des}}$ | kg | Silica gel requirement |
| 6 | Dew Point Depressurization | $\Delta T_{dp} = T_{amb} - T_{dp}$ | °C | Condensation risk |
| 7 | Thermal Conduction (Crate Wall) | $q = \frac{kA \cdot \Delta T}{L}$ | W | Heat ingress to container |
| 8 | Container Surface Heat Loss | $Q = h_c \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$ | W | Convective loss |
| 9 | Corrosion Rate (Tafel) | $i_{corr} = i_0 \left[\exp\left(\frac{\alpha F \eta}{RT}\right)\right]$ | A/m² | Electrochemical degradation |
| 10 | Stress in Cradle Beam | $\sigma = \frac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | Pa | Static load check |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, **always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the literal pipe `|` for absolute values** to prevent table-rendering failure.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Airbus A350 XWB transport** from Toulouse to final assembly in Tianjin, China uses **two Boeing 747-8 Dreamlifter** flights with specially designed cradles — the design of these cradles is a direct application of packaging engineering.
- **Lockheed C-130 Hercules** long-term storage at the "boneyard" (AMARG, Davis-Monthan AFB) uses **Type II preservation** allowing recovery to flight within **90 days**.
- **F-35 Lightning II** uses **shrink-wrap encapsulation** with **VCI emitters** for sea transport to allied nations, replacing older "spray-on" methods.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Desiccant Mass for a Sealed Container

> **Problem Setup:** A sealed transport container of internal volume $V = 12 \text{ m}^3$ is charged with air at $25$ °C and $70$ % RH at sealing. The desired final RH inside the container at $25$ °C must be $\le 35$ %. Determine the minimum mass of Type 4 silica gel required, assuming a desiccant efficiency $\eta_{des} = 0.30$ (industry standard for silica gel in sealed applications).

### Step 1 — Determine Initial Saturation Vapor Pressure at 25 °C

Using the Antoine equation for water with constants $A = 8.07131$, $B = 1730.63$, $C = 233.426$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(P_{sat,\,25}) &= 8.07131 - \frac{1730.63}{233.426 + 25} \\
&= 8.07131 - \frac{1730.63}{258.426} \\
&= 8.07131 - 6.69732 \\
&= 1.37399
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{sat,\,25} &= 10^{1.37399} \\
&\approx 23.756 \text{ mmHg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to Pascals: $P_{sat} = 23.756 \times 133.322 = 3167 \text{ Pa}$.

**Logic:** We must first establish the upper bound of water vapor partial pressure at $25$ °C — this is the saturation pressure at which condensation begins.

### Step 2 — Compute Initial and Final Partial Pressures of Water Vapor

Initial RH $= 70$ %:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{v,i} &= 0.70 \times 3167 \\
&= 2217 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Final RH $= 35$ %:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{v,f} &= 0.35 \times 3167 \\
&= 1108 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The desiccant must remove enough water to bring the partial pressure from $P_{v,i}$ down to $P_{v,f}$.

### Step 3 — Calculate Humidity Ratios

Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 101325$ Pa. Using $\omega = 0.622 \cdot P_v / (P_{atm} - P_v)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_i &= 0.622 \times \frac{2217}{101325 - 2217} \\
&= 0.622 \times \frac{2217}{99108} \\
&= 0.622 \times 0.022372 \\
&= 0.013915 \text{ kg/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_f &= 0.622 \times \frac{1108}{101325 - 1108} \\
&= 0.622 \times \frac{1108}{100217} \\
&= 0.622 \times 0.011057 \\
&= 0.006878 \text{ kg/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The humidity ratio converts partial pressures into a mass-based engineering quantity suitable for desiccant sizing.

### Step 4 — Compute Mass of Dry Air Inside the Container

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{air} &= \frac{P_{atm} \cdot V}{R_{air} \cdot T} \\
&= \frac{101325 \times 12}{287.05 \times 298.15} \\
&= \frac{1{,}215{,}900}{85{,}592} \\
&\approx 14.21 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** $R_{air} = 287.05$ J/(kg·K) is the specific gas constant for dry air; the temperature is converted to Kelvin ($25+273.15 = 298.15$ K).

### Step 5 — Compute Water Vapor to Be Removed

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta m_{water} &= m_{air} \times (\omega_i - \omega_f) \\
&= 14.21 \times (0.013915 - 0.006878) \\
&= 14.21 \times 0.007037 \\
&= 0.1000 \text{ kg} \\
&\approx 100 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** This is the absolute mass of water that must be adsorbed by the desiccant to achieve the target humidity.

### Step 6 — Apply Desiccant Efficiency

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{des} &= \frac{\Delta m_{water}}{\eta_{des}} \\
&= \frac{0.1000}{0.30} \\
&= 0.333 \text{ kg} \\
&\approx 333 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Type 4 silica gel has a nominal adsorption capacity of $\approx 30$ % of its dry mass at $25$ °C, $35$ % RH. Therefore, to adsorb $100$ g, we need $\approx 333$ g of dry silica gel — adding a **safety factor of 1.5×** for transit duration would give **$\approx 500$ g** for field use.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Vibration Transmission Through Cradle

> **Problem Setup:** A packaged aircraft section of mass $m = 25{,}000$ kg rests on a transport cradle whose effective stiffness $k = 5 \times 10^6$ N/m. Compute the natural frequency and the transmission ratio for an input vibration frequency of $f = 8$ Hz from a road transport.

### Step 1 — Natural Frequency

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_n &= \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{5 \times 10^6}{25{,}000}} \\
&= \sqrt{200} \\
&\approx 14.14 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_n &= \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} \\
&= \frac{14.14}{6.2832} \\
&\approx 2.25 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** This low natural frequency means the cradle behaves as a low-pass mechanical filter, isolating high-frequency road inputs from the airframe.

### Step 2 — Frequency Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{f}{f_n} \\
&= \frac{8}{2.25} \\
&\approx 3.56
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Since $r > \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$, the system is in the **isolation regime** — vibration will be attenuated.

### Step 3 — Transmission Ratio (Undamped Approximation)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \left\vert \frac{1}{1 - r^2} \right\vert \\
&= \left\vert \frac{1}{1 - 12.65} \right\vert \\
&= \left\vert \frac{1}{-11.65} \right\vert \\
&\approx 0.0859
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Only $\approx 8.6$ % of the input vibration is transmitted — a $91.4$ % reduction, which protects avionics and fatigue-sensitive joints.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Preservation Selection

| Aircraft Component | Duration < 7 d | 7 d – 90 d | > 90 d | Best Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Piston Engine | Engine covers | WPS + cyclic run-up | DPS + fogging + VCI | DPS for long-term |
| Turbofan Engine | Cowl plugs, covers | WPS + 30-day rotation | DPS + VPI in HPC | DPS + VPI |
| Avionics Bay | ESD bag + covers | Sealed + desiccant | Hermetic + dry nitrogen purge | $\text{N}_2$ purge |
| Landing Gear | Chocks, covers | Tire rotation | Jack up + chocks + grease reapplication | Grease renewal |
| Fuel Tanks | Drain water | Fuel with inhibitor | Empty + fog with VPI | Empty + VPI |
| Composite Surfaces | Covers only | UV-blocking covers + shrink wrap | Vacuum-sealed barrier | Vacuum barrier |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Aircraft Packaging Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Aircraft Receives Storage or Transport Order]
    nodeB{Storage Type or Transport Mode?}
    nodeC[Pre-Preservation Inspection and Documentation]
    nodeD[Drain All Fluids Oil Hydraulic Fuel]
    nodeE[Apply Corrosion Preventive Compound CPC to Exposed Surfaces]
    nodeF[Fog Engine Cylinders with MIL-PRF-21260 Oil]
    nodeG[Install Engine and Intake Plugs]
    nodeH[Apply Volatile Corrosion Inhibitor VCI Emitters in Tanks and Cavities]
    nodeI[Mount Aircraft on Transport Cradle and Compute CG]
    nodeJ[Apply Barrier Film 4 mil and Seal Container]
    nodeK[Insert Silica Gel Desiccant and Humidity Indicators]
    nodeL[Install Shock Tilt and Temperature Data Loggers]
    nodeM[Apply External Markings Per ISO 780 and IATA DGR]
    nodeN[Final QA Audit and Release for Shipment]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB -->|Short term less than 7 days| nodeC
    nodeB -->|Intermediate 7 to 90 days| nodeC
    nodeB -->|Long term more than 90 days| nodeC
    nodeB -->|Surface Transport| nodeC
    nodeB -->|Air Transport| nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Containerization Architecture (ATA Category III)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Outer Steel Crate ASTM D6251] --> nodeB[Inner Barrier Film 4 mil Aluminum Foil Laminate]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Cushioning Foam Closed Cell PUR]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Supported Airframe or Component]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Base Cradle Steel Welded Frame]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Shock Mounts Elastomer Pads]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Crate Base Plywood plus Steel]

    nodeH[VCI Emitters] --> nodeD
    nodeI[Silica Gel Bags] --> nodeC
    nodeJ[Humidity Indicator Cards] --> nodeC
    nodeK[Shock Indicator Label] --> nodeA
    nodeL[Temperature Data Logger] --> nodeD
    nodeM[Tilt Indicator 45 Degree] --> nodeA
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Depreservation and Return to Service

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Remove Outer Packaging and Verify Indicators]
    nodeB{Any Shock or Tilt Indicator Activated?}
    nodeC[Conduct Engineering Investigation and Possibly Reject]
    nodeD[Open Container in Controlled Humidity Environment]
    nodeE[Remove Desiccant and VCI Emitters]
    nodeF[Drain Preservation Oil and Flush Lubrication System]
    nodeG[Perform Borescope Inspection of Engine Internals]
    nodeH[Refill with Operational Oil and Hydraulic Fluid]
    nodeI[Ground Run Engine and Check Pressures]
    nodeJ[Avionics Functional Test and EMI Check]
    nodeK[Issue Certificate of Return to Service CRS]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB -->|Yes| nodeC
    nodeB -->|No| nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**

**Distinguish between Dry Preservation System (DPS) and Wet Preservation System (WPS) for aircraft piston engines. State any two situations where DPS is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Dry Preservation System (DPS):** The engine lubricating oil is drained and replaced with **MIL-PRF-21260 Grade 2 preservation oil**. The cylinders are **fogged** through the spark-plug or igniter ports using a pressurized atomizer at 35–70 kPa to deposit a thin protective film on cylinder walls, piston crowns, and valve stems. No oil is left in the sump, and the engine is **not rotated** during storage.

> **Wet Preservation System (WPS):** The engine is filled with preservation oil and **rotated by hand every 30 days** (typically 5–10 revolutions) to redistribute the protective oil film over bearing surfaces and prevent oil migration.

> **DPS is preferred in:** (i) Storage exceeding **90 days**, since WPS requires periodic hand-turning, and (ii) Environments with **high humidity or salt-laden air**, where a dry sump eliminates the risk of oil emulsification and corrosion from water-laden oil. *(1 mark)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**

**What are the functions of Volatile Corrosion Inhibitors (VCI) and Vapor Phase Inhibitors (VPI) in aircraft preservation? Mention any two examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

VCI/VPI are **chemical compounds** that sublime at ambient temperature, releasing protective molecules that **adsorb onto metal surfaces** and form a monomolecular barrier against moisture, oxygen, and chloride ions.

**Functions:**
1. Protect **internal cavities** (fuel tanks, landing-gear wells, control surfaces) where direct application of oil is impractical.
2. Provide **active, self-healing protection** — any breach in the film is replenished by continued sublimation.

**Examples:** **Nitrite-amine based VCI** (e.g., **Daubert Cromwell NOX-RUST**), **Benzotriazole-based VCI for copper alloys**, and **carbonate-based VPI** (e.g., **Shell Vaprotec VCI 629**).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme NEP]**

**(a)** Describe the engineering considerations for **containerization of a turbofan engine** for long-term sea transport. List the relevant **ATA Specification 300 Category III** requirements. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a suitable sketch, explain the **five-layer barrier packaging architecture** used for airworthy components. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution — Question A (a)**

**(i) Pre-Shipping Inspection (1 mark):** Prior to containerization, the engine is **boroscope inspected**, all **fluid systems drained**, and a baseline **preservation status report** is generated with serial numbers, hours, and cycles.

**(ii) Engine Preparation (2 marks):** The engine is mounted on a **welded steel transport cradle** matched to its CG. The HP and LP spools are **locked using shipping locks** to prevent rotation damage. All openings (inlet, exhaust, accessory drives, FADEC connectors) are sealed with **engraved aluminum plugs** to prevent FOD and moisture ingress.

**(iii) Preservation (2 marks):** **DPS method** is applied — lubricating oil drained, replaced with **MIL-PRF-21260 Grade 2** preservation oil, and cylinders fogged. **VPI emitters** are placed in the HPC, LPC, and accessory gearbox cavities. Desiccant bags (Type 4 silica gel, $500$ g minimum) are inserted in each internal cavity.

**(iv) Containerization (1 mark):** The engine is enclosed in a **4-mil aluminum foil barrier laminate**, placed inside a **welded steel crate**, cushioned with **closed-cell polyurethane foam (40 mm)**, and the crate is sealed. **Shock indicators** (4g), **tilt indicators** (45°), and a **temperature/humidity data logger** are attached externally.

**(v) ATA Spec 300 Category III Compliance (1 mark):**
- Hermetically sealed ≥ **1.4 kPa internal pressure** for **24 hours** with ≤ **2 % pressure loss**.
- Drop test: **305 mm height** without damage.
- Vibration test: **4g peak, 1–50 Hz, 30 minutes per axis**.
- Markings per **ISO 780** and **IATA DGR** (if shipped by air).

### **Model Solution — Question A (b)**

The **five-layer barrier packaging architecture** for airworthy components (from inside outward):

**Layer 1 — Direct Contact Wrap:** **Volatile Corrosion Inhibitor (VCI) paper** conforming to **MIL-PRF-22019**, wrapped directly around the cleaned, CPC-coated component surface.

**Layer 2 — Cushioning Layer:** **Closed-cell polyethylene or polyurethane foam** (density 30–50 kg/m³) shaped to the component contour, providing shock absorption.

**Layer 3 — Moisture Barrier:** **4-mil aluminum foil laminate** (typically **PET/Al/PE** — polyester, aluminum, polyethylene) with heat-sealed seams, providing a **water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) < 0.01 g/m²/day**.

**Layer 4 — Desiccant & Indicator Layer:** **Silica gel bags** (calculated per Section 3.1 derivation) plus **humidity indicator cards** (per **MIL-I-8835**) changing color from blue (dry) to pink (wet) at **30 % and 40 % RH** thresholds.

**Layer 5 — Rigid Outer Crate:** **Steel-framed plywood crate** per **ASTM D6251** with bolted access panels, external markings, and weather-resistant paint. Shock and tilt indicators are mounted externally.

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the **environmental control requirements** for long-term aircraft storage. Explain how humidity and temperature are monitored and controlled. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A transport container of internal volume **10 m³** is sealed at **30 °C and 80 % RH**. The desired final condition is **25 °C and 30 % RH**. Using the **Antoine equation**, calculate the **minimum mass of silica gel required**, assuming desiccant efficiency $\eta_{des} = 0.30$. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution — Question B (a)**

**(i) Need for Environmental Control (2 marks):** Long-term storage (Type II preservation, > 90 days) without environmental control leads to **filiform corrosion** on aluminum alloys, **hydrogen embrittlement** of high-strength steel (e.g., landing-gear pins), **electrochemical migration** in avionics PCBs, and **fungal growth** in cabin insulation. The **MIL-STD-2073-1D** standard mandates **< 35 % RH** and **-40 °C to +50 °C** envelope.

**(ii) Humidity Control — Active Methods (2 marks):** **Desiccant dehumidifiers** (silica gel or molecular sieve) inside sealed containers; **dry nitrogen purge** for avionics bays (oxygen content < 4 %, dew point < -40 °C); **barrier film heat-sealed enclosures**; and **VCI/VPI emitters** as supplementary chemical control.

**(iii) Temperature Control (2 marks):** **Insulated container walls** (PUR foam, $k = 0.024$ W/m·K); **passive thermal mass** inside the container to dampen diurnal swings; **active HVAC** for indoor storage hangars; **thermal blankets** for outdoor storage; and **phase-change material (PCM)** packs for sensitive avionics to maintain **20 ± 5 °C**.

**(iv) Monitoring (1 mark):** **Electronic data loggers** (e.g., HOBO U23-002) recording RH and temperature at **15-minute intervals** for the entire storage period; **humidity indicator cards** providing visual, low-cost verification; and **remote telemetry** (cellular/satellite link) for high-value assets.

### **Model Solution — Question B (b) — Full Numerical Walkthrough**

**Given:** $V = 10 \text{ m}^3$, $T_{seal} = 30$ °C, $RH_i = 80$ %, $T_{final} = 25$ °C, $RH_f = 30$ %, $\eta_{des} = 0.30$.

**Step 1 — $P_{sat}$ at 30 °C (Antoine equation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(P_{sat,\,30}) &= 8.07131 - \frac{1730.63}{233.426 + 30} \\
&= 8.07131 - \frac{1730.63}{263.426} \\
&= 8.07131 - 6.57014 \\
&= 1.50117
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{sat,\,30} &= 10^{1.50117} \\
&\approx 31.74 \text{ mmHg} \\
&= 31.74 \times 133.322 \\
&\approx 4232 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Partial pressure at sealing:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{v,i} &= 0.80 \times 4232 \\
&= 3386 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Partial pressure at final condition (25 °C, 30 %):** From derivation 1 we know $P_{sat,\,25} = 3167$ Pa, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{v,f} &= 0.30 \times 3167 \\
&= 950 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of dry air (using $T = 303.15$ K, sealing temperature):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{air} &= \frac{101325 \times 10}{287.05 \times 303.15} \\
&= \frac{1{,}013{,}250}{87{,}016} \\
&\approx 11.64 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Humidity ratios:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_i &= 0.622 \times \frac{3386}{101325 - 3386} \\
&= 0.622 \times \frac{3386}{97939} \\
&= 0.622 \times 0.03457 \\
&= 0.02151 \text{ kg/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_f &= 0.622 \times \frac{950}{101325 - 950} \\
&= 0.622 \times \frac{950}{100375} \\
&= 0.622 \times 0.009464 \\
&= 0.005887 \text{ kg/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Water to be removed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta m_{water} &= 11.64 \times (0.02151 - 0.005887) \\
&= 11.64 \times 0.015623 \\
&= 0.1819 \text{ kg} \\
&\approx 182 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Required desiccant mass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{des} &= \frac{0.1819}{0.30} \\
&= 0.606 \text{ kg} \\
&\approx 606 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

With a **1.5× safety factor** for transit duration, **recommend 1 kg of Type 4 silica gel**. *(Full marks: 7)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** use the sealing temperature for $P_{v,f}$ — always use the **final equilibrium temperature** for the final partial pressure.
> 2. **Do not** forget to convert mmHg → Pa using the factor **133.322**.
> 3. **Do not** omit the safety factor on desiccant — it costs marks.
> 4. **Do not** confuse $R_{air} = 287.05$ J/(kg·K) with $R_{water} = 461.5$ J/(kg·K).
> 5. **Always** state Antoine constants explicitly — examiners award a partial mark for the constants alone.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Packaging for Storage/Transport**

- [x] **Packaging** = Preservation + Protection + Containerization + Documentation.
- [x] **Three storage tiers:** Short (< 7 d), Intermediate (7–90 d), Long-term (> 90 d).
- [x] **DPS vs WPS:** DPS = drained + fogged + no rotation; WPS = oil-filled + 30-day rotation cycle.
- [x] **MIL-PRF-21260 Grade 2/3** is the standard preservation oil.
- [x] **Target humidity inside long-term storage: < 35 % RH.**
- [x] **MIL-STD-2073-1D** governs long-term storage environment.
- [x] **ATA Spec 300 Category III** = hermetically sealed, 4g shock rating, 305 mm drop test.
- [x] **Antoine equation constants for water (1–100 °C):** $A = 8.07131$, $B = 1730.63$, $C = 233.426$.
- [x] **Humidity ratio formula:** $\omega = 0.622 \cdot P_v / (P_{atm} - P_v)$.
- [x] **VCI/VPI** = Volatile/Vapor-phase Corrosion Inhibitors for internal cavities.
- [x] **Five-layer packaging:** VCI wrap → foam cushion → foil barrier → desiccant/indicator → rigid crate.
- [x] **Cradle natural frequency** must be **< input frequency / $\sqrt{2}$** for vibration isolation.
- [x] **Transmission ratio** $T = \vert 1/(1-r^2) \vert$ — aim for $T < 0.10$.
- [x] **Engine fogging pressure: 35–70 kPa** through spark-plug/igniter ports.
- [x] **Engine shipping locks** mandatory for turbofans to lock HP/LP spools.
- [x] **Depreservation** = reverse of preservation: flush, refill, borescope, ground run, certify.
- [x] **Markings per ISO 780** (arrows) + **IATA DGR** (hazardous) + **ASTM D5445** (Keep Dry).
- [x] **Type 4 silica gel** = standard desiccant; efficiency **$\eta_{des} \approx 0.30$** at 35 % RH, 25 °C.
- [x] **Desiccant safety factor: 1.5×** minimum for transit operations.
- [x] **Closed-cell PUR foam** = $k = 0.024$ W/(m·K) is preferred insulation in shipping containers.
- [x] **Drop test 305 mm** for ATA Cat III; **vibration 4g peak, 1–50 Hz, 30 min/axis.**
- [x] **Always** convert mmHg → Pa (× 133.322) and °C → K (+ 273.15) in numerical problems.
